hosting_target.rationale: Container orchestration for independent scaling of API, worker, and WebSocket pods; S3 for durable file storage

privacy_retention_policy.value: Project files retained until project owner deletes them; audit_events retained for 3 years; GDPR right-to-erasure for team member personal data within 30 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR for EU-based musicians; 3-year audit retention for SOC 2 evidence

future_scope.value: DAW plugin for in-app stem upload, mobile companion app, AI-powered mix note generation
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap; plugin SDK requires stable API contract first

constraints.value: All data must remain in us-east-1; file upload limit 2GB per stem; budget under $4000/month for initial 50 active projects
constraints.rationale: Label data residency requirements; practical upload size limits for uncompressed audio

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with distributed tracing, structured JSON logs, PagerDuty for P1 alerts, Datadog audit log dashboard for compliance team
observability_baseline.rationale: Enterprise label clients require SLA reporting and compliance dashboards

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints with feature flags; trunk-based development; staged rollout to label clients
execution_preference.rationale: Risk mitigation for security-sensitive features via feature flags before full rollout

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II controls required for label enterprise accounts; GDPR for EU team member data; NDA-backed confidentiality obligations for all project content
compliance_context.rationale: Risk level high and data sensitivity confidential triggers compliance context requirement

project.goal: Secure async collaboration for music production teams on unreleased projects
project.target_users: Producers, session musicians, mix engineers, label A&R

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for UI state

backend.jobs: Celery + Redis for waveform generation, virus scan, thumbnail jobs
backend.storage: Boto3 for S3 presigned URL generation

security.auth: Auth0 with MFA enforcement
security.encryption: SSE-KMS for S3, AES-256 for DB fields, TLS 1.3 in transit
security.ip_control: Per-project IP allowlist stored in access_policies table

data.cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 for sessions and Celery broker

reasoner_reviews.product: The project-scoped RBAC model with listener/reviewer/editor/owner roles is the right foundation for music collaboration. The distinction between reviewer (comment only) and editor (upload stems) roles is critical for session musician workflows where creative control must stay with the producer.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI with Celery is well-suited: async waveform generation and virus scanning should not block the upload acknowledgment. The version tree for stems is the most complex data modeling challenge - a closure table or adjacency list for stem_versions will need careful design to support branch and merge scenarios.
reasoner_reviews.security: Confidential unreleased music under NDA requires encryption at the KMS level with customer-managed keys so label clients can rotate keys on personnel changes. The immutable audit trail must use an append-only PostgreSQL table with RLS preventing UPDATE and DELETE, complemented by Datadog log forwarding for off-system audit evidence.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 2GB upload limit is workable; presigned S3 URLs offload transfer from the API. Budget of $4000/month supports EKS with 3 worker nodes at moderate load. IP allowlisting per project is operationally critical for enterprise label clients with fixed office egress IPs.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The admin panel scope for label account management needs explicit data isolation guarantees - a label admin must never be able to query stems from another label's projects even via the admin API. Row-level security on projects and PostgreSQL schemas per label would address this.

backend.service_design: FastAPI app with domain modules: projects, stems, versions, access, audit, admin; Celery workers for waveform_gen, virus_scan, thumbnail_gen jobs; WebSocket manager for real-time notification broadcast per project channel
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with versioned /api/v1/ prefix; presigned S3 URLs for upload (PUT) and download (GET) to offload binary traffic from API pods; SSE or WebSocket for notification feed; OpenAPI 3.1 spec auto-generated
backend.failure_handling: S3 presigned URL expiry (15min upload, 1hr download); Celery dead-letter queue for failed waveform jobs with Sentry alert; idempotency keys on stem upload to prevent duplicate version creation on retry

security.auth_design: Auth0 tenant with MFA enforcement policy; organization feature for label-level user grouping; JWT validated by FastAPI dependency via JWKS endpoint
security.authorization_model: PostgreSQL RLS policies on projects and stems tables keyed by project_id and user_id in JWT claims; project-scoped roles stored in project_members table; label admin role gated by Auth0 organization membership
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_events table: append-only with RLS USING (false) for UPDATE/DELETE; records actor_id, action ENUM, resource_type, resource_id, ip_address, user_agent, metadata_json, created_at; forwarded to Datadog via log drain for SOC 2 evidence
security.privacy_controls: GDPR erasure: celery task soft-deletes team member personal data and anonymizes audit_events actor references within 30 days of request
security.incident_response_notes: KMS key rotation procedure documented: label admin triggers key rotation in AWS console; Celery re-encryption job re-wraps all project S3 objects under new key version within 72 hours

data.entities: projects (id UUID PK, label_id FK, name, status ENUM active/archived/deleted, owner_id, ip_allowlist_cidrs TEXT[], created_at), stems (id UUID PK, project_id FK, name, audio_format ENUM wav/aiff/mp3/flac, duration_seconds REAL, sample_rate INT, created_by FK, current_version_id FK, created_at), stem_versions (id UUID PK, stem_id FK, version_number INT, s3_key, file_size_bytes BIGINT, checksum_sha256 VARCHAR, waveform_json TEXT, virus_scan_status ENUM pending/clean/quarantined, uploaded_by FK, created_at), project_members (id UUID PK, project_id FK, user_id FK, role ENUM owner/editor/reviewer/listener, invited_by FK, joined_at), access_policies (id UUID PK, project_id FK, ip_allowlist_cidrs INET[], session_timeout_minutes INT, require_mfa BOOL, updated_at), audit_events (id UUID PK, actor_id FK, action ENUM stem_upload/stem_download/version_restore/member_invite/policy_change, resource_type, resource_id, ip_address INET, user_agent TEXT, metadata_json JSONB, created_at), comments (id UUID PK, stem_id FK, author_id FK, timestamp_seconds REAL, body TEXT, resolved BOOL, created_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for relational data; S3 with SSE-KMS per-project KMS key prefix for stem file objects; ElastiCache Redis for JWT session cache and Celery task broker; waveform_json stored in stem_versions to avoid separate object reads

devops.deployment_topology: EKS cluster: 3 node groups (api-pods m6i.large x3, worker-pods m6i.xlarge x2 for Celery, ws-pods t3.medium x2 for WebSocket manager); ALB Ingress Controller; RDS Multi-AZ in same VPC private subnet; ElastiCache in private subnet
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest with 85% coverage gate, mypy type check, Bandit security scan, Docker build and push to ECR, Helm chart deploy to EKS via ArgoCD; migrations run as Kubernetes Job pre-deploy
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with FastAPI middleware injecting trace IDs; structured JSON logs via python-json-logger; custom metrics: stem_upload_duration_seconds histogram, virus_scan_queue_depth gauge, audit_event_rate counter; PagerDuty for error rate >0.5% or p99 latency >3s

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 15 App Router with TypeScript; server components for project/stem listings; client components for real-time comment threads and RBAC invite management
frontend.pages_and_flows: Dashboard, Project Detail (stem list + version tree), Stem Upload, Version History Browser, Real-Time Comment Thread, RBAC Invite Management, Admin Panel (label accounts)
frontend.state_management: React Query for server state (stems, versions, comments); WebSocket client for real-time comment events; Zustand for optimistic UI state
frontend.frontend_security_notes: End-to-end encrypted file URLs must be short-lived presigned URLs; stem previews must never expose raw storage bucket URLs to the client
frontend.frontend_risks: Version tree browser with large projects (hundreds of stems) must virtualize the list to prevent DOM bloat; test with 500+ stems

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a high-risk confidential platform where the architecture must be security-first without sacrificing collaboration UX. Key decisions: customer-managed KMS keys per label for key rotation on personnel changes; PostgreSQL RLS as the enforcement layer for project scoping rather than application-layer checks alone; append-only audit_events with RLS preventing DELETE/UPDATE for SOC 2 evidence; Celery for async file processing to avoid blocking upload acknowledgments; Auth0 organizations for label-level user grouping.
target_output.title: Remote Music Production Collaboration Platform Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A security-hardened fullstack collaboration platform for remote music production teams, built on Next.js 15 and FastAPI deployed on AWS EKS. Supports project-scoped RBAC for producers, session musicians, mix engineers, and label A&R with per-project customer-managed KMS encryption, immutable audit trails, and real-time stem comment threads. Designed to meet SOC 2 Type II controls and GDPR obligations for enterprise label clients handling unreleased masters.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture: Next.js 15 App Router frontend on CloudFront, FastAPI REST and WebSocket API on EKS behind ALB with WAF, PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with row-level security for project isolation. File uploads bypass the API via presigned S3 PUT URLs; downloads via presigned GET URLs with 1-hour expiry. Each label's S3 objects use a dedicated KMS key prefix, enabling key rotation on personnel change events. Celery workers on separate EKS node group handle waveform generation, virus scanning, and thumbnail creation asynchronously. Auth0 organizations map labels to user groups; JWT claims carry project_id and role for RLS enforcement in PostgreSQL.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 15, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, WebSocket client. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.x async, Celery 5, Boto3, python-jose. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ with RLS. Cache/Queue: ElastiCache Redis 7. File Storage: S3 with SSE-KMS per-label key prefixes. Auth: Auth0 with MFA enforcement and organizations. Infrastructure: EKS, ALB + WAF, CloudFront, ACM. Observability: Datadog APM, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Project Workspace: create project with label assignment, RBAC invite flow (owner/editor/reviewer/listener), per-project IP allowlist config. Stem Management: upload WAV/AIFF/MP3/FLAC up to 2GB via presigned S3 URL; version tree browser showing stem_versions with waveform visualization; restore to prior version. Comment Threads: timestamped comments on stem waveform positions, resolved/unresolved status, real-time delivery via WebSocket project channel. Audit Trail: filterable audit_events log visible to owner and label admin showing all stem access, version changes, and policy modifications. Admin Panel: label admin manages team members across projects, views storage usage per project, triggers KMS key rotation.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront distribution for Next.js static assets and API cache headers. ALB with WAF rules (OWASP core rule set + rate limiting) routing to EKS ingress. EKS API pods (FastAPI on uvicorn), Worker pods (Celery: waveform_gen, virus_scan, thumbnail_gen), WebSocket pods (FastAPI WebSocket manager with Redis pub/sub for cross-pod broadcast). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with read replica for audit report queries. ElastiCache Redis 7 for Celery broker and JWT session revocation list. S3 with SSE-KMS, per-label KMS key aliases, lifecycle rule archiving stem versions older than 2 years to S3 Glacier. AWS KMS for customer-managed keys with CloudTrail key usage logging.
target_output.workflows: Stem Upload: editor opens project, selects stem, requests presigned PUT URL from POST /stems/{id}/upload-url (FastAPI validates project membership and IP allowlist, returns S3 presigned URL with 15-min expiry and upload_token); client uploads directly to S3; S3 event notification triggers Celery chain: virus_scan -> on_clean -> waveform_gen -> thumbnail_gen -> mark version clean and notify project channel via WebSocket. Reviewer Access and Download: reviewer authenticates via Auth0 MFA, JWT includes project_id+role claims, RLS allows SELECT on stems where membership exists; reviewer requests presigned GET URL from GET /stems/{id}/download; FastAPI logs audit_event STEM_DOWNLOAD including ip_address and user_agent; presigned URL expires after 1 hour preventing long-lived link sharing. Key Rotation: label admin triggers rotation via admin panel; FastAPI enqueues Celery re-encryption job per S3 prefix; each object re-wrapped under new KMS key version; audit_event KEY_ROTATION recorded; completion notification sent to label admin email via SES.
target_output.data_model: projects (id UUID PK, label_id FK, name TEXT, status ENUM active/archived/deleted, owner_id FK, ip_allowlist_cidrs INET[], created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). stems (id UUID PK, project_id FK, name TEXT, audio_format ENUM wav/aiff/mp3/flac, duration_seconds REAL, sample_rate INT, created_by FK, current_version_id FK, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). stem_versions (id UUID PK, stem_id FK, version_number INT, s3_key TEXT, file_size_bytes BIGINT, checksum_sha256 CHAR(64), waveform_json JSONB, virus_scan_status ENUM pending/clean/quarantined, uploaded_by FK, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). project_members (id UUID PK, project_id FK, user_id FK, role ENUM owner/editor/reviewer/listener, invited_by FK, joined_at TIMESTAMPTZ). access_policies (id UUID PK, project_id FK, ip_allowlist_cidrs INET[], session_timeout_minutes INT, require_mfa BOOL, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_events (id UUID PK, actor_id FK, action ENUM stem_upload/stem_download/version_restore/member_invite/policy_change/key_rotation, resource_type TEXT, resource_id UUID, ip_address INET, user_agent TEXT, metadata_json JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). comments (id UUID PK, stem_id FK, author_id FK, timestamp_seconds REAL, body TEXT, resolved BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: REST /api/v1: POST /projects | GET /projects/{id} | POST /projects/{id}/members | DELETE /projects/{id}/members/{user_id} | GET /stems?project_id={id} | POST /stems | GET /stems/{id}/versions | POST /stems/{id}/upload-url -> {presigned_put_url, upload_token, expires_in:900} | GET /stems/{id}/download -> {presigned_get_url, expires_in:3600} | GET /audit-events?project_id={id}&actor_id={id}&action={enum} | GET /admin/labels/{id}/projects | POST /admin/labels/{id}/rotate-key. WebSocket ws/projects/{id}/feed: server pushes {type:stem_version_ready, stem_id, version_number} | {type:comment_added, comment_id} | {type:member_joined, user_id}. All endpoints require Auth0 JWT; project-scoped endpoints enforce RLS via db session.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 MFA enforcement policy for all users; organization feature maps labels to user groups for cross-project admin isolation. PostgreSQL RLS on projects and stems: policies use JWT sub claim extracted via current_setting('app.current_user_id') to restrict SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE to project members. S3 SSE-KMS with per-label KMS key alias; CloudTrail logs all key usage. Append-only audit_events table: RLS USING (false) for UPDATE and DELETE, preventing tampering even by DB admins. WAF OWASP core rule set + rate limit 200 req/min per IP. IP allowlist enforcement in FastAPI middleware: request IP checked against project access_policies.ip_allowlist_cidrs before any data operation. Session expiry: Auth0 absolute session 8hrs, idle timeout 30min configurable per project. SOC 2 evidence: CloudTrail, Datadog logs, and audit_events table provide access control, availability, and confidentiality evidence streams.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EKS cluster with 3 node groups in us-east-1a/1b/1c: api-pods (m6i.large, min 3 max 9, HPA on CPU 60%), worker-pods (m6i.xlarge, min 2 max 6, HPA on Celery queue depth), ws-pods (t3.medium, min 2 max 4). ALB Ingress with SSL termination and WAF. ArgoCD for GitOps deploy from Helm chart in git repo; pre-deploy Kubernetes Job runs Alembic migrations. GitHub Actions CI: pytest (85% coverage gate), mypy, Bandit security scan, Docker build + ECR push, Helm lint, ArgoCD sync. Rollback: ArgoCD previous revision in under 2 minutes. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager with 60-day rotation for DB passwords and Auth0 client secret. RDS automated daily snapshots retained 14 days.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM: FastAPI middleware injects X-Trace-Id; traces across API -> Celery worker -> S3 presign flow. Custom metrics: stem_upload_duration_seconds histogram by audio_format, virus_scan_queue_depth gauge, audit_event_write_rate counter, presigned_url_generation_latency_ms histogram. Structured JSON logs via python-json-logger with project_id, user_id, and trace_id fields. Datadog compliance dashboard: audit_event rate by action type for SOC 2 evidence reporting. PagerDuty escalation: API error rate >0.5%, p99 latency >3s, virus_scan_queue_depth >20, RDS connections >80%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ~$3200/month at 50 active projects with 500GB total stem storage. EKS API nodes auto-scale on CPU. S3 Intelligent-Tiering moves unaccessed stem files after 30 days. Glacier archive for versions older than 2 years reduces storage cost by ~40%. Worker nodes scale on Celery queue depth metric exported to Kubernetes HPA via KEDA. Reserved instances for baseline EKS nodes save ~30% over on-demand.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): project and stem management with RBAC, S3 presigned upload/download, stem version history, append-only audit trail, Auth0 MFA, PostgreSQL RLS, CI/CD pipeline, Datadog APM. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Celery waveform generation and waveform visualizer in UI, virus scanning with quarantine workflow, real-time WebSocket notification feed, comment threads on stem timeline. Phase 3 (5 weeks): admin panel for label account management, KMS key rotation workflow, IP allowlisting per project, GDPR erasure task, SOC 2 compliance dashboard in Datadog.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: ruff and mypy strict in CI; pytest with 85% coverage including RLS policy tests using separate DB role without bypass. Next.js: ESLint + Prettier; Playwright E2E for stem upload, download, and invite flows. Feature flags via LaunchDarkly for phased rollout of admin panel to label clients. PR requires 2 reviews for security-related modules (auth, audit, encryption). Trunk-based development with short-lived branches. All DB schema changes via Alembic versioned migrations. No direct S3 URL construction in application code; always use presigned URL generator service.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: presigned S3 URL forwarding by reviewers to unauthorized parties. Mitigation: short expiry (1hr download), audit_event logging of every download request, IP allowlist enforcement before URL generation. Risk: Celery worker backlog during large batch uploads from label with hundreds of stems. Mitigation: separate worker node group with KEDA HPA on queue depth; priority queue for virus_scan over waveform_gen. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL RLS enforcement over application-layer checks - RLS is non-bypassable even by API bugs but adds query plan complexity; mitigated by RLS-aware connection pooling in SQLAlchemy. Tradeoff: per-label KMS key over shared key - higher KMS API cost but enables individual label key rotation without affecting other clients.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected PostgreSQL RLS over application-layer RBAC as primary enforcement for defense-in-depth. Confirmed S3 presigned URLs over API-proxied streaming to stay within budget on large file transfers. Chose Auth0 organizations over custom multi-tenancy for faster time-to-SOC-2. Celery with Redis broker chosen over AWS SQS to keep infrastructure in EKS without additional AWS service dependencies for job queue.

metadata.notes: Security-hardened music collaboration platform with per-label KMS, PostgreSQL RLS, immutable audit trail, SOC 2 controls.

project_goal.value: Automate music supervision for film and TV by matching scene mood, tempo, and emotional arc against a licensed catalog and generating cue recommendations with rights clearance metadata
project_goal.rationale: Core business problem: supervisors spend hours manually searching catalogs; ML matching cuts search time by 80%

target_users.value: Film and TV music supervisors, licensing coordinators, and post-production editors
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in discovery sessions with three studio partners

project_class.rationale: ML embedding pipeline and LLM-generated rationales define the system core value proposition

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, analytics, batch_jobs, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack AI system with batch catalog ingestion, real-time recommendation retrieval, and rights clearance integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Millions of catalog tracks, high-dimensional embeddings, concurrent supervisor sessions, and LLM call orchestration

risk_level.rationale: Handles confidential unreleased film content and proprietary sync licensing terms under studio NDAs

data_sensitivity.rationale: Unreleased scene briefs, studio project names, and rights clearance negotiation data are commercially sensitive

external_exposure.rationale: Accessible only to credentialed supervisors and studio partners; no public endpoints

access_model.value: RBAC with supervisor, coordinator, admin, and read-only preview roles; MFA required for all
access_model.rationale: Different roles need different catalog write and rights clearance initiation permissions

feature_scope.value: Scene brief ingestion, ML mood/tempo embedding search, ranked cue recommendations, LLM narrative justification, rights clearance feasibility scoring, sync license management, supervisor dashboard, batch catalog indexing
feature_scope.rationale: End-to-end supervision workflow from scene brief to cleared cue

mvp_scope.value: Scene brief upload, embedding-based catalog search, top-10 cue recommendations with clearance flags, LLM justification for top 3 cues, supervisor review UI
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for studio pilot with two production companies

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest for scene briefs and catalog data, MFA enforced, Auth0 RBAC, WAF on ALB, per-project NDA access scoping
security_baseline.rationale: Studio NDA obligations and confidential content require strong access controls

frontend_stack.rationale: SSR for fast initial load of scene brief forms; team expertise in React ecosystem

backend_stack.rationale: Python ecosystem dominates ML tooling; FastAPI for async performance and OpenAPI generation

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension for audio mood embeddings; Redis 7 for recommendation caching
data_platform.rationale: pgvector avoids a separate vector DB for this catalog size; Redis caches costly embedding lookups

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate for API and batch workers; S3 for audio assets and scene briefs; CloudFront for supervisor UI
hosting_target.rationale: Fargate simplifies GPU-less batch scaling; S3 and CloudFront for secure asset delivery

privacy_retention_policy.value: Scene briefs deleted 90 days after project close; catalog embeddings retained indefinitely; supervisor activity logs retained 2 years; GDPR right-to-erasure for supervisor accounts
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Studio data minimization obligations and GDPR compliance for EU-based supervisors

future_scope.value: Real-time scene video analysis for automated mood extraction, direct clearance negotiation workflow with rights societies, mobile supervisor app
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap pending studio feedback from MVP pilot

constraints.value: Embedding generation must complete within 30 seconds per scene brief; catalog index must support 5M+ tracks; LLM calls budgeted at under $0.05 per recommendation set
constraints.rationale: Latency SLA for supervisor UX and cost ceiling for LLM usage at scale

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with custom traces for embedding pipeline and LLM call latency; structured JSON logs; PagerDuty alerts for embedding p95 > 25s or LLM error rate > 2%
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate observability standard plus ML-specific latency tracking

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints with monthly studio stakeholder demos
execution_preference.rationale: Studio partners require regular milestone visibility

llm_integration.value: OpenAI GPT-4o for cue narrative justification; prompts include mood descriptor, track metadata, and scene context; output structured as JSON with rationale, mood_alignment_score, and suggested_use_notes
llm_integration.rationale: GPT-4o chosen for nuanced emotional language quality; structured output prevents parsing failures

compliance_context.value: Studio NDAs for confidential film content; GDPR for EU supervisor personal data; sync licensing agreements with PROs and rights holders; SOC 2 controls for client data handling
compliance_context.rationale: High risk_level and confidential data sensitivity require explicit compliance scoping

project.goal: ML-powered cue recommendation engine with LLM justifications for film and TV music supervisors
project.target_users: Music supervisors, licensing coordinators, studio post-production teams
project.timeline: MVP pilot in 12 weeks, full launch in 6 months

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for session UI state

backend.api: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.1, polling for recommendation status

security.auth: Auth0 with MFA, RBAC roles
security.encryption: AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit

data.cache: Redis 7 for embedding and recommendation caching
data.object_store: S3 for scene briefs and audio assets

open_questions.embedding_model: Internal Essentia embeddings vs third-party audio embedding API to be decided in week 1

reasoner_reviews.product: The scene brief to cue recommendation workflow directly replaces manual catalog browsing that supervisors describe as their biggest time sink. LLM narrative justifications help supervisors pitch selections to directors without writing custom notes. MVP scope is well-bounded and achievable for a two-company studio pilot with a 100K-track catalog seed.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: pgvector on PostgreSQL 16 is the right call for 5M tracks at this stage, avoiding vector DB operational overhead while keeping relational rights data and embeddings in the same transactional boundary. The batch catalog ingestion worker must be SQS-decoupled from the API service to prevent catalog reindexing from impacting supervisor session latency. Celery with SQS broker fits the Python stack and allows per-job task routing.
reasoner_reviews.security: Confidential film content demands per-project NDA scoping at the data layer, not just RBAC. A project_access_policies table that gates supervisor access to film_projects is required. Scene brief files on S3 must use pre-signed URLs with 15-minute expiry and CloudFront signed cookies for UI access. Auth0 RBAC with MFA is sufficient for authentication; authorization must be application-enforced at the FastAPI dependency layer.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 30-second embedding latency is achievable with Essentia feature extraction on a 3-minute audio file on Fargate compute. Catalog ingestion of 5M tracks requires parallelized SQS-backed batch workers. LLM cost of $0.05 per set is achievable with GPT-4o given 3 justifications at roughly 200 tokens each.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The recommendation ranking logic needs explicit design. Pure cosine similarity on mood embeddings may surface tracks with correct vibe but unavailable clearances. The clearance feasibility score must be a first-class signal in the ranking function, not a post-filter applied after returning results to the supervisor.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application split into four modules: ingestion (catalog embedding batch jobs via SQS), recommendation (pgvector ANN similarity search and composite ranking), llm_justification (GPT-4o prompt orchestration with structured output), and clearance (rights availability lookups and sync license management).
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.1. POST /scene-briefs returns brief_id and status indexing. GET /recommendations/{brief_id} returns ranked top-10 with composite scores. GET /recommendations/{brief_id}/justifications polls LLM job status. POST /sync-licenses initiates clearance request. POST /catalog/ingest-batch triggers S3 batch ingestion job.
backend.llm_integration_design: GPT-4o called via OpenAI SDK with structured output mode enforcing JSON schema: rationale string, mood_alignment_score float, suggested_use_notes string. Prompt assembles mood_descriptors, track metadata including title, genre, BPM, key, mood_tags, and scene context. Retry with exponential backoff on rate limit; fallback to cached prior justification if LLM is unavailable.
backend.background_jobs: CatalogIngestionWorker: downloads audio from S3, runs Essentia BPM/key/mood extraction, generates 512-dim embedding, upserts catalog_entry and catalog_embedding rows. EmbeddingRefreshJob: nightly reindex of newly added tracks. ClearanceFeasibilityUpdater: daily refresh of rights availability flags from rights holder API calls.
backend.backend_risks: pgvector ANN search degrades past 10M embeddings without HNSW index re-tuning. Mitigation: build HNSW with ef_construction=200 and m=16, monitor recall monthly. OpenAI API latency spikes during peak load are mitigated by the async polling pattern that decouples supervisor UX from LLM response time.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 15 App Router with route groups: (supervisor) for scene brief submission and recommendation flows, (admin) for catalog management and user administration. Server components for recommendation result pages to minimize client JS payload.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Scene Brief Submission: upload brief text, mood descriptors, tempo_bpm, and select film project. Recommendation Results: ranked cue list with clearance flag badges and LLM justification panel for top 3 cues. Sync License Detail: rights holder info, clearance history, and license request initiation. Admin: catalog ingestion status, embedding job monitor, user management.
frontend.state_management: React Query for recommendation polling with refetchInterval until job status is complete. Zustand for supervisor session state tracking active film_project and selected cue shortlist. No global Redux; each page fetches its own data slice.
frontend.frontend_security_notes: CloudFront signed cookies restrict scene brief asset access to authenticated sessions. Auth0 access tokens stored in httpOnly cookies only. CSP headers block inline scripts. File uploads go directly to pre-signed S3 URLs; Next.js server never proxies audio data.

security.auth_design: Auth0 universal login with PKCE flow and mandatory MFA via TOTP or WebAuthn. Auth0 organizations map to studio clients, scoping visible film_projects per JWT audience claim.
security.authorization_model: Application-layer RBAC: project_access_policies table links supervisor_id to film_project_id and role. FastAPI dependency injects project access check on every recommendation and clearance endpoint. JWT claims provide identity; database enforces authorization.
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager for OpenAI API key, Auth0 client secret, and database credentials. Secrets rotated every 90 days. ECS task role grants Secrets Manager read-only access; no secrets in environment variables or image layers.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Immutable audit_events table logs every scene brief access, recommendation retrieval, cue shortlist action, and sync license request with supervisor_id, film_project_id, IP, and timestamp. CloudTrail captures S3 object access for scene brief files. Logs shipped to Datadog with 2-year retention.
security.privacy_controls: Scene briefs purged from S3 and database 90 days after project_closed_at. GDPR erasure API hard-deletes supervisor account and anonymizes audit_events by nulling user_id while retaining action type for compliance audit continuity.

data.entities: film_projects (id UUID PK, studio_id FK, title_encrypted TEXT, status ENUM active/closed, nda_scoped BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), scenes (id UUID PK, project_id FK, brief_text_encrypted TEXT, reference_audio_s3_key TEXT, mood_descriptors_json JSONB, tempo_bpm FLOAT, emotional_arc_tags TEXT[], created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), cue_recommendations (id UUID PK, scene_id FK, track_id FK, composite_rank_score FLOAT, clearance_feasibility_score FLOAT, mood_similarity_score FLOAT, llm_justification_json JSONB, shortlisted_by UUID, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), catalog_entries (id UUID PK, isrc CHAR(12) UNIQUE, title TEXT, artist TEXT, genre TEXT, bpm FLOAT, musical_key TEXT, mood_tags_json JSONB, rights_holder_id FK, clearance_status ENUM available/restricted/pending, indexed_at TIMESTAMPTZ), catalog_embeddings (id UUID PK, catalog_entry_id FK UNIQUE, embedding vector(512), model_version TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ) with HNSW index, sync_licenses (id UUID PK, track_id FK, project_id FK, license_type ENUM blanket/per_use/festival, territory TEXT, term_years INT, fee_usd NUMERIC(12,2), status ENUM negotiating/cleared/declined, cleared_at TIMESTAMPTZ)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector for all relational and embedding data. S3 with KMS encryption for scene brief uploads and catalog audio assets. Redis 7 for recommendation result caching TTL 1 hour and embedding ANN search result caching TTL 24 hours.
data.analytics_events: cue_viewed, cue_shortlisted, cue_rejected, llm_justification_read, sync_license_requested, scene_brief_submitted emitted to Datadog custom metrics for funnel analysis and recommendation quality monitoring.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster with two services: api-service scaling 2 to 8 tasks on p95 latency target 500ms, and batch-worker scaling 1 to 20 tasks on SQS queue depth with scale-in after 5-minute idle. CloudFront serves Next.js assets from S3 with OAC; API routed through ALB.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest with 85% coverage gate, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy minimum healthy 50%. Terraform Cloud manages VPC, ECS, RDS, SQS, S3, CloudFront, and WAF. Environment promotion: dev to staging to production with manual gate at staging.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM custom spans: embedding_search p95 target under 5s, llm_justification_call p95 target under 8s, clearance_lookup p95 target under 500ms. PagerDuty alerts: embedding p95 over 25s, LLM error rate over 2%, SQS ingest queue depth over 1000, RDS connections over 80%.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing an AI music supervision system where the core architectural challenge is combining pgvector ANN search with clearance feasibility scoring into a meaningful composite rank — pure mood similarity without clearance status produces unusable recommendations. The LLM justification layer must be async via SQS-backed Celery workers to avoid blocking supervisor UX, using a React Query polling pattern. Scene brief data requires per-project NDA access scoping enforced at the FastAPI dependency layer via project_access_policies, not just JWT claims. GPT-4o structured output mode eliminates JSON parsing fragility in the justification pipeline.
target_output.title: AI Music Supervision Cue Recommendation Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale AI system enabling film and TV music supervisors to submit scene briefs and receive ranked cue recommendations from a 5M-track licensed catalog, scored by a composite of mood embedding similarity and rights clearance feasibility. Built on Python FastAPI with PostgreSQL pgvector for HNSW-indexed embedding search and GPT-4o for narrative cue justifications, delivered through a Next.js 15 supervisor UI on AWS ECS Fargate with Auth0 RBAC, per-project NDA access scoping at the database layer, and immutable audit logging for SOC 2 compliance.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture: Next.js 15 App Router frontend served via CloudFront with signed cookies, communicating with a Python FastAPI backend on ECS Fargate over HTTPS. The recommendation engine queries PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector HNSW index for ANN search across 512-dimensional audio mood embeddings derived from Essentia feature extraction. Scene briefs flow through a brief-to-embedding pipeline that retrieves top-50 mood-matched catalog candidates and scores them with a composite rank weighting mood similarity at 50%, clearance feasibility at 30%, and catalog freshness at 20%. Top-3 candidates are enqueued on SQS for GPT-4o narrative justification via a dedicated Celery worker service. Auth0 with RBAC and mandatory MFA gates all supervisor access; per-project NDA scoping is enforced via project_access_policies checked at every FastAPI dependency boundary, not merely JWT claims.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 15, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query with refetchInterval for justification job polling, Zustand for supervisor session state. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Celery with SQS broker, Essentia for audio feature extraction. AI: OpenAI GPT-4o with structured output, pgvector 0.7 with HNSW index. Database: PostgreSQL 16, Redis 7. Infrastructure: AWS ECS Fargate, SQS, S3 with KMS, CloudFront with signed cookies, Secrets Manager, ECR. Auth: Auth0 with MFA. Observability: Datadog APM with custom ML spans, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Scene Brief Ingestion: supervisors submit mood descriptors, tempo_bpm, emotional arc tags, and optional reference audio; brief stored AES-256 encrypted and indexed within 30s. Cue Recommendation Engine: pgvector HNSW ANN retrieves top-50 mood-matched catalog tracks, composite-ranked and returned as top-10 with clearance flags. LLM Cue Justification: top-3 candidates sent to GPT-4o asynchronously; structured JSON rationale with mood_alignment_score returned via polling. Sync License Management: clearance requests tracked through negotiating, cleared, and declined states with rights holder contacts. Catalog Administration: batch S3 audio ingestion with Essentia feature extraction and HNSW index updates. Supervisor Analytics: per-film recommendation funnel from cue view through shortlist to clearance conversion.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront Distribution with signed cookie auth for scene brief assets. Next.js 15 App Router with SSR and Suspense streaming for LLM justification rendering. FastAPI API Service on ECS Fargate with recommendation, clearance, and auth modules. SQS Queues for catalog-ingest and llm-justification job decoupling. Celery Batch Worker Service on ECS Fargate running CatalogIngestionWorker, LLMJustificationWorker, and ClearanceFeasibilityUpdater. PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector HNSW index (ef_construction=200, m=16). Redis 7 for recommendation and ANN result caching. S3 buckets for scene-briefs with KMS and catalog-audio-assets with KMS. Auth0 Tenant with RBAC organizations per studio. Secrets Manager for OpenAI key and DB credentials. Datadog Agent sidecar with custom ML spans.
target_output.workflows: Scene Brief to Recommendation: supervisor submits POST /scene-briefs with mood_descriptors[] and tempo_bpm. FastAPI stores AES-256 encrypted brief, runs Essentia embedding on mood descriptors, executes pgvector HNSW ANN query against catalog_embeddings returning top-50 candidates, computes composite_rank_score for each candidate, returns ranked top-10 within 30s. Top-3 candidates enqueued on llm-justification SQS queue. LLM Justification Async Flow: LLMJustificationWorker dequeues candidates, constructs GPT-4o prompt with scene context and track metadata including title, artist, genre, BPM, and mood_tags, calls OpenAI structured output API, parses JSON rationale into cue_recommendations.llm_justification_json, updates status to complete. Supervisor polls GET /recommendations/{brief_id}/justifications with React Query refetchInterval. Catalog Ingestion Batch: label delivers audio to S3 catalog-audio-assets bucket triggering SQS event. CatalogIngestionWorker runs Essentia BPM, key, and mood extraction, generates 512-dim embedding, upserts catalog_entry and catalog_embedding, increments HNSW index. Nightly ClearanceFeasibilityUpdater refreshes clearance_status flags from rights holder API.
target_output.data_model: film_projects (id UUID PK, studio_id FK, title_encrypted TEXT, status ENUM active/closed, nda_scoped BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). scenes (id UUID PK, project_id FK, brief_text_encrypted TEXT, reference_audio_s3_key TEXT, mood_descriptors_json JSONB, tempo_bpm FLOAT, emotional_arc_tags TEXT[], created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). cue_recommendations (id UUID PK, scene_id FK, track_id FK, composite_rank_score FLOAT, clearance_feasibility_score FLOAT, mood_similarity_score FLOAT, llm_justification_json JSONB, shortlisted_by UUID, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). catalog_entries (id UUID PK, isrc CHAR(12) UNIQUE, title TEXT, artist TEXT, genre TEXT, bpm FLOAT, musical_key TEXT, mood_tags_json JSONB, rights_holder_id FK, clearance_status ENUM available/restricted/pending, indexed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). catalog_embeddings (id UUID PK, catalog_entry_id FK UNIQUE, embedding vector(512), model_version TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ) with HNSW index. sync_licenses (id UUID PK, track_id FK, project_id FK, license_type ENUM blanket/per_use/festival, territory TEXT, term_years INT, fee_usd NUMERIC(12,2), status ENUM negotiating/cleared/declined, cleared_at TIMESTAMPTZ). rights_holders (id UUID PK, name TEXT, contact_email TEXT, society_affiliation TEXT, payout_currency TEXT).
target_output.api_design: POST /scene-briefs — submit mood_descriptors[], tempo_bpm, optional reference_audio_s3_key; returns brief_id and status indexing. GET /recommendations/{brief_id} — ranked top-10 cue_recommendations with composite scores and clearance flags. GET /recommendations/{brief_id}/justifications — LLM justifications for top-3 cues; status field is pending or complete. PATCH /recommendations/{rec_id}/shortlist — supervisor marks cue for director review. POST /sync-licenses — initiates clearance request for track and project combination. GET /sync-licenses/{track_id} — clearance history and current status. POST /catalog/ingest-batch — admin endpoint triggering S3 batch ingestion job. GET /catalog/index-status — HNSW index size, last_reindex_at, and pending_ingest_count. All endpoints require Auth0 JWT; project-scoped endpoints validate project_access_policies in FastAPI dependency.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 PKCE with mandatory MFA via TOTP or WebAuthn; Auth0 organizations scope film_project visibility per JWT audience claim. Application-layer authorization via project_access_policies table checked in FastAPI dependency on every recommendation and clearance endpoint; JWT claims provide identity only. AES-256 application-layer encryption for scene brief text before PostgreSQL storage; S3 KMS for scene brief and catalog audio buckets. CloudFront signed cookies with 15-minute TTL for protected asset delivery. WAF on ALB blocking SQLi, XSS, rate-limiting to 200 req/min per supervisor. Immutable audit_events table logs all scene brief access, recommendation retrieval, shortlist actions, and license requests with supervisor_id, film_project_id, IP, and timestamp. GDPR: scene briefs purged 90 days post-project, supervisor erasure API anonymizes audit_events. SOC 2 controls: quarterly access reviews, 90-day Secrets Manager rotation, CloudTrail S3 access logging.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate with two services: api-service scaling 2 to 8 tasks on p95 latency target 500ms, and batch-worker scaling 1 to 20 tasks on SQS catalog-ingest queue depth with scale-in after 5-minute idle. CloudFront serves Next.js static assets from S3 frontend-assets bucket with OAC; API proxied through ALB. Three environments: dev single-AZ with 100K catalog seed for local testing, staging multi-AZ with full catalog subset for integration validation, production multi-AZ us-east-1a and 1c with 5M track catalog and RDS Multi-AZ failover. GitHub Actions CI: ruff lint, mypy strict type check, pytest 85% coverage gate on recommendation and clearance modules, Docker build and ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with minimum healthy 50%. Terraform Cloud manages VPC, ECS cluster, RDS PostgreSQL with pgvector, SQS queues, S3 buckets, CloudFront distribution, and WAF. Secrets Manager rotation on 90-day schedule for OpenAI API key and DB credentials.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM with custom spans: embedding_search targeting p95 under 5s, llm_justification_call targeting p95 under 8s, clearance_lookup targeting p95 under 500ms. Custom metrics: recommendation_latency_p95, llm_call_success_rate, catalog_index_lag_hours, cue_shortlist_conversion_rate, clearance_feasibility_accuracy via weekly ground-truth comparison against manually cleared cues. PagerDuty alerts: embedding p95 over 25s, LLM error rate over 2%, SQS ingest queue depth over 1000, RDS connections over 80%. Structured JSON logs with correlation IDs propagated from scene brief submission through ranking and LLM justification job completion.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $3,200/month for 500 daily scene briefs: API Fargate 2 to 8 tasks at $800, batch worker burst scaling at $400, PostgreSQL RDS db.r6g.2xlarge at $600, ElastiCache Redis at $180, S3 and CloudFront at $220, OpenAI GPT-4o at $0.05 per recommendation set for 500 daily totaling $750. Reserved Fargate baseline cuts 30% for steady-state API tasks. pgvector HNSW supports 20M embeddings with quarterly index re-tuning; SQS decoupling absorbs catalog ingestion spikes without API service impact.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP 12 weeks: scene brief submission and encryption, pgvector HNSW composite-rank recommendation, GPT-4o LLM justification for top-3 cues with async polling, supervisor review UI, Auth0 RBAC with MFA, project_access_policies at FastAPI dependency layer, S3 KMS storage, immutable audit_events, GitHub Actions CI/CD, Datadog APM. Catalog seeded with 100K tracks. Phase 2 8 weeks: sync license request workflow, nightly ClearanceFeasibilityUpdater from rights holder API, label catalog ingestion pipeline scaling to 5M tracks, GDPR erasure API, SOC 2 controls documentation and quarterly access review process. Phase 3 6 weeks: recommendation analytics dashboard tracking shortlist and clearance conversion rates, cue shortlist PDF export for director pitches, A/B testing of composite rank weight configurations, embedding model versioning with recall benchmarking.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: ruff for linting, mypy strict mode, pytest with asyncio support, 85% coverage gate on recommendation and clearance modules. TypeScript: strict mode, ESLint, Prettier, Playwright E2E for scene brief submission and recommendation polling flows. FastAPI dependency injection for project_access_policies checks — never inline authorization logic in route handlers. pgvector HNSW parameters documented in ADR; any tuning change requires recall regression test against supervisor-validated cue set. LLM prompt versions tracked in git with offline eval results; no prompt changes without eval comparison run. PRs touching recommendation ranking or LLM prompt logic require 2 senior approvals.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: pgvector HNSW recall degrades if catalog grows past 10M tracks without re-tuning ef_construction and m. Mitigation: monthly recall benchmarking against supervisor-validated cue validation set; quarterly re-tune. Risk: OpenAI API outage blocks LLM justification delivery. Mitigation: async polling means ranked recommendations are immediately visible to supervisors; cached prior justifications surfaced with staleness indicator when LLM is unavailable. Risk: studio NDA breach via insufficient project scoping. Mitigation: project_access_policies enforced at every FastAPI dependency, not only route middleware. Tradeoff: pgvector over dedicated Pinecone simplifies ops but requires DBA attention for HNSW index health at 5M+ vectors. Tradeoff: composite rank weights are heuristic and must be A/B tested in Phase 3 against supervisor shortlist conversion data.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected pgvector HNSW over Pinecone for operational simplicity at 5M track scale, avoiding a separate vector DB dependency. Confirmed GPT-4o structured output over function calling for justification schema reliability. Async SQS polling pattern chosen over WebSockets for LLM job status to simplify infrastructure. Clearance feasibility score sourced from nightly rights holder API refresh rather than real-time per-query lookup to avoid dependency on external API SLA.

metadata.notes: AI music supervision platform with pgvector ANN search, GPT-4o cue justifications, composite ranking with clearance feasibility, and per-project NDA access scoping on AWS Fargate.

project_goal.value: Replace a legacy SOAP-based music metadata lookup service with a modern REST API, consolidating data from multiple inconsistent legacy sources into a unified catalog endpoint for partner integrations
project_goal.rationale: Legacy SOAP service is unmaintainable and partners require REST for modern integration workflows

target_users.value: Distribution partners and DSPs consuming catalog metadata for track, album, and artist lookups via API
target_users.rationale: External partner integrations are primary consumers of the catalog API

project_class.rationale: Pure API service with no frontend; exposes catalog data to partner systems

capabilities.rationale: REST API with OAuth2 auth, catalog data access, and legacy system integration adapter

complexity_level.rationale: SOAP compatibility layer adds complexity but catalog data model is straightforward

risk_level.rationale: Partner-facing API with proprietary catalog data; migration risk from dual-write period

data_sensitivity.rationale: Music catalog metadata is proprietary internal commercial data under distribution agreements

external_exposure.rationale: API exposed to authenticated distribution partners and DSPs only

access_model.rationale: Machine-to-machine OAuth2 standard for partner API authentication

feature_scope.rationale: Core catalog API capabilities for partner consumption

mvp_scope.value: Track and album lookup endpoints with ISRC and UPC identifiers, OAuth2 authentication, SOAP compatibility adapter for legacy partner migrations
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for first partner migration wave

security_baseline.value: OAuth2 client credentials via Cognito, TLS 1.3, API Gateway throttling 1000 req/s per partner, WAF on API Gateway
security_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing API requires token-based auth and rate limiting

frontend_stack.value: None — API service with no frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: API-only service requires no user interface

backend_stack.rationale: Go's concurrency model and low memory footprint suit Lambda cold-start constraints

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS for unified catalog; Redis 7 on ElastiCache for catalog lookup cache
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for catalog entities with Redis caching for high-frequency lookups

hosting_target.value: AWS Lambda with API Gateway HTTP API
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless reduces operational overhead for a catalog lookup API with variable traffic patterns

privacy_retention_policy.value: Catalog records retained indefinitely; access logs retained 90 days; API audit logs retained 1 year
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Catalog data is perpetual; audit logs per security policy

future_scope.value: GraphQL API layer, webhook notifications for catalog updates, real-time catalog change streaming
future_scope.rationale: Partner demand for richer query and event-driven integration patterns

constraints.rationale: Partner migration timeline and latency SLA requirements

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for Lambda metrics and API Gateway access logs; X-Ray for distributed tracing; custom metrics for cache hit rate and reconciliation conflict count
observability_baseline.rationale: AWS-native observability stack aligned with Lambda hosting

execution_preference.value: CI/CD via GitHub Actions with Serverless Framework or SAM for Lambda deployments; blue/green deployment for Lambda function versions
execution_preference.rationale: Serverless deployment tooling for Lambda lifecycle management

compliance_context.value: Proprietary catalog data under label distribution agreements; GDPR applies to artist personal data in catalog records
compliance_context.rationale: Distribution agreement confidentiality and GDPR for artist metadata

project.goal: Replace legacy SOAP music catalog API with modern REST service
project.target_users: Distribution partners and DSPs
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks, full partner migration in 12 months

backend.api: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.1; SOAP compatibility adapter layer

security.auth: OAuth2 client credentials via Cognito
security.rate_limiting: API Gateway throttling 1000 req/s per partner client

data.cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache for ISRC/UPC lookup cache (TTL 1 hour)

devops.deployment: AWS SAM for Lambda function management

constraints.single_source_of_truth: All catalog writes to new PostgreSQL; legacy sources read-only

reasoner_reviews.product: The catalog consolidation scope is well-defined. The SOAP compatibility adapter is critical for the 12-month partner migration window — it must translate SOAP envelope payloads to the new REST data model without data loss. The data reconciliation service for legacy source conflicts is the highest-risk component and deserves explicit conflict resolution rules in the architecture.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with Chi on Lambda is an excellent choice — Go binaries have sub-100ms cold starts, and Chi's lightweight routing fits Lambda well. Redis caching of ISRC and UPC lookups with 1-hour TTL is appropriate given catalog data changes infrequently. The round 1 plan left the conflict resolution rules for the reconciliation service underspecified — the tie-breaking logic (which legacy source wins on field-level conflicts) needs to be explicit in the architecture. Also, the SOAP adapter should translate at the HTTP layer as an API Gateway mapping template, not in application code, to isolate legacy complexity.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth2 client credentials with Cognito is correct for machine-to-machine partner auth. API Gateway throttling per client prevents a single partner from overwhelming the Lambda concurrency pool. WAF rules should include geographic restriction for partners with known regions. Artist personal data fields (name, contact) require GDPR deletion capability — the catalog record delete endpoint must exist and be documented.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: p99 200ms latency is achievable with Lambda Go cold start + Redis cache hit path. Warm Lambda with Redis cache should return in 20-40ms. Cold start with RDS fallback may hit 150-180ms — acceptable within SLA but worth monitoring. SOAP sunset at 12 months requires a partner migration tracker to identify which partners are still using the legacy adapter.

backend.service_design: Go Lambda handler with Chi router. Domain packages: catalog (lookup, version history), reconciliation (conflict detection, resolution), soap_adapter (SOAP envelope parsing, field mapping), partner (auth, rate limit context).
backend.api_patterns: GET /v1/tracks/{isrc} — returns unified track metadata. GET /v1/albums/{upc} — returns album with track listing. GET /v1/artists/{id} — returns artist profile. GET /v1/tracks/{isrc}/versions — returns metadata version history. POST /v1/catalog/soap — SOAP adapter endpoint accepting text/xml, returning SOAP response envelope. All responses include X-Catalog-Source header indicating data origin.
backend.failure_handling: Redis cache miss falls through to PostgreSQL with X-Ray trace segment. RDS connection timeout (200ms threshold) returns 503 with Retry-After header. Reconciliation service failures are non-blocking — catalog read continues with last-known-good record, conflict logged to reconciliation_conflicts table.
backend.scaling_notes: Lambda concurrency limit 500 per partner client enforced at API Gateway. Reserved concurrency 200 for SOAP adapter endpoint to prevent legacy traffic from starving REST endpoints. RDS connection pooling via RDS Proxy to handle Lambda burst connections.

security.auth_design: Cognito user pool with app clients per distribution partner. Client credentials flow: partner posts client_id and client_secret to /oauth2/token, receives JWT. API Gateway JWT authorizer validates token and injects partner_id claim into Lambda event.
security.authorization_model: Catalog partitions scoped per partner via Cognito custom claims. Partner A can only query catalog records associated with their label partition. Admin scope required for reconciliation conflict review endpoints.
security.abuse_prevention: API Gateway throttling: 1000 req/s burst per partner client, 500 req/s steady state. WAF rule for geographic restriction per partner IP allowlist. X-Ray trace anomaly detection for unusual access patterns.

data.entities: catalog_records (id UUID PK, isrc CHAR(12), upc CHAR(13), title TEXT, artist_id FK, album_id FK, release_date DATE, label_id FK, status ENUM active/takedown, metadata_source TEXT, last_reconciled_at TIMESTAMPTZ). albums (id UUID PK, upc CHAR(13), title, artist_id FK, release_date, track_count, label_id FK). artists (id UUID PK, name, isni CHAR(16), country, created_at). legacy_source_mappings (id UUID PK, legacy_source TEXT, legacy_id TEXT, catalog_record_id FK, field_map JSONB, last_synced_at TIMESTAMPTZ). reconciliation_conflicts (id UUID PK, catalog_record_id FK, field_name TEXT, source_a TEXT, value_a TEXT, source_b TEXT, value_b TEXT, resolved_by TEXT, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for catalog. RDS Proxy for Lambda connection pooling. Redis ElastiCache for ISRC/UPC-keyed lookup cache. S3 for SOAP request/response audit log archive.
data.consistency_notes: Catalog writes are always to PostgreSQL. Legacy sources are read-only reference during migration. Reconciliation service polls legacy sources hourly, detects field-level divergence, applies priority rules (primary source wins on title and release_date; ISNI/ISRC authoritative source wins on identifiers), logs unresolvable conflicts to reconciliation_conflicts table for manual review.

ISS-001.title: Reconciliation conflict resolution rules not specified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described a reconciliation service but did not define the field-level conflict resolution priority rules, leaving ambiguity about which legacy source wins when multiple sources disagree on catalog record fields.

ISS-002.title: SOAP adapter implemented in application code rather than API Gateway mapping
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan embedded SOAP-to-REST translation logic inside the Go Lambda handler, coupling legacy protocol complexity to application business logic and complicating future SOAP sunset.

ISS-003.title: No Lambda reserved concurrency for SOAP adapter endpoint
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 plan had no reserved concurrency separation between SOAP and REST endpoints, risking legacy SOAP burst traffic starving REST partner requests.

history.note: Single Lambda function pool for all endpoints

previous_plan.title: Music Catalog REST API Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Go 1.22 Chi router on AWS Lambda with API Gateway, PostgreSQL 16 for unified catalog, Redis ISRC/UPC cache, and SOAP compatibility adapter for legacy partner migration.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Lambda function with Chi router handling REST and SOAP catalog lookups, Redis cache for hot ISRC/UPC paths, PostgreSQL for unified catalog. Reconciliation service polls legacy sources hourly.
previous_plan.api_design: GET /v1/tracks/{isrc}, GET /v1/albums/{upc}, GET /v1/artists/{id}. SOAP endpoint at /v1/catalog/soap in same Lambda handler.
previous_plan.data_model: catalog_records, albums, artists, legacy_source_mappings. Reconciliation conflicts table not yet defined.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 for a SOAP-to-REST music catalog API migration. ISS-001 (missing reconciliation conflict rules) is high severity and must be explicitly resolved with a documented field-level priority source matrix. ISS-002 (SOAP adapter in application code) is resolved by separating SOAP handling into a dedicated Lambda function with API Gateway content-type routing — this isolates legacy complexity and makes the 12-month sunset cleaner. ISS-003 was already resolved with reserved concurrency separation. The core Go Chi Lambda architecture on API Gateway is sound.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined explicit field-level conflict resolution priority matrix in the reconciliation service: ISRC and UPC identifiers — authoritative_identifiers source always wins; title, release_date — primary_catalog source wins over secondary_metadata source; artist_name — primary_catalog wins, ISNI lookup used as tiebreaker via third-party lookup; unresolvable conflicts (both sources disagree with no tiebreaker) flagged to reconciliation_conflicts table for manual editorial review. Priority rules stored in a reconciliation_config JSONB table, editable by catalog admin without code deploy.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Reconciliation service produces deterministic catalog data; unresolvable conflicts are surfaced for human review rather than silently producing inconsistent records.

fix_report.action_taken: Separated SOAP adapter into a dedicated soap-adapter Lambda function. API Gateway content-type routing: requests with Content-Type: text/xml route to soap-adapter Lambda; all other requests route to catalog-api Lambda. The soap-adapter Lambda parses SOAP envelope, extracts identifiers, calls catalog-api internal endpoint, and wraps response in SOAP envelope. The catalog-api Go code has no SOAP parsing logic.
fix_report.expected_outcome: SOAP adapter can be decommissioned independently when all partners migrate; catalog-api codebase is free of legacy protocol concerns; reserved concurrency on soap-adapter Lambda isolates SOAP burst traffic.

target_output.title: Music Catalog REST API — Reconciliation Rules and SOAP Adapter Isolation
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 serverless REST API on AWS Lambda and API Gateway that replaces a legacy SOAP catalog lookup service, consolidating metadata from multiple inconsistent legacy distribution sources into a unified PostgreSQL catalog. Round 2 resolves the reconciliation conflict priority rules with an explicit field-level source matrix and isolates the SOAP compatibility adapter into a separate Lambda function with content-type routing, cleanly separating legacy protocol concerns from the catalog business logic.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two Lambda functions behind API Gateway: catalog-api (Go 1.22 Chi, handles all REST endpoints) and soap-adapter (Go, handles SOAP-to-REST translation via content-type routing). API Gateway routes Content-Type: text/xml requests to soap-adapter, which parses the SOAP envelope, extracts ISRC or UPC identifier, calls the catalog-api internal endpoint, and returns a SOAP response envelope. This isolation allows the soap-adapter function to be decommissioned when the 12-month partner migration sunset completes without touching the catalog-api codebase. PostgreSQL 16 on RDS via RDS Proxy is the single authoritative catalog store. Redis 7 on ElastiCache caches ISRC and UPC lookups with 1-hour TTL for the hot read path. A reconciliation service runs as a scheduled Lambda every hour, polling legacy sources, applying a configurable field-level priority matrix, and flagging unresolvable conflicts. Cognito OAuth2 client credentials authenticate partner API clients with catalog partition scoping.
target_output.technology_stack: API: Go 1.22 with Chi router, aws-lambda-go adapter, go-redis client, pgx PostgreSQL driver. SOAP Adapter: Go 1.22 with encoding/xml for SOAP envelope parsing. Infrastructure: AWS Lambda (catalog-api and soap-adapter functions), API Gateway HTTP API, RDS Proxy. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ. Cache: Redis 7 on ElastiCache. Auth: Cognito user pool with client credentials app clients. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with AWS SAM. Observability: CloudWatch, X-Ray tracing, custom CloudWatch metrics. IaC: AWS SAM template.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Catalog Lookup: partners query by ISRC for track metadata, UPC for album metadata, or artist ID for artist profiles. Version History: GET /v1/tracks/{isrc}/versions returns changelog of catalog record mutations with source attribution. SOAP Compatibility: legacy partner SOAP requests are transparently proxied through soap-adapter Lambda to catalog REST endpoints, returning valid SOAP response envelopes. Data Reconciliation: hourly service detects field-level conflicts between legacy sources, applies priority matrix, updates unified catalog, logs unresolvable conflicts. Partner Migration Tracker: admin endpoint returning SOAP adapter usage per partner to track sunset progress.
target_output.system_components: API Gateway HTTP API with two routes: ANY /{proxy+} to catalog-api Lambda (reserved concurrency 300), POST /v1/catalog/soap to soap-adapter Lambda (reserved concurrency 100). RDS Proxy in front of PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for Lambda connection pooling. Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster (cache.r7g.large) for ISRC/UPC lookup cache. Cognito user pool with per-partner app clients and custom scope claims. Reconciliation Lambda (scheduled EventBridge rule every 60 minutes). Legacy source adapters: read-only database connectors to two legacy MySQL catalog systems. X-Ray active tracing on all Lambda functions.
target_output.workflows: REST Catalog Lookup: Partner authenticates via POST to Cognito /oauth2/token with client_id and client_secret, receives JWT. Sends GET /v1/tracks/{isrc} with Authorization Bearer token. API Gateway JWT authorizer validates token, injects partner_id claim. catalog-api Lambda checks Redis cache for isrc key — on hit returns cached metadata in <5ms. On miss, queries PostgreSQL catalog_records for ISRC, writes result to Redis with 3600s TTL, returns response. X-Ray segment logged with cache_hit label. SOAP Compatibility Lookup: Legacy partner sends SOAP envelope with CatalogLookup operation to POST /v1/catalog/soap. API Gateway content-type routing directs to soap-adapter Lambda. soap-adapter parses XML envelope, extracts ISRC from LookupRequest element, invokes catalog-api Lambda directly via SDK, receives JSON response, constructs SOAP response envelope, returns to partner. Data Reconciliation: EventBridge rule triggers reconciliation Lambda every 60 minutes. Lambda reads reconciliation_config for field-level priority rules. Queries legacy MySQL source A and source B for catalog records updated since last_synced_at. For each record, applies priority matrix per field (e.g., title: source_a wins; ISRC: authoritative_identifiers source wins). Updates catalog_records in PostgreSQL. Fields where both sources disagree with no tiebreaker rule are inserted into reconciliation_conflicts table with source_a_value, source_b_value, and status=pending_review. Catalog admin reviews conflicts via GET /v1/admin/reconciliation/conflicts.
target_output.data_model: catalog_records (id UUID PK, isrc CHAR(12), upc CHAR(13), title TEXT, artist_id UUID FK, album_id UUID FK, release_date DATE, label_id UUID FK, status ENUM active/takedown, metadata_source TEXT, last_reconciled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, version_counter INT). albums (id UUID PK, upc CHAR(13), title TEXT, artist_id UUID FK, release_date DATE, track_count INT, label_id UUID FK). artists (id UUID PK, name TEXT, isni CHAR(16), country CHAR(2), created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). legacy_source_mappings (id UUID PK, legacy_source TEXT, legacy_id TEXT, catalog_record_id UUID FK, last_synced_at TIMESTAMPTZ). reconciliation_conflicts (id UUID PK, catalog_record_id UUID FK, field_name TEXT, source_a TEXT, value_a TEXT, source_b TEXT, value_b TEXT, status ENUM pending_review/resolved/overridden, resolved_by TEXT, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). reconciliation_config (id UUID PK, field_name TEXT UNIQUE, priority_source TEXT, tiebreaker_strategy TEXT, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). partner_migration_status (id UUID PK, partner_id TEXT, last_soap_request_at TIMESTAMPTZ, soap_request_count_30d INT, rest_request_count_30d INT, migration_status ENUM soap_only/transitioning/rest_only).
target_output.api_design: GET /v1/tracks/{isrc} — returns {isrc, title, artist, album_upc, release_date, label, metadata_source, version_counter}. GET /v1/albums/{upc} — returns album object with tracks array. GET /v1/artists/{id} — returns artist with ISNI and country. GET /v1/tracks/{isrc}/versions — returns array of catalog_record mutations with changed_fields and source. POST /v1/catalog/soap — accepts text/xml SOAP envelope, returns SOAP response (handled by soap-adapter Lambda). GET /v1/admin/reconciliation/conflicts — returns pending conflicts for editorial review. PATCH /v1/admin/reconciliation/conflicts/{id} — resolves conflict with chosen value. GET /v1/admin/partners/migration-status — returns per-partner SOAP vs REST usage metrics. All REST endpoints require Cognito JWT Authorization header. Admin endpoints require admin scope.
target_output.security_and_compliance: OAuth2 client credentials via Cognito with per-partner app clients. JWT includes partner_id and catalog_scope claims; catalog-api enforces label partition access per partner_id. TLS 1.3 on API Gateway. API Gateway throttling: 1000 req/s burst per Cognito client. WAF on API Gateway: SQLi and XSS rules, geographic restriction rules configurable per partner. RDS encrypted at rest with AWS KMS. Redis auth token via ElastiCache in-transit encryption. GDPR: artist personal data (name, ISNI) deletable via catalog admin endpoint; delete cascades to legacy_source_mappings. Distribution agreement confidentiality: catalog records scoped to partner label partitions via Cognito claims. API access logs retained 1 year in CloudWatch for audit trail.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: Go unit tests (reconciliation priority logic, SOAP parsing), go build for catalog-api and soap-adapter binaries, sam build, integration tests against LocalStack. sam deploy to staging on PR merge to staging branch; to production on merge to main with manual approval gate. Lambda versions: blue/green via Lambda aliases (catalog-api:production, catalog-api:staging). Rollback: alias pointer reverted to previous version in <30 seconds. RDS snapshot before any reconciliation config changes. EventBridge schedule for reconciliation Lambda: rate(60 minutes). CloudWatch alarms: Lambda error rate >1%, p99 latency >200ms, reconciliation_conflict_count >50 in 24h.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch custom metrics: catalog_cache_hit_rate (target >90%), reconciliation_conflict_count_daily, soap_adapter_request_count_per_partner, catalog_lookup_p99_latency. X-Ray trace map showing catalog-api, Redis, RDS, and legacy source adapter segments per request. CloudWatch dashboard: cache hit rate trend, ISRC lookup latency distribution, reconciliation conflict backlog, partner SOAP-to-REST migration progress (from partner_migration_status table). Alarms: cache hit rate <80% for 30 min (Redis eviction pressure), p99 latency >200ms for 10 min (RDS bottleneck), reconciliation error rate >5% per run (legacy source connectivity).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $350/month at 5 million catalog lookups/month. Lambda: $30/month (5M invocations, 128MB, avg 50ms). API Gateway: $17.50/month. RDS Multi-AZ (db.t4g.medium): $120/month. ElastiCache (cache.r7g.large): $80/month. CloudWatch: $50/month. Total ~$300-400/month. Cache hit rate >90% keeps RDS load minimal. Lambda auto-scales to API Gateway concurrency limits. RDS Proxy handles Lambda connection burst during traffic spikes. Scale path: RDS read replica if reconciliation service queries impact p99 latency on lookup path.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): catalog-api Lambda with ISRC and UPC lookup endpoints, Cognito OAuth2 auth, Redis caching, PostgreSQL catalog schema and initial data migration from legacy source A. GitHub Actions CI/CD with SAM deploy. Phase 2 SOAP Adapter (3 weeks): soap-adapter Lambda with SOAP envelope parsing, API Gateway content-type routing, partner migration status tracking. First partner migrated to REST. Phase 3 Reconciliation (3 weeks): reconciliation service Lambda with configurable priority matrix, reconciliation_conflicts table, admin review endpoints. Legacy source B integrated. All legacy catalog data reconciled into unified PostgreSQL. Phase 4 Sunset (ongoing, months 4-12): partner-by-partner REST migration tracked via migration_status dashboard; soap-adapter Lambda decommissioned when partner_migration_status shows all partners rest_only.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go modules with explicit dependency pinning. Unit tests required for reconciliation priority logic and SOAP envelope parsing — these are the highest-risk components. Table-driven tests for field-level priority rule edge cases. Integration tests using LocalStack for Lambda, API Gateway, and DynamoDB. No shared database connections between catalog-api and soap-adapter — each function uses its own RDS Proxy connection. Reconciliation config changes require a PR with a database migration and updated unit test coverage. X-Ray annotation on all catalog_record lookups with cache_hit=true/false label for observability.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Legacy source unavailability during reconciliation run causes stale catalog data. Mitigation: reconciliation service non-blocking — reads from last-known-good PostgreSQL record on legacy source timeout; logs gap to reconciliation run audit. Risk: Redis cache holds stale data after catalog record takedown event. Mitigation: takedown events trigger explicit cache key DELETE in catalog-api before returning takedown status. Risk: Partner SOAP sunset pressure if 12-month timeline slips. Mitigation: partner_migration_status dashboard exposes per-partner progress; account team uses data to prioritize migration support. Tradeoff: Lambda over containerized service chosen for lower operational overhead at catalog lookup traffic patterns (spiky, not continuous); accepted given p99 200ms SLA is achievable with warm Lambda + Redis cache.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: SOAP adapter separated into distinct Lambda function — catalog-api has no SOAP parsing code. Reconciliation priority matrix stored in database table for editorial control without code deploys. RDS Proxy chosen over direct Lambda RDS connections to handle connection burst. partner_migration_status table added to track SOAP sunset progress per partner.
