project.goal: High-converting landing page for collaborative songwriting SaaS
project.target_users: Songwriters and lyricist duos seeking browser-based co-writing tools

frontend.analytics: Vercel Analytics with custom event hooks

backend.framework: None — static export via next export

data.email: Mailchimp embedded form for lead capture

constraints.runtime: Static export only — no API routes at runtime

reasoner_reviews.product: The landing page content architecture is standard for a SaaS product launch. The live collaboration demo embed is the primary differentiator and must be the visual centerpiece of the hero section. A/B testing on CTA copy is a smart conversion optimization move for a freemium product.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Three rounds of review on a landing page is unusual. The recurring issues reflect a persistent misalignment between the plan's technology choices and the contract's static export constraint. Round 2 introduced a server-side API route for A/B test variant assignment, which violates the contract's 'no server-side API calls at runtime' constraint. This must be corrected to use client-side experiment assignment via Vercel Edge Config or a simple localStorage-based random assignment. Next.js 14 static export mode does not support API routes or server components with dynamic data — the plan must strictly use pages with getStaticProps or App Router with generateStaticParams only.
reasoner_reviews.security: Rounds 1 and 2 both omitted the CSP header configuration in next.config.js. This remains unresolved. The Mailchimp form script must be allowed in script-src. The demo embed iframe origin must be in frame-src. Without these, the browser blocks the third-party Mailchimp form and the collaborative demo embed.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The Lighthouse >90 constraint is at risk if the demo embed is loaded eagerly. The embed should be deferred with an IntersectionObserver-based lazy load, replacing with a thumbnail until in-viewport.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 App Router with static export (output: export in next.config.js). Single route: app/page.tsx. All sections are server components rendering static HTML. No client components except DemoEmbed (lazy), CTAForm (Mailchimp embed), and ABTestCTA (client:idle for experiment assignment).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Single page with sections: Hero (headline, demo embed placeholder, primary CTA), FeaturesGrid (3-column co-writing feature highlights), DemoEmbed (lazy-loaded iframe of live collaboration demo), PricingTiers (Free vs Pro comparison), TestimonialsCarousel (static quotes), FAQ (accordion), Footer (links, legal). No multi-page routing needed for MVP.
frontend.performance_notes: next/image for all hero and feature images with automatic WebP and AVIF output. Demo embed lazy-loaded via IntersectionObserver — shows static thumbnail until user scrolls into view. Tailwind CSS tree-shaken at build. Font loading with font-display: swap to prevent layout shift.
frontend.frontend_security_notes: CSP headers in next.config.js async headers() function: script-src 'self' chimpstatic.com cdn.mcsv.net vercel-insights.com; frame-src 'self' [demo-embed-domain]; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'. Applied to all routes via matcher: ['/(.*)']. Mailchimp form embedded via their hosted form URL in an iframe — no Mailchimp JS injected into page context.

security.auth_design: No authentication. Public static page.
security.abuse_prevention: Vercel DDoS protection at edge. No server-side endpoints to abuse. Mailchimp handles spam protection for email form submissions.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Vercel Analytics aggregate page views and Web Vitals. Custom analytics events: cta_clicked (variant, position), demo_played, signup_submitted tracked via Vercel Analytics track() API.

devops.deployment_topology: Vercel project connected to GitHub repository. Static output directory .next/out deployed to Vercel edge network on push to main.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: bun install, bun run build (next build with output:export), lighthouse-ci against built output, deploy to Vercel via Vercel CLI action. PR branches get preview deployment URLs automatically from Vercel.
devops.rollback_strategy: Vercel instant rollback to any prior deployment via Vercel dashboard. Zero-downtime as all deployments are immutable static assets.

ISS-001.title: CSP headers omitted in all prior rounds
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1 and 2 both failed to include CSP header configuration in next.config.js, leaving the Mailchimp form embed and demo iframe blocked by default browser CSP and enabling unrestricted third-party scripts.

previous_plan.title: Collaborative Songwriting Landing Page Architecture v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Next.js 14 static landing page on Vercel with Tailwind CSS, Mailchimp signup, demo embed, and A/B test via /api/ab-assign route.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 App Router deployed to Vercel. A/B testing via /api/ab-assign API route returning variant assignment. Demo embed in hero section as eager iframe.
previous_plan.api_design: GET /api/ab-assign — returns {variant: 'a'|'b'} for CTA copy test. GET / — static home page. POST action via Mailchimp embedded form.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: HTTPS via Vercel. No CSP headers configured. Mailchimp handles email data.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 on a landing page — the plan has been getting worse, not better. Round 2 introduced a server-side API route for A/B testing that is fundamentally incompatible with Next.js static export mode. This is a contract violation: the contract explicitly states no server-side API calls at runtime and static export deployment. The fix is to replace the API route with a client-side localStorage random assignment in a useEffect hook. CSP headers have been missing for all three rounds and must be added to next.config.js headers(). Demo embed must be lazy-loaded. These are the blocking flaws — plan quality is intentionally flawed to train the agent to recognize contract violations and persistent recurring issues.

fix_report.action_taken: Removed /api/ab-assign API route entirely — it does not exist in Next.js static export output. Replaced with ABTestCTA client component using useEffect on mount to read or assign a random variant (Math.random() < 0.5 ? 'a' : 'b') stored in localStorage key 'cta_variant'. Variant persists across sessions for consistent user experience. Vercel Analytics track('cta_clicked', {variant}) fires on CTA button click.
fix_report.expected_outcome: A/B test variant assignment works in static export mode with no server-side calls; variant persists in localStorage for returning visitors; analytics events track variant performance.

fix_report.action_taken: Added async headers() function to next.config.js applying Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, and Referrer-Policy headers to all routes. CSP script-src allows 'self', vercel-insights.com (Vercel Analytics). CSP frame-src allows 'self' and app.cowrite.io (demo embed domain). Mailchimp form embedded via hosted iframe URL — no Mailchimp JavaScript injected into page.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Browser enforces CSP; Mailchimp hosted form iframe and co-writing demo embed load correctly; unrestricted third-party scripts blocked.

fix_report.action_taken: Demo embed iframe wrapped in DemoEmbed client component using IntersectionObserver — shows a static thumbnail image (next/image, pre-optimized AVIF) until the section scrolls into viewport, then replaces with iframe. This removes 800KB iframe from initial load path, recovering Lighthouse performance score.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Lighthouse performance score meets >90 constraint; demo embed renders correctly after scroll; thumbnail provides visual context before load.

target_output.title: Collaborative Songwriting Landing Page — Static Export Compliance and CSP Fix
target_output.executive_summary: A Next.js 14 static export landing page on Vercel for a real-time collaborative songwriting tool, featuring a live demo embed, freemium CTA with Mailchimp signup, and client-side A/B testing. Round 3 corrects a contract-violating API route introduced in round 2 by replacing it with localStorage-based client-side variant assignment, adds missing CSP headers for the third time of asking, and defers the demo embed iframe to fix Lighthouse performance regression.
target_output.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 App Router with output: 'export' in next.config.js generates a fully static site with no server-side runtime. The built output is a directory of HTML, CSS, and JS files deployed to Vercel's edge network as immutable assets. There are no API routes, server components with dynamic data, or runtime Node.js processes. The page has three client-side interactive components: ABTestCTA (localStorage variant assignment on mount, no server call), DemoEmbed (IntersectionObserver lazy iframe load), and CTAForm (Mailchimp hosted form iframe). All other sections are static HTML rendered at build time. CSP headers are configured in next.config.js headers() and applied by Vercel at the edge.
target_output.technology_stack: Framework: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, output: export mode. Styling: Tailwind CSS with @tailwindcss/typography. Images: next/image with AVIF/WebP output. Analytics: Vercel Analytics with custom track() events. Email capture: Mailchimp hosted form via iframe embed. Hosting: Vercel Edge Network. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Bun, Lighthouse CI. Content updates: direct TypeScript/MDX edits — no CMS for MVP.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero Section: headline targeting co-writer use case ('Write together, anywhere'), primary CTA button with A/B variant copy ('Start for free' vs 'Try it free'), demo embed thumbnail with play-to-load behavior. Features Grid: three highlights specific to co-writing — simultaneous lyric editing, real-time chord chart sync, version history for lyric drafts. Live Demo Embed: IntersectionObserver-triggered iframe loading an interactive preview of the co-writing canvas. Pricing Tiers: Free (2 co-writers, unlimited sessions) vs Pro ($12/month, up to 10 co-writers, export to PDF). CTA Signup: Mailchimp hosted form iframe capturing email and songwriter role. Testimonials: static quotes from beta songwriters. FAQ Accordion: client component for expand/collapse.
target_output.system_components: Vercel Edge Network hosting static HTML, CSS, and JS assets globally. Next.js 14 static export build (bun run build generating .next/out directory). next/image optimization generating AVIF/WebP thumbnails at build time. ABTestCTA client component (useEffect, localStorage, Vercel Analytics track). DemoEmbed client component (IntersectionObserver, iframe lazy load, static thumbnail placeholder). CTAForm section embedding Mailchimp hosted form URL in sandboxed iframe. next.config.js headers() applying CSP and security headers at Vercel edge. Vercel Analytics for aggregate page view and custom event metrics. GitHub Actions CI with Lighthouse CI step validating >90 score before deploy.
target_output.workflows: Page Load: Visitor arrives at landing page. Vercel edge serves static HTML with CSP headers. Hero section renders with demo embed thumbnail (not iframe). ABTestCTA client component mounts, reads cta_variant from localStorage; if absent, assigns Math.random() < 0.5 ? 'a' : 'b', stores in localStorage. CTA button renders with variant-appropriate copy. Demo Embed Interaction: As visitor scrolls down, IntersectionObserver in DemoEmbed component fires when embed section enters viewport. Component replaces static thumbnail with co-writing demo iframe loaded from app.cowrite.io. Vercel Analytics track('demo_played') fires. Signup Flow: Visitor clicks CTA button. Vercel Analytics track('cta_clicked', {variant: 'a'|'b', position: 'hero'}) fires. Page scrolls to CTAForm section. Visitor completes Mailchimp hosted form in iframe with email and songwriter role. Mailchimp confirms submission. Vercel Analytics track('signup_submitted') fires via postMessage listener from Mailchimp form.
target_output.data_model: No server-side data model. Client-side only: localStorage key 'cta_variant' (string 'a'|'b') persists A/B test assignment per browser. Analytics events schema (Vercel Analytics): cta_clicked {variant: 'a'|'b', position: 'hero'|'pricing'}, demo_played {}, signup_submitted {}. Page content structured as TypeScript constants in /src/data/: features array, testimonials array, pricing_tiers array, faq_items array — no CMS or database.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. No Next.js API routes — incompatible with static export. Client-side only: Vercel Analytics track() function called on user interactions. Mailchimp form submission handled entirely by Mailchimp's hosted form endpoint via iframe. External demo embed served from app.cowrite.io. All content served as static files from Vercel edge.
target_output.security_and_compliance: next.config.js async headers() applying to matcher: ['/(.*)']: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' vercel-insights.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data: https:; frame-src 'self' list.mailchimp.com app.cowrite.io; connect-src 'self' vercel-insights.com. X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN. X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin. No first-party cookies or personal data stored. Vercel Analytics cookieless — no GDPR consent banner required. Mailchimp manages email list with compliant unsubscribe links. No server-side execution attack surface.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions workflow: bun install, bun run build (next build with output:export), Lighthouse CI against localhost preview of built output (fail build if score <90), Vercel CLI action deploys to Vercel on success. PR branches get Vercel preview URLs automatically. Production deploy on merge to main. Rollback via Vercel dashboard: instant promotion of any prior immutable deployment. Build time under 45 seconds. No server infrastructure to manage.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics dashboard: page views, unique visitors, Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, FID) per deployment. Custom events: cta_clicked with variant property for A/B result comparison, demo_played for demo engagement rate, signup_submitted for overall conversion rate. Lighthouse CI score tracked per build — build fails if <90. Vercel deployment success/failure notifications via GitHub Actions status checks. No server-side metrics needed.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Hobby/Pro plan $0-20/month covers all landing page traffic. Vercel Edge Network auto-scales globally with no configuration. Lighthouse CI via open-source action: free. Mailchimp free tier (up to 500 subscribers) covers launch phase. Total infrastructure cost: ~$0-20/month for typical landing page traffic volumes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Launch (2 weeks): Next.js 14 static export scaffold, Hero with A/B CTA (localStorage assignment), Features Grid, DemoEmbed with IntersectionObserver lazy load, CTAForm with Mailchimp iframe, CSP headers in next.config.js, Lighthouse CI gate, Vercel deployment. Phase 2 Post-launch (2 weeks): Pricing tier section, Testimonials carousel, FAQ accordion, Vercel Analytics A/B result dashboard review and winner implementation. Phase 3 Growth: Localized versions for key markets (es, fr, pt), blog section for content marketing, integration with product waitlist API when available.
target_output.development_guidelines: next.config.js must include output: 'export' — any contributor adding an API route or dynamic server function will see a build error. No getServerSideProps or server-only imports. All interactive components use 'use client' directive and are isolated to ABTestCTA, DemoEmbed, and FAQ accordion. Lighthouse CI must pass >90 before any Vercel production deploy. Images committed as source files (PNG/JPEG) only — next/image handles optimization. No third-party JavaScript injected into page context — only Vercel Analytics script (loaded via next/script strategy='afterInteractive') and iframe embeds.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: localStorage A/B assignment is cleared when visitor clears browser storage, causing variant re-randomization. Mitigation: accepted tradeoff for static export constraint compliance; cookie-based assignment would require cookie consent banner. Risk: Mailchimp hosted form iframe appearance is less customizable than JavaScript-embedded form. Mitigation: accepted to avoid Mailchimp JS in page context (CSP compliance). Risk: IntersectionObserver not supported in very old browsers. Mitigation: polyfill via intersection-observer npm package loaded conditionally. Tradeoff: Next.js over Astro chosen for team familiarity; Astro would offer better islands performance but requires learning curve.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: A/B test implementation: localStorage random assignment chosen over server-side or cookie-based for static export compatibility. Mailchimp embed: hosted form iframe over JS embed for CSP compliance. Demo embed: IntersectionObserver lazy load over eager iframe for Lighthouse performance. API routes: confirmed absent from project — next.config.js output:export setting enforces this at build time.

metadata.notes: Round 3 landing page with intentional prior-round flaws; round 2 introduced API route violating static export constraint; round 3 corrects violations and resolves persistent CSP gap.

project_goal.value: Enable independent music producers to publish and monetize beat licenses with tiered non-exclusive, exclusive, and unlimited licensing, previews, and self-service checkout
project_goal.rationale: Direct-to-buyer beat licensing eliminates distributor middlemen and improves producer earnings

target_users.value: Independent hip-hop and R&B beat producers as sellers; recording artists and content creators as buyers
target_users.rationale: Two-sided marketplace with distinct seller and buyer onboarding journeys

project_class.rationale: Browser-first marketplace with producer storefronts, beat preview players, and Stripe checkout

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, payments, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Marketplace requires auth, payment processing, download delivery, and earnings analytics for producers

complexity_level.rationale: Standard marketplace CRUD plus Stripe payment flows and time-limited download link generation

risk_level.rationale: Handles buyer payment data via Stripe and producer payout records; PCI-DSS delegated to Stripe

data_sensitivity.rationale: Producer payout records, buyer purchase history, and Stripe payment metadata are financial data

access_model.rationale: Low friction discovery drives conversion; auth gates protected assets and financial actions

feature_scope.rationale: Full marketplace feature set covering upload-to-payout flow

mvp_scope.value: Beat upload, 3 license tier pricing, streaming preview, Stripe checkout, S3 download grants, producer earnings dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for 20-producer beta launch

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 in transit, Auth0 for authentication, Stripe for payment handling, time-limited signed S3 URLs for beat downloads, CSRF protection
security_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS scope delegated to Stripe; download links must expire to prevent unauthorized redistribution

frontend_stack.rationale: SSR for producer storefront SEO; React ecosystem for interactive beat player

backend_stack.rationale: JavaScript end-to-end with team familiarity; Express for lightweight API layer

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for relational marketplace data; S3 for beat audio files and license PDFs
data_platform.rationale: Relational integrity for purchases and license assignments; S3 for durable audio asset storage

hosting_target.rationale: Cost-effective managed hosting within startup budget; simpler ops than EKS

privacy_retention_policy.value: Purchase and payout records retained 7 years for tax compliance; GDPR right-to-erasure for buyer accounts (anonymize purchase records, delete personal data); beat audio and licenses retained while producer account is active
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial record retention law and GDPR obligations for EU buyers and producers

constraints.rationale: Startup budget ceiling and timeline for beta launch

observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight observability appropriate for startup stage

execution_preference.rationale: Fast iteration cadence for beta feedback

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS delegated to Stripe; GDPR for EU buyer and producer personal and financial data; income reporting obligations for producer payouts via Stripe Connect 1099-K
compliance_context.rationale: Financial data sensitivity and EU user base require explicit compliance treatment

project.goal: Two-sided beat licensing marketplace for independent producers and artists
project.target_users: Beat producers as sellers; recording artists and content creators as buyers
project.timeline: 8-week MVP beta launch, full launch in 4 months

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for player state

security.auth: Auth0 with email and social login
security.payments: Stripe Checkout and Stripe Connect for payouts

data.storage: S3 for beat audio files and license PDFs

reasoner_reviews.product: The tiered licensing model (non-exclusive, exclusive, unlimited) is the marketplace core differentiator. Exclusive licenses should lock the beat from further sale after purchase, which requires careful state management in the beats table. The streaming preview player is critical for conversion — buyers need to audition beats before committing to a license tier.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: DigitalOcean App Platform is appropriate for this scale and budget. Stripe Connect Express is the right payout mechanism for 1099-K compliance and minimizes financial platform liability. Signed S3 URLs with short expiry prevent unauthorized redistribution of purchased beats. The exclusive license state transition (available to sold) should be handled in a database transaction with the purchase record creation to prevent race conditions.
reasoner_reviews.security: Download links must be single-use or short-lived signed S3 URLs generated only after confirmed Stripe webhook receipt, not on payment intent creation. The Stripe webhook endpoint must validate the signature header to prevent replay attacks. Buyer email addresses are PII under GDPR; ensure Auth0 does not store more data than necessary for authentication.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Budget of $400/month on DigitalOcean App Platform supports a basic-size dyno for API, a starter PostgreSQL managed database, and S3 equivalent (DigitalOcean Spaces). This is tight but viable for beta scale with 20 producers and under 1000 monthly purchases.

backend.service_design: Express API with Prisma ORM organized into route modules: beats (CRUD, upload, tagging), licenses (tier management, pricing), checkout (Stripe session creation, webhook handling), downloads (signed S3 URL generation), and producers (earnings, payout dashboard). Stripe webhook handler runs as a dedicated middleware-free route to preserve raw body for signature validation.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0. POST /beats for upload metadata, GET /beats for discovery with filter and sort, GET /beats/{id}/preview for streaming URL, POST /checkout/sessions creates Stripe Checkout session for a license tier, POST /webhooks/stripe handles payment confirmation and triggers download_grant creation, GET /downloads/{grant_id} returns signed S3 URL.
backend.failure_handling: Stripe webhook idempotency: purchase records keyed by Stripe session ID to prevent duplicate grants on webhook retry. Exclusive beat lock via database transaction: purchase INSERT and beat status UPDATE to sold in single Prisma transaction. S3 pre-signed URL generation retried up to 3 times on AWS SDK error before returning 503.
backend.backend_risks: Race condition on exclusive beat purchase if two buyers simultaneously reach Stripe checkout for the same beat. Mitigation: optimistic lock on beats.status in the webhook handler — if status is already sold when webhook arrives, refund is triggered automatically via Stripe refund API and buyer notified.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 15 App Router with public routes for beat discovery and producer storefronts; authenticated routes for buyer purchase history, producer dashboard, and beat upload. Beat preview player built as a client component with Zustand for playback state shared across the discovery grid.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Beat Discovery: filterable grid by genre, BPM, key, price with inline audio player. Producer Storefront: public profile with beat catalog and license tier display. License Selection: modal showing tier comparison (non-exclusive, exclusive, unlimited) with prices. Stripe Checkout: redirect to Stripe-hosted checkout. Post-Purchase: download page with license PDF and audio file links. Producer Dashboard: earnings chart, per-beat sale history, payout initiation via Stripe Connect.
frontend.state_management: React Query for beat catalog, purchase history, and producer earnings API calls. Zustand for audio player state: currently_playing beat_id, playback position, is_playing flag shared across discovery grid and player bar.

security.auth_design: Auth0 with email/password and Google OAuth. Roles: buyer and producer, set at registration. Auth0 access token stored in httpOnly cookie via Auth0 Next.js SDK.
security.authorization_model: Producer-owned resources (beats, earnings) validated by checking beats.producer_id against JWT sub claim in Express middleware. Download grants validated by checking download_grants.buyer_id against JWT sub and grant.used_at is null.
security.abuse_prevention: Stripe webhook signature validation with raw body buffer. Rate limiting on POST /checkout/sessions to 10 requests per minute per IP via express-rate-limit. Download grant single-use enforcement: used_at timestamp set on first access, subsequent requests return 410 Gone.

data.entities: beats (id UUID PK, producer_id FK, title TEXT, genre TEXT, bpm INT, musical_key TEXT, mood_tags TEXT[], audio_s3_key TEXT, preview_s3_key TEXT, status ENUM available/sold/unlisted, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), license_tiers (id UUID PK, beat_id FK, tier_type ENUM non_exclusive/exclusive/unlimited, price_cents INT, license_pdf_s3_key TEXT, max_sales INT NULLABLE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), purchases (id UUID PK, buyer_id FK, beat_id FK, license_tier_id FK, stripe_session_id TEXT UNIQUE, amount_paid_cents INT, stripe_payout_transfer_id TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), download_grants (id UUID PK, purchase_id FK, buyer_id FK, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, used_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), payout_records (id UUID PK, producer_id FK, stripe_transfer_id TEXT UNIQUE, amount_cents INT, status ENUM pending/completed/failed, period_start DATE, period_end DATE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), producer_profiles (id UUID PK, user_id FK UNIQUE, display_name TEXT, bio TEXT, stripe_connect_account_id TEXT, payout_enabled BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 managed on DigitalOcean for all relational marketplace data. DigitalOcean Spaces (S3-compatible) for beat audio files, 30-second preview clips, and license PDF templates. Prisma migrations for schema evolution.
data.retention_and_deletion: Purchase and payout records retained 7 years for tax compliance and not subject to GDPR erasure. GDPR erasure on buyer account: personal data nulled in purchases and download_grants, Auth0 account deleted. Producer erasure: beats unlisted, personal data nulled, stripe_connect_account_id retained for payout audit trail.

devops.deployment_topology: DigitalOcean App Platform: Next.js frontend and Express API as separate app components. DigitalOcean Managed PostgreSQL Basic (1 vCPU, 1GB RAM) scaled to Standard on launch. DigitalOcean Spaces for audio assets with CDN enabled. GitHub Actions CI/CD deploying to App Platform on push to main after test gate.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint, TypeScript type check, Jest unit tests with 75% coverage gate on checkout and download modules, build, deploy to DigitalOcean App Platform via doctl. Separate deploy steps for API and frontend components. Database migrations run via Prisma migrate deploy as a pre-deploy task.
devops.devops_risks: DigitalOcean App Platform has no fine-grained auto-scaling — vertical scaling only. Mitigation: monitor App Platform metrics and upgrade dyno size before beta launch if load testing reveals bottlenecks. S3-compatible Spaces CDN has limited edge presence vs CloudFront; acceptable for beta but plan migration for full launch.

ISS-001.title: Exclusive beat race condition on concurrent checkout
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan did not specify a locking mechanism for exclusive beat purchases. Two concurrent buyers could both reach Stripe checkout for the same exclusive-tier beat, resulting in duplicate purchase grants for a beat that should only sell once.

history.note: Identified during security review of checkout flow

ISS-002.title: Download link generation before webhook confirmation
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan generated download_grants on Stripe payment_intent.created rather than checkout.session.completed webhook, allowing download access before payment is confirmed as captured.

history.note: Identified during security review of Stripe integration

focus_issues.title: Exclusive beat race condition on concurrent checkout
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan did not specify a locking mechanism for exclusive beat purchases — two buyers could purchase simultaneously.

focus_issues.title: Download link generation before webhook confirmation
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan generated download grants on payment_intent.created rather than checkout.session.completed, allowing premature access.

latest_recommendations: Add optimistic locking or FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED on exclusive beat purchase to prevent duplicate grants
Move download_grant creation strictly to checkout.session.completed webhook handler with Stripe signature validation
Document single-use download grant enforcement in the security section

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity checkout security issues unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Solid marketplace architecture on DigitalOcean with good Stripe integration intent, but two high-severity payment security gaps prevent passing: the exclusive beat race condition and premature download grant creation both represent exploitable checkout vulnerabilities.
strengths: Producer and buyer role separation is well designed
License tier comparison modal flow is clear and conversion-optimized
DigitalOcean App Platform choice is appropriate for startup budget
concerns: No locking mechanism specified for exclusive beat concurrent checkout
Download grants created on wrong Stripe event, before payment capture confirmed
blocking_issues: Exclusive beat sold multiple times due to race condition
Buyers gain download access before payment is captured
recommendations: Use Prisma transaction with SELECT FOR UPDATE on beats row in checkout.session.completed webhook to atomically check and set status to sold
Generate download_grants only on checkout.session.completed with validated Stripe-Signature header; reject requests using payment_intent events

issue_updates.title: Exclusive beat race condition
issue_updates.detail: Plan references Stripe checkout but does not address concurrent exclusive tier purchase locking at the database level

issue_updates.title: Premature download grant
issue_updates.detail: Checkout flow description implies grant creation on payment intent rather than confirmed session completion event

previous_plan.title: Beat Licensing Marketplace Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A marketplace enabling independent producers to sell tiered beat licenses to artists, built on Next.js 15 and Express on DigitalOcean App Platform with Stripe for payments and S3-compatible Spaces for audio storage.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Next.js 15 frontend and Express REST API deployed on DigitalOcean App Platform. PostgreSQL 16 for relational marketplace data. Stripe Checkout for payment processing with Connect Express for producer payouts. DigitalOcean Spaces for beat audio and license PDFs.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Auth0 for authentication. Stripe handles PCI-DSS scope. Download links as signed S3 URLs. GDPR erasure for buyer accounts.
previous_plan.workflows: Beat upload: producer uploads audio, sets license tier prices, publishes storefront. Purchase: buyer selects tier, redirected to Stripe, download link generated on payment.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a round 2 revision addressing two high-severity checkout security gaps from the previous audit. ISS-001 requires an exclusive beat lock implemented as a Prisma transaction with row-level locking in the checkout.session.completed webhook handler. ISS-002 requires moving download_grant creation strictly to the checkout.session.completed event with Stripe signature validation, not payment_intent.created. Both fixes must be explicit in the workflows, api_design, and security sections. The overall architecture is sound; the revision is targeted at these two payment security flows.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit optimistic lock on exclusive beat purchase: checkout.session.completed webhook handler now wraps beats.status check and UPDATE in a Prisma transaction using $transaction with SELECT FOR UPDATE, automatically triggering Stripe refund if status is already sold when webhook arrives
fix_report.expected_outcome: Concurrent exclusive beat purchases result in exactly one grant; second buyer receives automatic refund within seconds

fix_report.action_taken: Moved download_grant creation strictly to checkout.session.completed webhook event with Stripe-Signature header validation using stripe.webhooks.constructEvent; removed any download grant logic from payment_intent events; added used_at single-use enforcement
fix_report.expected_outcome: Download access is granted only after Stripe confirms payment capture; replay attacks on the webhook endpoint are rejected via signature validation

target_output.title: Beat Licensing Marketplace Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A two-sided web marketplace for independent beat licensing built on Next.js 15 and Node.js Express on DigitalOcean App Platform, with PostgreSQL 16 for relational marketplace data and Stripe Checkout plus Connect Express for payments and producer payouts. This revision hardens two critical checkout security gaps: exclusive beat concurrent purchase race conditions are resolved via Prisma transactional row locking in the Stripe webhook handler, and download grants are now strictly created on checkout.session.completed with signature validation, preventing premature audio file access.
target_output.architecture_overview: Server-side rendered Next.js 15 frontend served by DigitalOcean App Platform proxies beat discovery and producer storefront pages with SSR for SEO. Authenticated actions route to a Node.js Express API on a separate App Platform component. PostgreSQL 16 managed database stores beats, license_tiers, purchases, download_grants, and payout_records. Beat audio files and license PDFs live on DigitalOcean Spaces with CDN. Stripe Checkout handles payment; Stripe Connect Express manages producer payouts with 1099-K compliance. The checkout.session.completed Stripe webhook is the authoritative payment confirmation signal: it atomically acquires a row lock on the exclusive beat, creates the purchase record, generates the download_grant, and triggers Stripe Connect payout — all inside a single Prisma transaction to prevent partial state.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 15, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand for audio player state. Backend: Node.js 22, Express, Prisma ORM 5 with PostgreSQL 16, stripe-node SDK, aws-sdk v3 for Spaces. Auth: Auth0 with Next.js SDK, httpOnly cookies for tokens. Payments: Stripe Checkout, Stripe Connect Express, Stripe webhooks with signature validation. Storage: DigitalOcean Spaces with CDN enabled. Infrastructure: DigitalOcean App Platform, Managed PostgreSQL. Observability: Sentry, Grafana Cloud. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, doctl CLI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Beat Discovery: publicly browsable grid filtered by genre, BPM, key, price with inline 30-second streaming preview. Producer Storefront: SEO-optimized public profile with active beat catalog. License Tier Selection: non-exclusive (unlimited sales), exclusive (single buyer, beats.status flips to sold), unlimited (unlimited sales, highest tier). Stripe Checkout: redirect to hosted Stripe checkout for selected tier. Post-Purchase: download page generated after confirmed checkout.session.completed webhook with single-use signed Spaces URL and license PDF link. Producer Dashboard: per-beat earnings, total revenue, Stripe Connect payout initiation with 1099-K status. Buyer Purchase History: license tier, beat title, download re-issue within 30-day window.
target_output.system_components: DigitalOcean App Platform Next.js Component (SSR, static assets, API proxy). DigitalOcean App Platform Express API Component (beats, checkout, webhooks, downloads, producers). DigitalOcean Managed PostgreSQL 16 (relational marketplace data with Prisma migrations). DigitalOcean Spaces with CDN (beat audio files, preview clips, license PDFs). Stripe Checkout (hosted payment page, PCI-DSS scope). Stripe Connect Express (producer payouts, 1099-K generation). Auth0 Tenant (email and Google OAuth, buyer and producer roles). Sentry (error tracking for API and frontend). Grafana Cloud (beat download rate, checkout conversion, API p95 latency metrics).
target_output.workflows: Beat Purchase (Non-Exclusive): buyer selects non-exclusive tier, POST /checkout/sessions creates Stripe Checkout session with beat_id and tier_id in metadata, buyer redirected to Stripe-hosted page, Stripe fires checkout.session.completed webhook, Express handler validates Stripe-Signature header with stripe.webhooks.constructEvent, creates purchase record and download_grant in Prisma transaction, returns success redirect with grant_id. Buyer visits download page, GET /downloads/{grant_id} checks grant exists and used_at is null, sets used_at, returns 1-hour signed Spaces URL. Exclusive Beat Purchase: identical flow except webhook handler opens Prisma $transaction with $queryRaw SELECT FOR UPDATE on beats row, checks status, if available sets status to sold and creates purchase and grant atomically; if status is sold, creates Stripe refund via stripe.refunds.create and returns 409 conflict to buyer with notification email. Stripe signature replay prevention: constructEvent raises error on invalid signature; 400 returned to Stripe triggering no retry. Producer Payout: producer dashboard shows accrued balance from completed purchases minus Stripe Connect fees; producer clicks initiate payout, Express calls stripe.transfers.create to Connect account, payout_record created with stripe_transfer_id.
target_output.data_model: beats (id UUID PK, producer_id FK, title TEXT, genre TEXT, bpm INT, musical_key TEXT, mood_tags TEXT[], audio_spaces_key TEXT, preview_spaces_key TEXT, status ENUM available/sold/unlisted, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). license_tiers (id UUID PK, beat_id FK, tier_type ENUM non_exclusive/exclusive/unlimited, price_cents INT, license_pdf_spaces_key TEXT, max_sales INT NULLABLE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). purchases (id UUID PK, buyer_id FK, beat_id FK, license_tier_id FK, stripe_session_id TEXT UNIQUE, amount_paid_cents INT, platform_fee_cents INT, stripe_connect_transfer_id TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). download_grants (id UUID PK, purchase_id FK, buyer_id FK, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, used_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). payout_records (id UUID PK, producer_id FK, stripe_transfer_id TEXT UNIQUE, amount_cents INT, status ENUM pending/completed/failed, period_start DATE, period_end DATE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). producer_profiles (id UUID PK, user_id FK UNIQUE, display_name TEXT, bio TEXT, stripe_connect_account_id TEXT, payout_enabled BOOL, onboarded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). users (id UUID PK, auth0_sub TEXT UNIQUE, email TEXT, role ENUM buyer/producer, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE).
target_output.api_design: GET /beats — paginated beat discovery with filters: genre, bpm_min, bpm_max, key, max_price_cents, sort_by. GET /beats/{id} — beat detail with license tier pricing. GET /beats/{id}/preview — 1-hour signed Spaces URL for 30-second preview clip. POST /beats — producer uploads beat metadata; audio upload to Spaces via pre-signed PUT URL returned in response. POST /checkout/sessions — creates Stripe Checkout session for beat_id and license_tier_id; returns session_url for redirect. POST /webhooks/stripe — raw body route, validates Stripe-Signature, handles checkout.session.completed with transactional beat lock and grant creation. GET /downloads/{grant_id} — validates grant ownership via JWT, checks used_at null, sets used_at, returns signed Spaces URL. GET /producers/{id}/earnings — producer earnings summary with per-beat breakdown. POST /producers/payouts — initiates Stripe Connect transfer for producer balance.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 PKCE with email and Google OAuth; buyer and producer roles set at registration; access tokens in httpOnly cookies via Auth0 Next.js SDK. Beat ownership enforced in Express middleware: beats.producer_id must match JWT sub for write operations. Stripe webhook signature validation: all POST /webhooks/stripe requests validated with stripe.webhooks.constructEvent using STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET; invalid signatures return 400 and are not processed. Exclusive beat transactional lock: SELECT FOR UPDATE on beats row inside Prisma $transaction in webhook handler; concurrent webhook for same session returns no-op after lock acquired. Download grant single-use: used_at set atomically on first access; subsequent requests return 410 Gone. Signed Spaces URLs with 1-hour expiry generated only after confirmed purchase; no pre-signed URLs on checkout session creation. GDPR: buyer erasure nulls email and personal fields in users table, deletes Auth0 account; purchases and payout_records retained 7 years with buyer_id replaced by anonymized hash. PCI-DSS scope delegated entirely to Stripe-hosted checkout; Express never touches raw card data. CSRF protection via Auth0 Next.js SDK state parameter.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: DigitalOcean App Platform with two components: next-frontend (Next.js 15 build, 1 Basic dyno, scales to Professional on launch) and express-api (Node.js, 1 Basic dyno, scales to Professional). DigitalOcean Managed PostgreSQL Basic tier upgraded to Standard on launch. Spaces CDN serves preview clips and license PDFs with cache-control headers. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: ESLint, TypeScript strict check, Jest unit tests for checkout and download modules with 75% coverage gate, Prisma migrate dry-run, Docker build, deploy via doctl app update. Environment promotion: feature branches to dev App Platform app, PRs to staging, main to production with manual approval. Database migrations run as Prisma migrate deploy pre-deploy command. Stripe webhook endpoint registered per environment with separate signing secrets.
target_output.observability: Sentry for unhandled Express errors and Next.js client errors with release tagging per deploy. Grafana Cloud with Node.js Prometheus exporter: custom metrics beat_purchase_total by tier_type, download_grant_created_total, exclusive_beat_refund_total (alert if > 0 per hour), stripe_webhook_validation_failure_total. API p95 latency tracked per route. Alert channels: Sentry Slack for error spikes, Grafana alert for checkout conversion drop below 3% or download error rate above 1%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $340/month: App Platform two Basic dynos at $24 each, Managed PostgreSQL Basic at $15, Spaces at $5 plus CDN bandwidth, Stripe fees at approximately 2.9% plus $0.30 per transaction absorbed by platform fee on purchases. Scales to $520/month on Professional dynos for full launch. DigitalOcean App Platform vertical scaling via dyno size upgrade; no horizontal auto-scaling at this tier. Redis caching for beat discovery results can be added pre-launch if discovery query latency degrades.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP 8 weeks: beat upload, 3 license tier pricing, 30-second preview generation, Stripe Checkout with checkout.session.completed webhook and transactional exclusive beat lock, download_grant single-use enforcement, producer storefront, buyer purchase history, Stripe Connect Express onboarding, producer earnings dashboard, Auth0 auth, GDPR erasure endpoint, CI/CD pipeline, Sentry and Grafana observability. Phase 2 4 weeks: beat discovery filters and BPM/key search, producer analytics with conversion funnel, license PDF templating per tier, download re-issue within 30-day window, email notifications via SendGrid for purchases and payouts. Phase 3 4 weeks: stem pack licensing with multiple audio files per purchase, custom commission request flow, producer collaboration revenue splits, mobile-responsive polish, SEO metadata and Open Graph for producer storefronts.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode throughout Next.js and Express. Prisma migrations committed to git and run via migrate deploy in CI. Stripe webhook handler is a dedicated Express route using express.raw middleware to preserve body buffer for signature validation — never use express.json on this route. Exclusive beat lock tested with a Jest integration test that fires two concurrent checkout.session.completed requests for the same exclusive beat and asserts exactly one purchase and one refund. ESLint with no-floating-promises rule enforced to catch unawaited Prisma calls. Feature flags via environment variables for beta-only features. PRs touching checkout or download modules require review from a second engineer.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: DigitalOcean App Platform Basic dyno cold start latency on low-traffic periods. Mitigation: upgrade to Professional dyno with always-on before full launch; implement keep-alive health check ping from Grafana. Risk: Stripe Connect Express 1099-K threshold changes requiring producer identity verification. Mitigation: Stripe Connect onboarding flow is self-service; monitor Stripe compliance emails. Risk: exclusive beat lock contention under load if many buyers attempt the same popular beat simultaneously. Mitigation: SELECT FOR UPDATE with SKIP LOCKED pattern; second buyer redirected to non-exclusive tier with UX explanation. Tradeoff: DigitalOcean over AWS simplifies ops and reduces cost for beta but lacks CloudFront-class CDN for global audio delivery; planned migration to AWS for full launch.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Stripe Connect Express over Standard for simpler producer onboarding and Stripe-managed 1099-K generation. Spaces CDN selected over S3 for cost consistency within DigitalOcean stack. Single-use download_grants chosen over time-limited multi-use to prevent unauthorized redistribution sharing. Prisma $transaction with $queryRaw SELECT FOR UPDATE chosen over application-level locking to handle exclusive beat race condition at the database level.

metadata.notes: Round 2 beat marketplace revision; closes ISS-001 exclusive beat race condition with Prisma SELECT FOR UPDATE and ISS-002 premature download grant with strict checkout.session.completed webhook gating.

project_goal.value: Managed API service exposing ML-powered stem separation, allowing music production apps to submit audio tracks and receive separated vocal, drum, bass, and instrument stems asynchronously
project_goal.rationale: Core product: commoditize GPU-backed stem separation behind a clean REST API for partner apps

target_users.value: Music production app developers and audio software partners integrating stem separation into their products
target_users.rationale: B2B partner-facing API targeting DAW plugin and mobile music app integrators

project_class.rationale: Pure backend API with async job processing; no frontend required

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, public_api, batch_jobs, integrations
capabilities.rationale: GPU batch processing with API key auth and webhook callback integration for partners

complexity_level.rationale: GPU worker orchestration, async job queue, model versioning, and partner API management

risk_level.rationale: Internal audio data processed transiently; partner API key compromise is main risk vector

data_sensitivity.rationale: Audio files processed ephemerally; no personal data retained after job completion

external_exposure.rationale: B2B API consumed by vetted partner applications; not open to anonymous public traffic

access_model.value: API key authentication with per-partner rate limits and usage quotas
access_model.rationale: Partner keys issued via developer portal; scoped to separation model access and job submission

mvp_scope.rationale: Core pipeline needed before A/B routing and webhook callbacks

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all API traffic, API key rotation on compromise, audio files purged from S3 after 48h, no persistent PHI
security_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing API with transient audio data; key rotation and data purge are primary controls

frontend_stack.value: None - API service only; partner developer portal is out of scope
frontend_stack.rationale: api_service project class; no UI component

backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI provides async request handling and OpenAPI spec generation; Python ecosystem for ML model integration

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for job state, Redis 7 for queue and job locks, S3 for audio input and stem output storage
data_platform.rationale: Postgres for durable job tracking, Redis for fast queue operations, S3 for large binary audio files

hosting_target.rationale: Fargate for API containers; G4dn GPU instances for separation workers via ECS task definitions

privacy_retention_policy.value: Audio inputs purged from S3 after 48 hours; stem outputs expire via signed URL TTL (24h); job metadata retained 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Transient data model minimizes partner audio exposure window; 90-day job log for billing reconciliation

execution_preference.rationale: Model updates require safe rollout to avoid degrading partner stem quality

project.goal: Async stem separation API with GPU workers and partner webhook callbacks
project.target_users: Music production app partners

backend.framework: FastAPI with Celery for async workers

security.auth: Auth0 machine-to-machine for API key issuance
security.data_handling: S3 server-side encryption, 48h audio purge lifecycle

devops.hosting: ECS Fargate plus G4dn GPU workers

reasoner_reviews.product: The core partner value proposition is reliability and stem quality consistency across model versions. A/B model routing with configurable rollout percentages lets the team promote model v2 confidently. Webhook callbacks reduce partner polling overhead for high-volume integrations.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The bottleneck is GPU worker availability. Pre-warming a pool of G4dn workers reduces cold start impact but increases idle cost. A hybrid approach using a minimum warm pool of 2 workers with auto-scaling handles burst load. Redis-backed Celery queues with priority lanes ensure enterprise partners get faster dispatch.
reasoner_reviews.security: API key scoping to specific model versions prevents partners from accessing unreleased models. S3 presigned URLs with 24h TTL limit stem download exposure. Audio inputs must be scanned for malformed files before GPU submission to prevent model crashes.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 10-minute SLA for 5-minute tracks requires a 2x real-time processing target. Current Demucs v4 on G4dn.xlarge achieves 3-4x real-time, giving comfortable headroom. Worker scaling policy needs to account for concurrent multi-track submissions from high-volume partners.

backend.service_design: FastAPI app with separation_jobs router, api_keys auth middleware, and model_registry service. Celery workers registered in separate ECS task definitions targeting G4dn GPU instances
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/jobs (submit audio, returns job_id), GET /v1/jobs/{job_id} (poll status), GET /v1/jobs/{job_id}/stems (stem download URLs), POST /v1/webhooks (register callback URL), GET /v1/models (list available model versions)
backend.background_jobs: Celery beat schedules S3 audio purge job every 6h. GPU worker pulls from high and standard priority queues. Job state machine: queued to processing to completed or failed
backend.failure_handling: Dead letter queue for jobs failing after 3 retries. GPU worker crash triggers job requeue with incremented attempt counter. Stale jobs (processing more than 15 min) reaped by watchdog cron.
backend.backend_risks: GPU instance availability in eu-west-1 during peaks; mitigated by multi-AZ GPU capacity reservation and fallback to on-demand spot

security.auth_design: Auth0 machine-to-machine clients issue API keys scoped to job:submit, job:read, stem:download permissions
security.secrets_management: GPU worker PyTorch model weights stored in S3 with KMS encryption; loaded into worker memory on startup; never written to ECS task filesystem
security.abuse_prevention: Per-partner rate limit: 100 job submissions per hour; file size cap 500MB; audio duration cap 60 minutes; malformed audio rejection before GPU queue entry
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All job submissions logged with partner_id, source_ip, file_size_bytes, model_version to Datadog; stem download events logged with signed URL expiry timestamp

data.entities: separation_jobs (id UUID PK, partner_id FK, status ENUM queued/processing/completed/failed, model_version VARCHAR, audio_s3_key, submitted_at, completed_at, attempt_count INT), stem_outputs (id UUID PK, job_id FK, stem_type ENUM vocals/drums/bass/other, s3_key, file_size_bytes, created_at), api_keys (id UUID PK, partner_id FK, key_hash VARCHAR, permissions TEXT[], created_at, revoked_at), model_versions (version VARCHAR PK, s3_weights_ecr_tag TEXT, is_active BOOL, promoted_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ for job state durability. S3 buckets: audio-inputs (SSE-KMS, 48h lifecycle purge), stem-outputs (SSE-KMS, 7-day lifecycle). Redis 7 ElastiCache for Celery broker and job lock keys
data.consistency_notes: Job status transitions guarded by Redis SETNX locks to prevent duplicate GPU processing of the same job_id under concurrent worker polling

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster: api-service task (2 vCPU, 4GB), celery-worker-gpu task (G4dn.xlarge, 16GB GPU). ALB routes HTTPS to api-service. GPU workers pull from SQS-backed Celery broker. Auto-scaling on queue depth: scale out when depth greater than 20, scale in after 5 min idle
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint (ruff), test (pytest 85% coverage gate), build Docker images for api-service and worker, push to ECR, ECS rolling deploy for api-service, blue/green for GPU workers to avoid in-flight job interruption
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM traces across FastAPI and Celery. Custom metrics: separation_job_queue_depth, gpu_worker_utilization_pct, job_p95_processing_time_seconds, stem_s3_upload_bytes. PagerDuty alert on queue_depth greater than 50 or p95_processing_time greater than 600s

ISS-001.title: GPU worker cold start exceeds 90-second SLA during scale-out events
ISS-001.detail: ECS task definition pulls the 4GB Demucs model from S3 on every cold start, adding 60-90s to worker readiness. During burst load, new workers cannot accept jobs within SLA.

history.note: Identified in round 1 architecture review; no mitigation implemented in prior plan

ISS-002.title: No webhook retry mechanism for failed partner callback deliveries
ISS-002.detail: The plan describes webhook callbacks but does not specify retry policy, dead letter handling, or partner notification when callbacks fail repeatedly.

history.note: Flagged in round 1 audit; webhook reliability not addressed

focus_issues.title: GPU worker cold start exceeds 90-second SLA during scale-out events
focus_issues.detail: ECS task definition pulls the 4GB Demucs model from S3 on every cold start, adding 60-90s to worker readiness.

focus_issues.title: No webhook retry mechanism for failed partner callback deliveries
focus_issues.detail: The plan describes webhook callbacks but does not specify retry policy, dead letter handling, or partner notification when callbacks fail repeatedly.

latest_recommendations: Pre-bake the Demucs model weights into the GPU worker Docker image to eliminate S3 download on cold start
Add explicit webhook retry policy with exponential backoff and dead letter queue for undeliverable callbacks

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues from round 1 require targeted fixes before convergence

previous_audits.summary: Solid async job architecture with well-defined GPU worker orchestration, but cold start latency risk and incomplete webhook reliability design leave partner SLAs at risk.
strengths: Clear job state machine (queued/processing/completed/failed) with Redis lock guards
S3 lifecycle purge policy well-defined for transient audio data
Auth0 M2M API key scoping to job permissions is appropriate for partner model
concerns: GPU worker cold start pulls 4GB model from S3, risking 90-second SLA breach during scale-out
Webhook delivery has no retry policy, dead letter queue, or partner failure notification
blocking_issues: ISS-001: GPU cold start SLA breach risk needs architectural resolution before production partner onboarding
recommendations: Bake Demucs model weights into GPU worker Docker image; accept larger image size in exchange for deterministic cold start under 30 seconds
Implement webhook delivery with exponential backoff retry (3 attempts at 1/5/15 min intervals), dead letter queue for undeliverable events, and partner email notification after final failure

issue_updates.title: GPU worker cold start exceeds 90-second SLA
issue_updates.detail: S3 model download on every cold start is the root cause; image pre-baking eliminates this

issue_updates.title: No webhook retry mechanism
issue_updates.detail: Webhook section describes callback URL registration but omits retry semantics

previous_plan.title: Stem Separation API - Round 1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Async stem separation service with FastAPI, Celery GPU workers on ECS G4dn, PostgreSQL job state, and Redis queue. Partners submit audio via REST, poll for completion, and download stems via signed S3 URLs.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: FastAPI API service on ECS Fargate fronted by ALB. Celery workers on G4dn.xlarge GPU instances load Demucs v4 model from S3 on startup. Jobs queued in Redis, status tracked in PostgreSQL, stems written to S3 output bucket.
previous_plan.system_components: ALB, ECS Fargate (API), ECS G4dn (Celery GPU worker), PostgreSQL RDS, Redis ElastiCache, S3 (audio-inputs, stem-outputs), Auth0 M2M
previous_plan.workflows: Partner submits POST /v1/jobs with presigned audio URL. Celery worker dequeues job, loads Demucs model from S3, processes audio, writes stems to S3, updates job status to completed. Partner polls GET /v1/jobs/{id} or receives webhook.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision addressing two focused issues: GPU cold start SLA breach (ISS-001) resolved by baking Demucs model weights into the worker Docker image, and webhook delivery reliability (ISS-002) resolved by adding exponential backoff retry with dead letter handling. The rest of the architecture from round 1 is sound and preserved.

fix_report.action_taken: Demucs v4 model weights (4.2GB) are now baked into the GPU worker Docker image during CI build via a multi-stage Dockerfile that downloads weights from S3 to the image layer. Cold start is now bounded by ECS task launch time (~25-35s on G4dn.xlarge) with no runtime S3 download. Image pushed to ECR and cached; model weight updates trigger a new CI build and ECR push.
fix_report.expected_outcome: GPU worker cold start consistently under 40 seconds, satisfying the 90-second SLA with 50-second buffer. Docker image size increases to ~6GB but ECR layer caching keeps pull time under 20 seconds for subsequent scaling events.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit webhook delivery design: after job completion, a dedicated Celery webhook-delivery worker retries POST to partner callback URL with exponential backoff (attempt 1 immediate, attempt 2 after 60s, attempt 3 after 300s). After 3 failures, event is written to a webhook_dead_letters table and the partner is notified via SES. Partners can replay dead-letter events via GET /v1/webhooks/dead-letters.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Partner callback failures are surfaced and recoverable. Webhook delivery reliability metric added to Datadog; alert fires when dead letter rate exceeds 5% of completed jobs.

target_output.title: Stem Separation API - GPU Cold Start and Webhook Reliability Fixes
target_output.executive_summary: A managed async stem separation API built on FastAPI and Celery GPU workers on AWS ECS G4dn instances. This round addresses GPU cold start SLA risk by pre-baking Demucs model weights into the worker image, and adds a reliable webhook delivery system with exponential backoff retries and dead letter handling for partner callback failures.
target_output.architecture_overview: FastAPI API service on ECS Fargate behind an ALB handles partner job submission and status queries. Celery workers run on ECS G4dn.xlarge GPU task definitions with the Demucs v4 model (4.2GB) pre-loaded in the Docker image, eliminating runtime S3 model downloads and achieving deterministic cold starts under 40 seconds. Job state tracked in PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ. Redis 7 on ElastiCache serves as Celery broker with priority queues for enterprise vs standard partners. Audio inputs stored in S3 with SSE-KMS and 48h lifecycle purge; stem outputs written to S3 with 24h presigned URL TTL. A dedicated Celery webhook-delivery worker handles partner callback retries with exponential backoff and writes failed deliveries to a dead letter table with partner email notification via SES.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Celery 5.3, PyTorch 2.3, Demucs v4. Databases: PostgreSQL 16 (RDS Multi-AZ), Redis 7 (ElastiCache). Storage: S3 with SSE-KMS lifecycle policies. Auth: Auth0 M2M API key issuance. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate (API), ECS G4dn.xlarge (GPU workers), ALB, ECR. Notifications: SES for dead letter partner alerts. CI: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Job Submission: POST /v1/jobs accepts partner audio file reference with model_version selection; returns job_id and estimated_completion_time. Stem Delivery: GET /v1/jobs/{job_id}/stems returns presigned S3 download URLs for vocals, drums, bass, and other stems with 24h TTL. Webhook Callbacks: partners register callback URL via POST /v1/webhooks; delivery includes job_id, status, stem_urls, and model_version. A/B Model Routing: operators configure routing_pct per model_version; jobs assigned deterministically by job_id hash. Dead Letter Recovery: GET /v1/webhooks/dead-letters returns undeliverable events; replay endpoint triggers re-delivery.
target_output.system_components: ALB (HTTPS, partner traffic routing). ECS Fargate api-service (2 vCPU, 4GB, 2-4 tasks). ECS G4dn.xlarge celery-worker-gpu (Demucs pre-baked image, 2-8 tasks auto-scaling on queue depth). ECS Fargate celery-worker-webhook (1 vCPU, 2GB, handles callback retry logic). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ. Redis 7 ElastiCache (Celery broker, job locks). S3 audio-inputs (48h lifecycle). S3 stem-outputs (7-day lifecycle). Auth0 M2M tenant. SES dead letter notifications. Datadog APM.
target_output.workflows: Stem Separation Job: Partner POST /v1/jobs with source_audio_url and model_version. API validates API key, checks partner rate limit in Redis, creates separation_jobs row (status=queued), enqueues task to high or standard priority Celery queue. GPU worker dequeues, acquires Redis SETNX job lock, loads pre-baked Demucs model from image memory, processes audio, writes vocals/drums/bass/other WAV files to S3 stem-outputs, updates job status to completed. Webhook Failure Recovery: Callback POST fails; delivery worker retries at T+60s and T+300s. After third failure, writes to webhook_dead_letters table, sends SES email to partner technical contact with dead letter replay endpoint URL.
target_output.data_model: separation_jobs (id UUID PK, partner_id UUID FK, status ENUM queued/processing/completed/failed, model_version VARCHAR, source_audio_s3_key TEXT, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, attempt_count SMALLINT DEFAULT 0). stem_outputs (id UUID PK, job_id UUID FK, stem_type ENUM vocals/drums/bass/other, s3_key TEXT, file_size_bytes BIGINT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). api_keys (id UUID PK, partner_id UUID FK, key_hash CHAR(64), permissions TEXT[], created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ). model_versions (version VARCHAR PK, routing_pct NUMERIC, is_active BOOL, promoted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). webhook_dead_letters (id UUID PK, job_id UUID FK, partner_id UUID FK, callback_url TEXT, last_attempt_at TIMESTAMPTZ, attempt_count SMALLINT, error_detail TEXT).
target_output.api_design: RESTful OpenAPI 3.0. POST /v1/jobs (submit separation job, returns job_id and estimated_seconds). GET /v1/jobs/{job_id} (poll status and completed_at). GET /v1/jobs/{job_id}/stems (returns vocals_url, drums_url, bass_url, other_url, and expires_at). POST /v1/webhooks (register callback URL). GET /v1/webhooks/dead-letters (paginated dead letter events). POST /v1/webhooks/dead-letters/{id}/replay (trigger re-delivery). GET /v1/models (list model versions with routing_pct). Auth: Bearer API key in Authorization header validated against api_keys.key_hash.
target_output.security_and_compliance: TLS 1.3 via ALB for all partner API traffic. Auth0 M2M issues API keys scoped to job:submit, job:read, stem:download. API key hashed (SHA-256) before storage; raw key shown only at issuance. Per-partner rate limit of 100 jobs/hour via Redis sliding window. Audio files scanned by Lambda pre-processor for malformed headers before GPU queue admission. S3 audio-inputs: SSE-KMS encryption, 48h lifecycle delete, no public access. Partner DPA covers GDPR transient processing obligations. Dead letter partner notifications via SES maintain transparency on callback failures.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline: ruff lint, pytest 85% coverage gate, docker build for api-service and celery-worker-gpu with Demucs weights COPY in Dockerfile, push to ECR, ECS rolling update for api-service, blue/green ECS deployment for GPU workers to prevent in-flight job interruption. GPU worker image build (~8 min, 4.2GB weight layer) cached in ECR; only rebuilds on model version bump. Environments: dev (single G4dn spot), staging (multi-AZ), production (multi-AZ, 2 warm GPU tasks baseline, scale to 8 on queue depth greater than 20). Rollback via ECS service previous task definition revision. Secrets managed via AWS Secrets Manager injected into ECS task environment.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces FastAPI requests and Celery task execution. Custom metrics: separation_job_queue_depth (alert greater than 50), gpu_worker_utilization_pct (alert greater than 85), job_p95_processing_time_seconds (alert greater than 600), webhook_dead_letter_rate_pct (alert greater than 5), api_key_auth_failure_rate (alert greater than 10/min). Datadog dashboard shows real-time queue depth, active GPU workers, job completion rate, and partner stem download counts. PagerDuty integration for production alerts.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: G4dn.xlarge on-demand ~$0.526/hour. 2 baseline warm workers: ~$760/month. Scale to 8 workers covers 4x burst. ECS Fargate API: ~$40/month. RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium: ~$80/month. ElastiCache cache.t3.micro: ~$25/month. S3 100GB audio/stem turnover: ~$30/month. Estimated baseline total ~$935/month. GPU spot instances for dev/staging reduce non-production cost by 70%.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Core Pipeline (4 weeks): FastAPI job submission and status endpoints, Celery GPU worker with pre-baked Demucs image, PostgreSQL job state, Redis queue, S3 storage, Auth0 API key auth, CI/CD pipeline. Phase 2 - Reliability (2 weeks): Webhook callback system with exponential backoff retries, dead letter table, SES partner notifications, dead letter replay endpoint. Phase 3 - A/B Routing and Scale (2 weeks): model_versions table, job routing by routing_pct hash, Datadog dashboard, auto-scaling policies, partner usage quota enforcement.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with ruff for linting. Type annotations on all FastAPI route handlers and Celery task signatures. Job state transitions only through JobStateService to enforce Redis lock semantics. GPU worker tested with mocked PyTorch inference in unit tests; integration tests use real Demucs model on G4dn dev instance. 85% pytest coverage gate in CI. No model weights committed to git - always built from ECR image layer.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: 6GB GPU worker Docker image increases ECR storage cost and initial pull time on scale-out. Mitigation: ECR layer caching means only changed layers re-pull; model weight layer is pinned and rarely changes. Risk: Demucs v4 GPU memory near G4dn.xlarge VRAM limit (16GB) under concurrent loads. Mitigation: one job per worker task enforces isolation. Tradeoff: pre-baked model image trades larger image size for deterministic cold start - acceptable for SLA compliance. Tradeoff: exponential backoff webhook retry improves delivery reliability at the cost of reduced callback immediacy in failure scenarios.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Resolved ISS-001: Demucs model weights baked into Docker image, eliminating S3 cold start download and satisfying 90-second SLA. Resolved ISS-002: Webhook delivery with 3-attempt exponential backoff, dead letter table, and SES partner notification fully specifies callback reliability contract. Confirmed Auth0 M2M over custom key generation for audit trail and revocation management.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision fixing GPU cold start SLA (ISS-001) and webhook reliability (ISS-002) for partner-facing stem separation API.

profile.domain: DJ Automation and Mix Transition Detection

project_goal.value: Provide an API service that wraps an ML pipeline for automatic DJ mix transition point detection, analyzing audio energy curves and beat grids to suggest optimal cue points for DJ automation software
project_goal.rationale: DJ automation tools need algorithmic cue point suggestions to replace manual track analysis

target_users.value: DJ automation software vendors and hardware controllers integrating transition detection into their products
target_users.rationale: B2B partner API consumed by DJ software products, not end-user consumers

project_class.rationale: Pure backend API service; no frontend required; consumed by partner DJ software products

capabilities.rationale: Backend ML processing, partner authentication, result storage, and public API surface

complexity_level.rationale: ML pipeline with audio processing, Redis caching, and API key auth is moderately complex

risk_level.rationale: No personal or financial data; partner API keys are the only sensitive credential type

data_sensitivity.rationale: Audio files submitted are DJ tracks with no personal data; analysis results are acoustic metadata

external_exposure.rationale: API exposed to partner DJ software integrations only; no public consumer access

access_model.value: API key authentication with per-partner rate limits; no end-user auth required
access_model.rationale: B2B API pattern with partner-scoped keys and usage quotas

feature_scope.value: Audio file analysis endpoint, BPM and beat grid extraction, energy curve computation, transition cue scoring, ranked cue suggestion response, Redis result caching by audio hash, API key management
feature_scope.rationale: Full transition detection feature set from audio submission to cue ranking

mvp_scope.value: Audio upload endpoint, BPM and energy extraction, top-5 transition cue suggestions with confidence scores, Redis cache by file hash, API key auth
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for two DJ software partner integrations

security_baseline.value: HTTPS only, API key validation on every request, rate limiting per partner key, no audio files persisted after analysis
security_baseline.rationale: Low risk service; API key protection and no data retention are sufficient controls

frontend_stack.rationale: No frontend required; partner software consumes the REST API directly

backend_stack.rationale: Python for ML library compatibility (Librosa, Essentia); FastAPI for async handling and OpenAPI generation

data_platform.value: MongoDB 7 for analysis result persistence; Redis 7 for result caching by audio hash
data_platform.rationale: MongoDB document model fits variable-length beat grid and cue suggestion arrays; Redis for cache-aside pattern

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Run for API service; Memorystore Redis for caching
hosting_target.rationale: Cloud Run scales to zero for cost efficiency on variable partner usage patterns

privacy_retention_policy.value: Audio files deleted immediately after analysis; analysis results retained 30 days then purged; no personal data collected
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No retention obligations given no personal data; 30-day retention window for partner result retrieval

future_scope.value: Streaming audio analysis via chunked upload, genre-adaptive cue scoring models, batch analysis job API
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP improvements based on partner feedback

constraints.value: Analysis must complete within 10 seconds for tracks under 10 minutes; GCP Cloud Run budget under $200/month
constraints.rationale: Partner DJ software requires near-realtime analysis results; startup cost ceiling

observability_baseline.value: Google Cloud Monitoring for Cloud Run metrics; structured JSON logs; alerting on error rate above 2%
observability_baseline.rationale: GCP-native monitoring fits Cloud Run deployment

execution_preference.value: Continuous deployment on merge to main; no staged environments for MVP
execution_preference.rationale: Small team with two partner integrations; staging environment added post-MVP

project.goal: ML-powered DJ transition cue detection API for partner DJ automation software
project.target_users: DJ software vendors and hardware controller manufacturers
project.timeline: MVP in 6 weeks for two partner integrations

security.auth: API key header validation
security.data: No audio file persistence after analysis

data.cache: Redis 7 via Memorystore for hash-based result caching

open_questions.beat_detection_library: Librosa vs Essentia for beat grid accuracy to be benchmarked in week 1

reasoner_reviews.product: The core value is algorithmic transition cue scoring that saves DJs from manual beatmatching analysis. Confidence scores on each cue suggestion let partner software surface only high-confidence candidates. The cache-by-audio-hash pattern is essential for cost control since partner software may submit the same popular track repeatedly.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Cloud Run with scale-to-zero is appropriate for variable partner request volume. MongoDB's document model fits beat_grids and transition_cues arrays without schema rigidity. Redis cache-aside by SHA256 audio hash is the right approach — compute analysis once and serve from cache on repeat submissions. The 10-second latency SLA needs benchmarking against Librosa BPM detection on a 10-minute track on Cloud Run's CPU tier.
reasoner_reviews.security: API key validation must include rate limiting per partner to prevent abuse. Audio files should be processed in memory and never written to disk or persisted to storage. The hash-based cache key must use SHA256 of the audio content, not the filename, to correctly identify duplicate submissions.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 10-second analysis on Cloud Run requires profiling. Librosa beat tracking on a 10-minute 44.1kHz WAV file can take 15-20 seconds on a single CPU core. Recommend benchmarking and considering downsampling to 22050Hz for analysis to halve compute time.

backend.service_design: Single FastAPI application with three modules: analysis (Librosa BPM extraction, energy curve computation, cue scoring), cache (Redis hash lookup and result storage), and auth (API key validation middleware). No worker queue for MVP given 10-second synchronous response target.
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/analyze — multipart audio file upload, API key in header, returns analysis_id and transition_cues array with confidence scores. GET /v1/analyses/{analysis_id} — retrieve stored result by ID. GET /v1/status — health check returning service version and cache hit rate.
backend.failure_handling: If Librosa analysis exceeds 12 seconds, return 504 with retry guidance. Redis unavailability falls back to full analysis without caching. MongoDB write failure logs error and returns analysis result without persistence.

security.auth_design: API key passed in X-API-Key header. Keys stored as SHA256 hashes in MongoDB api_keys collection with partner_id, rate_limit_per_minute, and active flag. FastAPI dependency validates key on every request.
security.authorization_model: Partner-scoped: each API key maps to a partner_id. Analysis results tagged with partner_id to scope GET /analyses/{id} to the submitting partner only.
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting via Redis sliding window counter per API key. Keys exceeding quota return 429 with Retry-After header. Oversized audio files (above 100MB) rejected at FastAPI upload validation before processing.

data.entities: analysis_requests (id UUID, partner_id TEXT, audio_sha256 TEXT, status ENUM processing/complete/failed, submitted_at DATETIME), track_analyses (id UUID, analysis_request_id REF, bpm FLOAT, beat_grid_array ARRAY, energy_curve_array ARRAY, transition_cues ARRAY of {position_seconds FLOAT, confidence FLOAT, cue_type TEXT}, model_version TEXT, processing_time_ms INT, created_at DATETIME)
data.storage_design: MongoDB 7 Atlas-compatible for analysis result documents. Redis Memorystore for cache-aside: key is audio_sha256, value is serialized track_analysis JSON, TTL 30 days.
data.data_risks: Cache poisoning if SHA256 collision occurs (negligible at this scale). MongoDB document size limits if beat_grid_array exceeds 16MB for very long tracks at high resolution; mitigate by downsampling beat grid to 1-second resolution for storage.

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Run service with minimum 0 instances (scale-to-zero), maximum 10 instances, 2GB memory and 2 CPU for Librosa processing. Memorystore Redis Basic tier. MongoDB Atlas free tier for MVP scaled to M10 on launch.
