devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build trigger on main branch push: pytest unit tests for beat detection and cue scoring modules, Docker build, push to Artifact Registry, deploy to Cloud Run with traffic migration. No staging environment for MVP.
devops.devops_risks: Cloud Run cold start adds 2-3 seconds to first request after scale-to-zero. Mitigation: set minimum instances to 1 during partner integration testing windows; scale-to-zero during off-hours only.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a moderate-complexity API service for DJ transition cue detection where the main architectural concern is latency: Librosa beat tracking on a 10-minute track can exceed the 10-second SLA on a single Cloud Run CPU instance. The cache-by-audio-hash pattern is the primary cost and latency mitigation. MongoDB's document model is a reasonable fit for variable-length beat grids and cue arrays. This is a low-risk, low-data-sensitivity service; the plan is intentionally moderate in depth given plan_quality weak.
target_output.title: DJ Mix Transition Point Detection API
target_output.executive_summary: A partner-facing REST API that analyzes DJ tracks using Librosa-based BPM detection, beat grid extraction, and energy curve computation to return ranked transition cue suggestions with confidence scores. Built on Python FastAPI deployed to GCP Cloud Run with MongoDB 7 for result persistence and Redis Memorystore for SHA256 hash-based result caching to avoid reprocessing popular tracks submitted by multiple partner integrations.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Cloud Run service running a Python FastAPI application exposing a REST API for audio submission and transition cue retrieval. On each analysis request, the service validates the API key against MongoDB, computes the SHA256 hash of the uploaded audio, checks Redis cache for a prior result, and if not cached runs the Librosa ML pipeline for BPM detection, beat grid extraction, and energy curve segmentation to score and rank transition cue candidates. Results are written to MongoDB and cached in Redis with 30-day TTL. Partner software retrieves analysis results via the analysis_id. No audio files are persisted after processing.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Librosa for beat grid and energy analysis, NumPy for energy curve computation. Database: MongoDB 7 Atlas for analysis result documents. Cache: Redis 7 via GCP Memorystore. Infrastructure: GCP Cloud Run, Artifact Registry, Cloud Build. Auth: API key header validation with SHA256-hashed key storage. Observability: Google Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging structured JSON.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Audio Analysis: multipart POST endpoint accepts audio file, computes SHA256 hash, checks Redis cache, runs Librosa BPM and beat grid extraction if cache miss, computes energy envelope using RMS windowing, scores transition points by energy drop depth and beat alignment, returns top-5 cue suggestions with position_seconds and confidence values. Result Retrieval: GET endpoint returns stored analysis by ID scoped to submitting partner. Cache Hit Rate Monitoring: /v1/status endpoint returns cache hit rate over last 1000 requests for partner SLA reporting.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Run Service (FastAPI application, 2GB RAM, 2 CPU, scale 0 to 10 instances). Memorystore Redis Basic (result cache, SHA256 key, 30-day TTL). MongoDB Atlas M0 MVP scaled to M10 (analysis_requests and track_analyses collections). Artifact Registry (Docker image storage). Cloud Build (CI/CD pipeline). Google Cloud Monitoring (Cloud Run request count, latency p95, error rate metrics).
target_output.workflows: DJ Software Submits Track: partner app sends POST /v1/analyze with audio file in multipart body and X-API-Key header. FastAPI validates API key SHA256 against MongoDB api_keys, rejects with 401 if invalid. Service computes SHA256 of audio content. Redis checked for cached result by SHA256 key. On cache hit, returns cached track_analysis JSON directly. On cache miss, runs Librosa analysis pipeline: beat_track() for BPM and beat grid, compute RMS energy curve in 0.5-second windows, identify energy drops greater than 20% at beat boundaries as candidate transition points, score by drop depth and beat alignment regularity, rank top-5 by confidence score. Result written to MongoDB and Redis with 30-day TTL. Returns analysis_id and transition_cues array. Repeat Submission: second partner submitting same track hash returns cached result in under 100ms without Librosa reprocessing.
target_output.data_model: analysis_requests collection: {id UUID, partner_id TEXT, audio_sha256 TEXT INDEXED, audio_filename TEXT, status ENUM processing/complete/failed, submitted_at ISODate, completed_at ISODate}. track_analyses collection: {id UUID, analysis_request_id UUID REF, bpm FLOAT, beat_grid [{beat_index INT, time_seconds FLOAT}], energy_curve [{window_start_seconds FLOAT, rms_energy FLOAT}], transition_cues [{position_seconds FLOAT, confidence FLOAT, energy_drop_ratio FLOAT, cue_type ENUM phrase_boundary/energy_drop/beat_aligned}], model_version TEXT, processing_time_ms INT, created_at ISODate}. api_keys collection: {id UUID, partner_id TEXT UNIQUE INDEXED, key_hash TEXT UNIQUE, rate_limit_per_minute INT, active BOOL, created_at ISODate}.
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/analyze — multipart form with audio file, X-API-Key header required; returns {analysis_id, status, transition_cues: [{position_seconds, confidence, cue_type}], bpm, processing_time_ms, cached BOOL}. GET /v1/analyses/{analysis_id} — retrieve prior result by ID, scoped to partner by API key. GET /v1/status — returns {version, cache_hit_rate_last_1000, uptime_seconds}. All endpoints require X-API-Key header. Rate limit: configurable per partner_id, default 60 requests/minute, returns 429 with Retry-After on breach.
target_output.security_and_compliance: API key authentication: X-API-Key header validated by SHA256 hash lookup in MongoDB api_keys collection on every request via FastAPI dependency. Rate limiting per partner via Redis sliding window counter with 1-minute window. Audio files processed in-memory only; no disk writes or cloud storage of submitted audio. Analysis results do not contain personal data. HTTPS enforced by Cloud Run HTTPS endpoint. Oversized uploads above 100MB rejected at FastAPI upload validator before processing to prevent resource exhaustion.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GCP Cloud Run service configured with minimum 0 instances for cost control during off-hours, maximum 10 instances, 2GB memory and 2 vCPU per instance for Librosa processing headroom. Memorystore Redis Basic 1GB for result cache. MongoDB Atlas M0 for MVP with migration to M10 on partner launch. Cloud Build trigger on main branch push: pytest unit tests for cue scoring module, Docker build, Artifact Registry push, Cloud Run deploy with 10% traffic migration for smoke test then 100% promotion. Single production environment for MVP.
target_output.observability: Google Cloud Monitoring dashboards: Cloud Run request_count, request_latencies p50/p95/p99, container instance count, memory utilization. Cloud Logging structured JSON with fields: partner_id, audio_sha256, cache_hit BOOL, analysis_duration_ms, cue_count. Custom log-based metric for cache_hit_rate. Alert policies: error rate above 2% for 5-minute window, p95 latency above 12 seconds, Cloud Run instance count at maximum 10 for over 10 minutes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $120/month: Cloud Run at $0.00002400/vCPU-second and $0.00000250/GB-second, approximately $60 for moderate partner usage. Memorystore Redis Basic 1GB at $35/month. MongoDB Atlas M0 free for MVP. Cache hit rate drives cost reduction: popular tracks analyzed once and served from Redis. Scale ceiling: 10 Cloud Run instances handle approximately 50 concurrent analyses at 10 seconds each.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP 6 weeks: POST /v1/analyze with Librosa BPM and energy extraction, top-5 cue suggestions, Redis hash cache, API key auth, rate limiting, Cloud Run deployment, Cloud Build CI, Cloud Monitoring observability. Two partner integrations validated. Phase 2 4 weeks: GET /v1/analyses/{id} for result retrieval, batch analysis endpoint for multiple tracks per request, improved cue scoring with genre-adaptive thresholds, staging environment, MongoDB Atlas M10 upgrade. Phase 3 ongoing: streaming chunked audio analysis, custom model training on DJ-annotated transition datasets, partner usage dashboard.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python ruff for linting, pytest for unit tests on beat detection and cue scoring functions with 70% coverage gate. FastAPI dependency injection for API key validation middleware. Librosa analysis functions wrapped in timeout context manager to enforce 12-second hard limit before returning 504. Audio SHA256 computed from raw bytes content, not filename. Docker image built from python:3.12-slim with Librosa pre-installed to minimize cold start size.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Librosa beat_track exceeds 10-second SLA on long tracks at full sample rate. Mitigation: downsample audio to 22050Hz before analysis; benchmark worst-case 10-minute track before partner launch. Risk: Cloud Run cold start adds 2-3 seconds to first post-idle request. Mitigation: configure minimum 1 instance during peak partner usage windows. Tradeoff: synchronous analysis response over async job pattern simplifies partner integration but limits throughput; revisit for Phase 2 batch endpoint.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Librosa selected over Essentia for broader Python ecosystem support and simpler Cloud Run containerization. SHA256 of audio content chosen over filename for cache keying to handle identical tracks submitted under different filenames. Synchronous response chosen over async job polling for MVP simplicity given 10-second analysis target.

metadata.notes: Weak-quality DJ transition detection API; plan is functional but several sections are at minimum depth; latency risk with Librosa on Cloud Run CPU is noted but not fully mitigated.

profile.domain: Performing Rights Organization Royalty Management API

project_goal.value: Rights management API for a performing rights organization that tracks public performance licenses, calculates royalties from broadcaster performance reports, and distributes payout batches to registered rights holders
project_goal.rationale: Core mission of PRO: accurate royalty calculation and timely rights holder payouts under copyright law

target_users.value: Broadcasters (radio, streaming) submitting performance reports; rights holders checking royalty statements; PRO staff managing payouts and registrations
target_users.rationale: Three distinct actor types with different API access patterns and data needs

project_class.rationale: Backend API service consumed by broadcasters, rights holders, and internal PRO systems; no standalone frontend

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, public_api, payments, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Royalty calculation, payout processing, broadcaster integrations, and partner-facing API surface

complexity_level.rationale: Millions of performance report rows per quarter, complex royalty allocation rules, and batch payout processing at scale

risk_level.rationale: Financial data, copyright law compliance, and regulatory obligations for PRO licensing

data_sensitivity.rationale: Royalty statements and payout records contain financial data; rights holder bank account details handled

external_exposure.rationale: API consumed by vetted broadcaster partners and rights holder portals; not open to anonymous traffic

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC with broadcaster, rights_holder, and pro_admin roles; mTLS for broadcaster integrations
access_model.rationale: Multi-tenant access model with role-scoped API surfaces; mTLS for high-volume broadcaster connections per partner SLA

feature_scope.value: Performance report ingestion from broadcasters, works registry lookup, royalty calculation engine using rate tables, quarterly statement generation, payout batch processing, society registration management
feature_scope.rationale: Full PRO operational lifecycle from report receipt to rights holder payout

mvp_scope.value: Performance report ingestion, works registry matching, royalty calculation for registered works, statement generation API, manual payout initiation
mvp_scope.rationale: Core calculation-to-statement flow required before automating quarterly payout batches

security_baseline.value: mTLS for broadcaster API connections, AES-256 encryption at rest for rights holder financial data, field-level encryption for bank account details, TLS 1.3, RBAC, full audit trail for all financial operations
security_baseline.rationale: PRO financial data requires bank-grade controls; copyright law mandates audit trails for all royalty decisions

frontend_stack.value: None - API service only; rights holder and broadcaster portals are separate projects
frontend_stack.rationale: api_service project class; UI portals are out of scope

backend_stack.rationale: Spring Boot ecosystem maturity for complex domain logic; Java 21 virtual threads for high-throughput report ingestion

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with read replicas for reporting; Redis 7 for caching works registry lookups
data_platform.rationale: Postgres ACID guarantees for royalty transaction integrity; read replicas isolate statement generation load from transactional writes

hosting_target.value: Azure AKS with multi-AZ node pools
hosting_target.rationale: AKS for container orchestration at scale; Azure preferred by PRO for compliance with EU data residency obligations

privacy_retention_policy.value: Rights holder personal and financial data retained for 7 years per copyright law; performance reports retained for 5 years; bank account details encrypted and purged on account closure
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Copyright and financial regulations mandate long-term record retention for audit purposes

future_scope.value: ML-based cue-sheet fraud detection, self-service broadcaster onboarding portal, real-time royalty accrual dashboard for rights holders
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch roadmap to reduce manual PRO operations and increase rights holder transparency

constraints.value: Must process quarterly performance reports of up to 50 million rows within 48-hour window; payout batches must reconcile to cent accuracy; EU data residency required
constraints.rationale: Regulatory filing deadlines and copyright law accuracy requirements

observability_baseline.value: New Relic APM for API latency and throughput; custom financial reconciliation metrics; PagerDuty for payout batch failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Financial system requires rapid alerting on reconciliation errors or batch failures

execution_preference.value: Domain-driven design with quarterly release cadence aligned to PRO royalty distribution calendar
execution_preference.rationale: Business logic complexity warrants DDD; release cadence matches quarterly royalty cycle

compliance_context.value: Copyright Act compliance, GDPR for rights holder personal and financial data, SOC 2 Type II for financial data handling, EU data residency for all rights holder PII
compliance_context.rationale: PRO operates under statutory licensing framework; SOC 2 required for broadcaster enterprise agreements

project.goal: Royalty calculation and payout API for performing rights organization
project.target_users: Broadcasters, rights holders, PRO staff
project.timeline: Q4 launch for annual royalty cycle

backend.framework: Spring Boot 3.2 with Spring Data JPA
backend.api: REST with OpenAPI 3.0 plus mTLS for broadcaster connections

security.auth: Keycloak with broadcaster/rights_holder/pro_admin roles
security.transport: mTLS for broadcasters, TLS 1.3 for all traffic
security.encryption: AES-256 at rest, field-level encryption for bank account numbers

data.cache: Redis 7 for works registry lookup cache
data.storage: Azure Blob Storage for performance report CSV uploads

reasoner_reviews.product: The core accuracy requirement - cent-level reconciliation of royalties across potentially millions of works and thousands of rights holders - demands immutable financial transaction records and idempotent batch processing. Performance report matching against the works registry is the highest-complexity operation; a fuzzy matching fallback for unregistered works must feed a manual review queue rather than drop data.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Java 21 virtual threads are well-suited for the I/O-bound performance report ingestion workload - reading 50M CSV rows from Azure Blob Storage and matching against PostgreSQL. The royalty calculation engine should be a pure domain service with no I/O side effects, enabling unit testing at scale. Quarterly batch jobs must be idempotent and resumable from checkpoint to handle the 48-hour processing window without requiring a restart on failure.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS for broadcaster connections prevents unauthorized performance report submissions that could dilute royalty pools. Field-level encryption for bank account numbers means a database dump cannot expose payment routing details. All royalty calculation decisions must be logged to an immutable audit table with the input performance report ID, matched work ISRC, applied rate table version, and calculated amount for regulatory review.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 50 million rows in 48 hours is ~290 rows/second average throughput, but peak ingestion from multiple broadcasters submitting simultaneously will require horizontal scaling of the ingestion workers. PostgreSQL bulk COPY for CSV ingestion is orders of magnitude faster than row-by-row inserts. Cent-level reconciliation requires NUMERIC(18,2) throughout - no floating point arithmetic anywhere in the royalty pipeline.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The plan needs explicit handling for multi-claimant works where multiple rights holders share a performance royalty by configured splits. The split allocation algorithm and rounding strategy must be specified upfront; rounding errors compound across millions of rows and will cause reconciliation failures at the payout stage.

backend.service_design: Spring Boot application organized into bounded contexts: performance_ingestion (CSV parsing, broadcaster auth, bulk load), works_registry (ISRC lookup, fuzzy match, manual review queue), royalty_calculation (pure domain service, rate table evaluation, split allocation), statements (statement generation, PDF export), payouts (batch orchestration, SWIFT integration, reconciliation)
backend.api_patterns: REST API with OpenAPI 3.0 spec. Broadcaster endpoints: POST /v1/performance-reports (multipart CSV upload), GET /v1/performance-reports/{id}/status. Rights holder endpoints: GET /v1/royalties/statements, GET /v1/royalties/statements/{period}. Admin endpoints: POST /v1/payouts/batches, GET /v1/payouts/batches/{id}/reconciliation, PUT /v1/rate-tables/{id}
backend.background_jobs: Spring Batch jobs: (1) performance_report_processor - parallel chunk-oriented reading with 10K row chunks, ISRC matching, royalty allocation writes; (2) statement_generator - quarterly aggregation per rights holder; (3) payout_batch_initiator - SWIFT transfer initiation with reconciliation ledger update. All jobs idempotent with checkpoint restart support.
backend.scaling_notes: Horizontal scaling of ingestion pods via AKS HPA on CPU during quarterly batch windows. PostgreSQL COPY for bulk row ingestion. Read replicas serve statement generation queries to isolate from transactional batch writes.
backend.backend_risks: ISRC matching failure rate impacts royalty pool completeness; unmatched works must route to manual review, not be discarded. SWIFT gateway downtime during payout window requires retry queue with timeout escalation.

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm with three clients: broadcaster (mTLS + client credentials), rights_holder (OIDC authorization code), pro_admin (OIDC with MFA). API gateway validates JWT claims before routing to backend services.
security.authorization_model: RBAC: broadcaster role scoped to report submission and status only; rights_holder scoped to own royalty data via subject claim validation; pro_admin full access with 4-eyes approval required for rate table changes.
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault for SWIFT credentials, Keycloak client secrets, and database encryption keys. Field-level encryption of bank_account_number and bank_routing_code using Azure Key Vault envelope encryption before PostgreSQL storage.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Immutable royalty_audit_events table: every royalty calculation logged with performance_report_id, isrc_code, rights_holder_id, rate_table_version, calculated_amount NUMERIC(18,2), allocated_at. Payout audit trail in payout_ledger table. New Relic logs for API access events. 7-year retention aligned with copyright law.

data.entities: rights_holders (id UUID PK, legal_name, society_registration_id, bank_account_encrypted, bank_routing_encrypted, joined_at), licensed_works (id UUID PK, isrc_code CHAR(12) UNIQUE, title, primary_rights_holder_id FK, work_type ENUM musical/sound_recording, registered_at), performance_reports (id UUID PK, broadcaster_id FK, reporting_period DATE, row_count BIGINT, status ENUM received/processing/matched/settled, submitted_at), performance_report_rows (id UUID PK, report_id FK, isrc_reported VARCHAR, broadcast_count INT, broadcast_duration_seconds INT, matched_work_id FK NULLABLE, match_status ENUM matched/unmatched/manual_review), royalty_statements (id UUID PK, rights_holder_id FK, period DATE, gross_amount NUMERIC(18,2), deduction_amount NUMERIC(18,2), net_amount NUMERIC(18,2), generated_at), payout_batches (id UUID PK, period DATE, total_amount NUMERIC(18,2), status ENUM pending/initiated/settled/failed, swift_reference VARCHAR, settled_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 primary (Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server, 32 vCPU, 256GB RAM) with 2 read replicas. Partitioned performance_report_rows table by reporting_period (quarterly ranges) for efficient purge and query. Azure Blob Storage for raw CSV uploads (7-year retention). Redis 7 cache for licensed_works ISRC lookup (TTL 24h, invalidated on registry update).
data.analytics_events: royalty_calculation_completed (rights_holder_id, period, net_amount), performance_report_matched (report_id, match_rate_pct), payout_initiated (batch_id, total_amount), unmatched_works_count (report_id, count) - fed to New Relic for financial operations dashboards
data.data_risks: Duplicate performance report submissions from broadcaster system retries must be idempotency-checked at report and row level to prevent royalty double-counting. cent-level precision requires NUMERIC(18,2) throughout; any JOIN introducing floats must be caught in code review.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster: system node pool (Standard_D4s_v3), ingestion node pool (Standard_F8s_v2, scales 2-20 nodes during quarterly batch), API node pool (Standard_D4s_v3, 3-8 nodes always-on). Azure Application Gateway with WAF routes external traffic. Internal services communicate over cluster network.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps pipeline: Maven build, JUnit (90% coverage gate), SonarQube scan, Docker build, push to ACR, Helm chart deploy to AKS. Separate pipeline stage for quarterly batch job deployment with manual approval gate.
devops.observability_stack: New Relic APM with Spring Boot integration. Custom metrics: report_rows_processed_per_second, royalty_calculation_throughput, isrc_match_rate_pct, payout_batch_settlement_time_hours, unmatched_works_queue_depth. PagerDuty alert on payout_batch_failed or reconciliation_variance_detected.
devops.cost_controls: Ingestion node pool autoscales to zero between quarterly batch windows (~8 weeks idle), saving ~$3,200/month outside batch periods. Azure Reserved Instances for always-on API node pool. PostgreSQL read replicas paused between quarterly reporting cycles.

target_output.thinking_summary: High-scale financial API for a performing rights organization. Key architectural decisions: Spring Batch for idempotent, resumable 50M-row quarterly processing with checkpoint restart; NUMERIC(18,2) throughout the royalty pipeline to prevent floating point reconciliation errors; field-level encryption for bank account details via Azure Key Vault envelope encryption; immutable royalty_audit_events table for copyright law compliance. The multi-claimant split allocation algorithm needs explicit rounding strategy (half-up to nearest cent) to prevent compounding errors.
target_output.title: Performing Rights Organization Royalty Management API
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale Java Spring Boot API service for a performing rights organization that ingests broadcaster performance reports (up to 50M rows/quarter), matches reported ISRCs against the licensed works registry, calculates royalties using versioned rate tables, generates rights holder statements, and initiates quarterly SWIFT payout batches. Built on Azure AKS with PostgreSQL 16, mTLS for broadcaster connections, and field-level encryption for financial data in compliance with copyright law and SOC 2 requirements.
target_output.architecture_overview: Domain-driven Spring Boot API organized across five bounded contexts: performance ingestion, works registry, royalty calculation, statement generation, and payout orchestration. Broadcasters connect via mTLS-authenticated endpoints and upload CSV performance reports to Azure Blob Storage; a Spring Batch ingestion job reads reports in 10K-row chunks, performs ISRC matching against the licensed_works registry (Redis-cached), calculates royalties using the current versioned rate table, and writes results to the royalty_calculation_results table with cent-level NUMERIC(18,2) precision throughout. Quarterly Spring Batch jobs aggregate per-rights-holder statements, then a payout batch job initiates SWIFT transfers and writes to an immutable reconciliation ledger. AKS autoscales the ingestion node pool from 2 to 20 nodes during the 48-hour quarterly batch window. Rights holder and broadcaster portals consume the API independently; this service is API-only with Keycloak RBAC and New Relic APM for financial operations monitoring.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Java 21 (virtual threads for I/O-bound ingestion), Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Batch 5, Spring Data JPA, Spring Security with Keycloak adapter. Database: PostgreSQL 16 (Azure Flexible Server, 32 vCPU, quarterly partitioning on performance_report_rows). Cache: Redis 7 (ISRC works registry lookup). Auth: Keycloak (mTLS broadcaster clients, OIDC rights holder and admin). Payment integration: SWIFT messaging via partner gateway. Storage: Azure Blob Storage (CSV report uploads, 7-year retention). Encryption: Azure Key Vault for field-level bank account encryption. CI/CD: Azure DevOps with Maven and Helm. Monitoring: New Relic APM. Infrastructure: AKS multi-AZ with Azure Application Gateway WAF.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Performance Report Ingestion: broadcasters POST multipart CSV to /v1/performance-reports; bulk COPY to PostgreSQL partition; status polling via GET /v1/performance-reports/{id}/status. ISRC Matching Engine: Redis-cached licensed_works lookup with exact ISRC match; Levenshtein fuzzy match fallback for variant ISRCs; unmatched rows routed to manual_review_queue table. Royalty Calculation: pure domain service evaluating rate_tables by work_type and broadcaster_class; multi-claimant split allocation with half-up rounding; writes to royalty_calculation_results with full audit row. Statement Generation: quarterly Spring Batch job aggregates results per rights holder into royalty_statements with gross, deduction, and net amounts. Payout Orchestration: POST /v1/payouts/batches triggers SWIFT transfer initiation; reconciliation report available at GET /v1/payouts/batches/{id}/reconciliation.
target_output.system_components: Azure Application Gateway (WAF, TLS termination, mTLS for broadcaster clients). AKS API node pool (Spring Boot API pods, 3-8 nodes). AKS ingestion node pool (Spring Batch worker pods, 2-20 nodes, autoscales quarterly). PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server primary plus 2 read replicas (EU region). Redis 7 Azure Cache (ISRC lookup). Azure Blob Storage (performance report CSV uploads, 7-year retention). Keycloak cluster (broadcaster, rights_holder, pro_admin realms). Azure Key Vault (bank account field encryption keys, SWIFT credentials). New Relic APM agents. SWIFT partner gateway (bank transfer initiation).
target_output.workflows: Broadcaster Report Submission: Broadcaster authenticates via mTLS client certificate, uploads quarterly CSV (up to 5GB) via POST /v1/performance-reports multipart. API writes report metadata to performance_reports table (status=received), stores CSV to Azure Blob Storage, enqueues ingestion Spring Batch job. Job reads CSV in 10K-row chunks via PostgreSQL COPY, lookups each ISRC against Redis works registry cache, writes matched rows to performance_report_rows with matched_work_id, routes unmatched to manual_review_queue. Quarterly Payout Cycle: PRO admin triggers POST /v1/payouts/batches for the quarter. Spring Batch aggregates royalty_calculation_results per rights_holder_id, deducts PRO administrative fee per configured schedule, generates royalty_statements, writes payout_batches row (status=pending). SWIFT integration sends transfer instructions; on settlement confirmation, updates status to settled and writes payout_ledger entries with Swift reference. Reconciliation report available at GET /v1/payouts/batches/{id}/reconciliation showing sum of individual statement net_amounts vs total SWIFT transfer amount with variance field.
target_output.data_model: rights_holders (id UUID PK, legal_name VARCHAR, society_registration_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, bank_account_encrypted BYTEA, bank_routing_encrypted BYTEA, country_code CHAR(2), joined_at TIMESTAMPTZ). licensed_works (id UUID PK, isrc_code CHAR(12) UNIQUE, title VARCHAR, primary_rights_holder_id UUID FK, co_rights_holder_splits JSONB, work_type ENUM musical/sound_recording, registered_at TIMESTAMPTZ). performance_reports (id UUID PK, broadcaster_id UUID FK, reporting_period DATE, csv_blob_path TEXT, row_count BIGINT, status ENUM received/processing/matched/settled, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). performance_report_rows (id UUID PK, report_id UUID FK, isrc_reported VARCHAR, broadcast_count INT, broadcast_duration_seconds INT, matched_work_id UUID FK NULLABLE, match_status ENUM matched/unmatched/manual_review) PARTITION BY RANGE (reporting_period). royalty_calculation_results (id UUID PK, performance_row_id UUID FK, rights_holder_id UUID FK, rate_table_version VARCHAR, calculated_amount NUMERIC(18,2), allocated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). royalty_statements (id UUID PK, rights_holder_id UUID FK, period DATE, gross_amount NUMERIC(18,2), deduction_amount NUMERIC(18,2), net_amount NUMERIC(18,2), generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). payout_batches (id UUID PK, period DATE, total_amount NUMERIC(18,2), status ENUM pending/initiated/settled/failed, swift_reference VARCHAR, settled_at TIMESTAMPTZ). royalty_audit_events (id UUID PK, performance_report_row_id UUID FK, isrc_code VARCHAR, rights_holder_id UUID FK, rate_table_version VARCHAR, calculated_amount NUMERIC(18,2), allocated_at TIMESTAMPTZ) - append-only, no UPDATE/DELETE.
target_output.api_design: REST OpenAPI 3.0. Broadcaster: POST /v1/performance-reports (multipart CSV upload, returns report_id), GET /v1/performance-reports/{id}/status (ingestion progress). Rights holder: GET /v1/royalties/statements (list statements by period), GET /v1/royalties/statements/{period} (statement detail with line items), GET /v1/royalties/statements/{period}/pdf (PDF export). Admin: POST /v1/payouts/batches (initiate quarterly payout, returns batch_id), GET /v1/payouts/batches/{id} (batch status), GET /v1/payouts/batches/{id}/reconciliation (variance report), PUT /v1/rate-tables/{id} (update rate table, requires 4-eyes approval header), GET /v1/works/unmatched (manual review queue with fuzzy match suggestions). Auth: Keycloak JWT Bearer for rights_holder and pro_admin; mTLS mutual certificate for broadcaster.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS enforced at Azure Application Gateway for all broadcaster API connections; broadcaster certificate CNs validated against registered broadcaster registry. Keycloak RBAC: broadcaster role scoped to report submission and ingestion status only; rights_holder role validates sub claim against rights_holder_id in all data endpoints; pro_admin requires MFA and 4-eyes dual approval for rate table changes. Field-level encryption: bank_account_number and bank_routing_code encrypted using Azure Key Vault envelope encryption (AES-256-GCM) before PostgreSQL storage; decryption only at payout initiation time. NUMERIC(18,2) enforced throughout royalty pipeline - no floating point. Immutable royalty_audit_events table: no UPDATE or DELETE permissions granted; row-level security ensures rights holders can only read own audit rows. SOC 2 Type II controls: audit log export to Azure Monitor, access reviews quarterly. GDPR: rights holder personal data in EU region, right-to-erasure implemented via anonymization (legal name redacted, bank details purged) preserving financial records for 7-year copyright law retention.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps pipeline: Maven build, JUnit (90% coverage gate), SonarQube SAST scan, Docker build, push to Azure Container Registry, Helm chart deploy to AKS dev/staging/production via separate pipeline stages. Production deployment requires manual approval gate. AKS node pools: api (Standard_D4s_v3, 3-8 nodes, always-on), ingestion (Standard_F8s_v2, 2-20 nodes, HPA on CPU 70% target during batch windows, scales to zero off-quarter). Blue/green deployment for API service to maintain zero-downtime during quarterly batch window. Quarterly batch jobs: manual trigger via POST /v1/payouts/batches with Spring Batch admin approval. Secrets: Azure Key Vault with AKS managed identity binding. Database migrations: Flyway with Spring Boot integration; migration scripts reviewed by DBA before production deploy.
target_output.observability: New Relic APM traces Spring Boot API requests and Spring Batch job execution steps. Custom financial metrics: report_rows_ingested_per_second (alert below 200 during batch), isrc_match_rate_pct (alert below 90%), royalty_calculation_throughput_rows_per_sec, payout_batch_settlement_time_hours (alert above 24h), reconciliation_variance_cents (alert on any non-zero variance), unmatched_works_queue_depth. PagerDuty escalation on payout_batch_failed or reconciliation_variance_detected. Azure Monitor for infrastructure health and Key Vault access anomalies.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS API node pool (3 Standard_D4s_v3 reserved): ~$800/month. Ingestion node pool scales to zero between quarterly batches (~8 weeks of quarterly burst): ~$4,800/batch period, ~$0 off-quarter. PostgreSQL Flexible Server (32 vCPU): ~$1,800/month. Redis Azure Cache (P1): ~$120/month. Azure Blob Storage (50GB/year CSV archive): ~$15/month. Estimated steady-state: ~$2,800/month; peaks during quarterly batch windows with auto-scale burst.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Core Pipeline (10 weeks): Performance report ingestion API with mTLS, ISRC matching engine with Redis cache and manual review queue, royalty calculation engine with rate table evaluation, PostgreSQL schema with quarterly partitions, Keycloak RBAC, Azure Key Vault bank account field encryption, CI/CD pipeline, SOC 2 audit logging. Phase 2 - Statements and Payouts (6 weeks): Statement generation Spring Batch job, PDF export endpoint, payout batch orchestration, SWIFT integration, reconciliation report endpoint, payout_ledger immutable table. Phase 3 - Operations Hardening (4 weeks): New Relic financial dashboards, AKS auto-scaling ingestion node pool, 4-eyes rate table approval workflow, GDPR right-to-erasure anonymization job, SonarQube security gate.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 with strict null safety via @NonNull annotations. NUMERIC(18,2) mandatory for all monetary fields - code review rejects BigDecimal.valueOf(double) patterns. Spring Batch chunk-oriented processing only for ingestion - no row-by-row JPA saves for bulk operations. Royalty calculation domain service has zero Spring dependency and 100% unit test coverage. Flyway migrations peer-reviewed by DBA before merge. SonarQube quality gate blocks PRs with critical security issues. 4-eyes approval enforced in Azure DevOps for production pipeline triggers.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ISRC fuzzy matching algorithm produces false positive work matches, misrouting royalties to wrong rights holders. Mitigation: fuzzy matches route to manual_review_queue with confidence score displayed to PRO staff; no automated payment on fuzzy matches. Risk: 50M row batch fails mid-run due to PostgreSQL connection saturation. Mitigation: Spring Batch checkpoint restart from last committed chunk; ingestion node pool scaling ensures connection pool headroom. Risk: SWIFT gateway downtime during quarterly payout window. Mitigation: payout_batches row remains in pending status with retry queue; PagerDuty alert triggers manual escalation. Tradeoff: field-level bank account encryption over transparent data encryption - adds decryption latency at payout time but ensures bank details are unreadable even with database access. Tradeoff: quarterly Spring Batch over streaming for royalty calculation - simpler reconciliation and audit trail at the cost of real-time rights holder balance visibility.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected NUMERIC(18,2) over DECIMAL(20,4) for royalty amounts: two decimal places sufficient for cent-level accuracy at the payout granularity required by law. Confirmed half-up rounding strategy for multi-claimant split allocations to minimize aggregate rounding variance across millions of rows. Chose ISRC as the canonical work identifier with fuzzy fallback rather than title matching only, to handle transcription errors in broadcaster CSV submissions. Confirmed Azure region as West Europe for EU data residency compliance with rights holder GDPR obligations.

metadata.notes: High-scale PRO royalty management API with mTLS, field-level financial encryption, NUMERIC precision, and Spring Batch 50M-row processing.

project_goal.value: Greenfield data pipeline ingesting streaming platform play-count events from Spotify and Apple Music partner feeds, aggregating per-track listener geography and retention metrics, and delivering chart-ready analytics datasets to music label A&R teams via Looker dashboards
project_goal.rationale: A&R teams rely on weekly manual exports; pipeline enables daily decision-making on track and artist investment

target_users.value: Music label A&R managers, catalog analysts, and marketing teams consuming streaming performance data for artist and release decisions
target_users.rationale: Internal data consumers identified in discovery; no external user-facing UI required

project_class.rationale: Pure batch data ingestion, transformation, and delivery pipeline with no user-facing application layer

capabilities.rationale: BigQuery for storage, Airflow for orchestration, Spotify/Apple Music API integrations, Looker for downstream analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-source ingestion with schema normalization, incremental loading, data quality gates, and SLA-driven delivery to Looker

risk_level.rationale: Internal data under platform partner data sharing agreements; breach would violate contracts but no PII at risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: Streaming metrics commercially sensitive under platform data sharing agreements; not PII or financial

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline and analytics datasets are internal-only; no public API endpoints

access_model.value: GCP IAM service accounts for pipeline jobs; Looker SSO via Google Workspace for A&R data consumers; BigQuery dataset-level ACLs by team role
access_model.rationale: Pipeline SA has BigQuery write only; analysts have read-only dataset access

feature_scope.value: Hourly play-count ingestion from Spotify and Apple Music partner APIs, geographic listener breakdown per track, listener retention curve computation, weekly chart snapshot materialization, A&R Looker dashboard with track and artist drill-down
feature_scope.rationale: Covers full A&R analytics workflow from raw event ingestion to boardroom-ready charts

mvp_scope.value: Daily play-count ingestion from Spotify, basic geographic aggregation, and static BigQuery tables for Looker; Apple Music and retention curves in Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Spotify covers 70% of label streaming revenue; daily granularity sufficient for MVP A&R decisions

security_baseline.value: GCP IAM service accounts with minimal permissions; Spotify and Apple Music API tokens stored in Secret Manager; BigQuery column-level security for unreleased track metadata; pipeline logs retained in Cloud Logging
security_baseline.rationale: Platform data sharing agreements require access controls and audit logging for streaming metrics

frontend_stack.value: None - no application frontend; Looker for analytics UI
frontend_stack.rationale: Data pipeline delivers to BigQuery; Looker is the existing BI tool for A&R teams

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Airflow 2.9 on Cloud Composer 3
backend_stack.rationale: Airflow native on Cloud Composer for managed orchestration; Python for ingestion, dbt for transformations

data_platform.value: BigQuery for data warehouse (partitioned by event_date, clustered by track_id); dbt Core 1.8 for transformations; GCS for raw event landing zone
data_platform.rationale: BigQuery columnar storage optimized for Looker aggregation queries; GCS for raw event archival and replay

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Composer 3 Autopilot for Airflow; BigQuery serverless; Cloud Build for dbt CI jobs
hosting_target.rationale: Fully managed GCP stack minimizes operational overhead for 2-engineer team

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw play-count events retained in GCS for 2 years via Nearline lifecycle; BigQuery aggregated tables retained indefinitely; platform API credentials rotated every 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Platform data sharing agreements specify minimum retention; no PII in scope so no GDPR deletion obligations

future_scope.value: Apple Music integration, real-time Kafka-based event stream for intraday charts, Spotify editorial playlist tracking, predictive breakout artist model
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2-3 roadmap after MVP proves data quality and A&R adoption

constraints.value: Pipeline must deliver daily aggregates to BigQuery by 06:00 UTC; Spotify API rate limit 1000 req/min; GCP project budget $1500/month
constraints.rationale: A&R teams start workday at 09:00 local time; 06:00 UTC deadline ensures freshness across US and EU time zones

observability_baseline.value: Cloud Monitoring for Airflow DAG success/failure metrics; Grafana dashboards for ingestion lag and row count anomalies; PagerDuty for SLA breach alerts; dbt test failures reported to Slack data-quality channel
observability_baseline.rationale: Data SLA breach is business-critical; dbt tests catch silent data quality regressions

execution_preference.value: Two data engineers; 2-week sprints; dbt models in version-controlled repo; incremental development with Airflow DAG per data source
execution_preference.rationale: Small team requires modular DAG design for independent iteration

compliance_context.value: Spotify and Apple Music platform data sharing agreements restrict use to internal label analytics only; unauthorized redistribution prohibited; access logging required for compliance audits
compliance_context.rationale: Risk level medium and data sensitivity internal under platform contract obligations triggers compliance context

project.goal: Daily streaming analytics pipeline from Spotify and Apple Music to BigQuery for A&R Looker dashboards
project.target_users: A&R managers, catalog analysts, marketing teams
project.timeline: MVP in 8 weeks delivering Spotify daily ingestion

backend.extraction: httpx for Spotify/Apple Music API calls

data.warehouse: BigQuery partitioned by event_date clustered by track_id
data.landing_zone: GCS raw event bucket with date-prefixed NDJSON paths

devops.ci: Cloud Build with dbt test gate

constraints.sla: BigQuery daily aggregates ready by 06:00 UTC

reasoner_reviews.product: The A&R use case is well-defined: track-level daily plays, geographic breakdown, and listener retention curves drive label investment decisions. The 06:00 UTC SLA is achievable with an incremental load strategy that avoids reprocessing the full event history each day.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Airflow on Cloud Composer with dbt for transformations is the right pattern. BigQuery tables partitioned by event_date and clustered by track_id make Looker queries cost-efficient. Raw GCS landing zone is critical for replay capability when API schemas change. Incremental dbt models using event_date as watermark keep daily runs under 30 minutes.
reasoner_reviews.security: Platform data sharing agreements require Spotify and Apple Music API tokens in Secret Manager, not Airflow Variables. BigQuery dataset-level ACLs must restrict raw event tables to pipeline service accounts only; analysts access only the dbt-materialized summary layers.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Spotify API rate limit of 1000 req/min means the extraction DAG needs a concurrent request limiter and exponential backoff. With catalog sizes of 50K tracks, a full refresh requires 50 API pages at 1000 tracks per page, feasible within 5 minutes at the rate limit.

backend.service_design: Airflow DAG per source: spotify_plays_daily and apple_music_plays_daily; each DAG sequence: extract -> validate -> load_raw_gcs -> dbt_run -> dbt_test -> alert_on_failure
backend.business_modules: SpotifyExtractor operator: Client Credentials auth, paginated track batch requests, NDJSON write to GCS with 1000 req/min rate limiter. DataQualityGate operator: row count greater than 0, no duplicate track_play_id, play_count_total non-negative checks before BigQuery load
backend.background_jobs: dbt Cloud Run Job transformation chain: stg_spotify_plays (raw cleanse), int_play_counts_daily (deduped per track per day), fct_play_counts (final grain), dim_tracks (ISRC/title/artist/label), rpt_geographic_breakdown (country+track weekly), rpt_retention_curves (7/14/30-day cohort self-join on play_events_raw)
backend.failure_handling: Airflow DAG SLA miss callback triggers PagerDuty; dbt test failure posts to Slack and halts downstream marts; GCS raw files retained for manual replay; idempotent dbt incremental models via unique_key on track_id+event_date

security.auth_design: GCP service account for Airflow DAGs: roles/bigquery.dataEditor on raw dataset, roles/bigquery.dataViewer on staging dataset, roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor for API tokens
security.secrets_management: Spotify client_id, client_secret, Apple Music team_id and key_id stored in Secret Manager; Airflow DAGs fetch at runtime via google-cloud-secret-manager SDK; no credentials in DAG source code or Airflow Variables
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Cloud Audit Logs enabled on BigQuery datasets and Secret Manager; Airflow task logs forwarded to Cloud Logging with dag_run_id and task_id as structured fields; retained 2 years for platform compliance audits

data.entities: play_events_raw (source ENUM spotify/apple_music, track_id TEXT, isrc CHAR(12), play_count INT, country_code CHAR(2), event_date DATE, ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, raw_json STRING) PARTITION BY event_date. fct_play_counts (track_id TEXT, event_date DATE, total_plays INT, unique_listeners INT, country_code CHAR(2), platform ENUM spotify/apple_music) PARTITION BY event_date CLUSTER BY track_id. dim_tracks (track_id TEXT PK, isrc CHAR(12), title TEXT, artist_name TEXT, label_id TEXT, release_date DATE, catalog_status ENUM active/archived). rpt_geographic_breakdown (track_id TEXT, country_code CHAR(2), week_start DATE, weekly_plays INT, weekly_unique_listeners INT). rpt_retention_curves (track_id TEXT, cohort_date DATE, day_7_return_rate REAL, day_14_return_rate REAL, day_30_return_rate REAL). chart_snapshots (snapshot_date DATE, track_id TEXT, rank INT, weekly_plays INT, weekly_change_pct REAL). label_reports (id UUID PK, label_id TEXT, report_period TEXT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, gcs_export_uri TEXT)
data.storage_design: BigQuery datasets in us-central1 multi-region; GCS bucket in us-central1 for raw NDJSON with 2-year Nearline lifecycle; BigQuery materialized views for frequently-accessed Looker aggregates
data.analytics_events: dbt exposures define Looker explores: track_performance (fct_play_counts + dim_tracks), geographic_heatmap (rpt_geographic_breakdown), retention_analysis (rpt_retention_curves)

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Composer 3 Autopilot in us-central1; BigQuery datasets in us-central1 multi-region; GCS raw bucket in us-central1; Cloud Build triggers on git push to main
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build steps: dbt parse syntax check, dbt test on staging models, dbt build full transformation chain, dbt docs generate and push to GCS; Airflow DAGs auto-synced from GCS DAG bucket via Cloud Build on merge to main
devops.observability_stack: Cloud Monitoring for Airflow DAG success_rate and task_duration_p95; Grafana dashboard for daily_ingestion_row_count by source with anomaly band; PagerDuty for 06:00 UTC SLA miss and row_count below threshold

target_output.thinking_summary: Greenfield GCP data pipeline for music label A&R analytics. Key decisions: BigQuery partitioned by event_date clustered by track_id for cost-efficient Looker queries; dbt incremental models with unique_key on track_id+event_date for idempotent daily runs; raw GCS landing zone for API schema change replay; Spotify tokens in Secret Manager only. The rpt_retention_curves dbt model uses a cohort self-join on play_events_raw to compute 7/14/30-day listener return rates per track.
target_output.title: Music Label Streaming Analytics Pipeline Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A greenfield GCP data pipeline ingesting Spotify and Apple Music play-count events via partner APIs, transforming through a dbt model layer in BigQuery, and delivering daily chart-ready analytics datasets to label A&R teams via Looker. Cloud Composer 3 Airflow orchestrates hourly extraction, data quality gating, and dbt transformation runs with a 06:00 UTC SLA for A&R morning briefings.
target_output.architecture_overview: ELT pipeline on GCP: Airflow DAGs on Cloud Composer 3 Autopilot extract play-count events from Spotify and Apple Music partner APIs, write NDJSON to a date-prefixed GCS raw landing zone, then load to BigQuery play_events_raw partitioned tables. dbt Core 1.8 runs the transformation layer with staging models cleansing raw events, intermediate models deduplicating at track+day grain, and fact/report models materializing geographic breakdowns and listener retention curves. BigQuery tables are partitioned by event_date and clustered by track_id. Looker connects to BigQuery via dbt-defined exposure layer for A&R track performance, geographic heatmap, and retention analysis dashboards. API credentials are stored in Secret Manager and fetched at runtime by Airflow task operators.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration: Apache Airflow 2.9 on Cloud Composer 3 Autopilot. Extraction: Python 3.12, httpx for async API calls, google-cloud-secret-manager. Transformation: dbt Core 1.8 with BigQuery adapter and dbt-expectations. Warehouse: BigQuery with event_date partitioning and track_id clustering. Raw Storage: GCS NDJSON with 2-year Nearline lifecycle. BI: Looker via BigQuery service account with dataViewer role. CI/CD: Cloud Build. Monitoring: Cloud Monitoring, Grafana, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Spotify Play Ingestion: daily incremental extraction of per-track play counts by country from Spotify Charts API; raw NDJSON to GCS; loaded to play_events_raw partitioned table. Geographic Aggregation: dbt rpt_geographic_breakdown computes weekly plays and unique listeners by track_id and country_code for Looker geographic heatmap. Listener Retention Curves: dbt rpt_retention_curves computes cohort-based 7/14/30-day listener return rates per track via self-join on play_events_raw. Weekly Chart Snapshots: chart_snapshots table materialized weekly ranking tracks by weekly_plays with week-over-week change percentage. Label Reports: on-demand BigQuery export to GCS CSV for A&R email distribution.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Composer 3 Autopilot Airflow hosting spotify_plays_daily and apple_music_plays_daily DAGs. SpotifyExtractor Python operator with Client Credentials auth, 1000 req/min rate limiter, NDJSON write to GCS. DataQualityGate operator validating row count, duplicate key, and null ISRC before BigQuery load. BigQueryInsertJobOperator loading raw NDJSON to play_events_raw. dbt Core 1.8 Cloud Run Job executing stg->int->fct->rpt model chain. GCS raw event bucket with 2-year lifecycle to Nearline. Secret Manager for Spotify client_secret and Apple Music private key. Looker explores defined via dbt exposures YAML.
target_output.workflows: Daily Spotify Ingestion: Airflow spotify_plays_daily DAG triggers at 01:00 UTC; SpotifyExtractor fetches play counts for active catalog tracks in batches of 1000, writing NDJSON to gs://label-raw-events/spotify/YYYY-MM-DD/; DataQualityGate validates row count and deduplication; BigQueryInsertJobOperator loads raw file to play_events_raw; dbt Cloud Run Job executes stg_spotify_plays, int_play_counts_daily, fct_play_counts, rpt_geographic_breakdown; dbt tests run per model; SLA check confirms fct_play_counts rows for yesterday present by 06:00 UTC; Slack success notification to data-pipeline channel. Weekly Chart Snapshot: Airflow chart_snapshot_weekly DAG triggers Sunday 02:00 UTC; dbt chart_snapshots model re-materialized ranking tracks by weekly_plays with week-over-week pct change; Looker content auto-refreshes via BigQuery query cache invalidation.
target_output.data_model: play_events_raw (source ENUM spotify/apple_music, track_id TEXT, isrc CHAR(12), play_count INT, country_code CHAR(2), event_date DATE, ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, raw_json STRING) PARTITION BY event_date. fct_play_counts (track_id TEXT, event_date DATE, total_plays INT, unique_listeners INT, country_code CHAR(2), platform ENUM spotify/apple_music) PARTITION BY event_date CLUSTER BY track_id. dim_tracks (track_id TEXT PK, isrc CHAR(12), title TEXT, artist_name TEXT, label_id TEXT, release_date DATE, catalog_status ENUM active/archived). rpt_geographic_breakdown (track_id TEXT, country_code CHAR(2), week_start DATE, weekly_plays INT, weekly_unique_listeners INT). rpt_retention_curves (track_id TEXT, cohort_date DATE, day_7_return_rate REAL, day_14_return_rate REAL, day_30_return_rate REAL). chart_snapshots (snapshot_date DATE, track_id TEXT, rank INT, weekly_plays INT, weekly_change_pct REAL). label_reports (id UUID PK, label_id TEXT, report_period TEXT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, gcs_export_uri TEXT).
target_output.api_design: No external API surface. Internal integration: Spotify Charts API GET /v1/charts/regional/{country}/weekly (paginated, OAuth2 Client Credentials, 1000 req/min). Apple Music Analytics API GET /v1/data/tracks/plays (JWT-signed requests). BigQuery read for Looker via service account with roles/bigquery.dataViewer on analytics dataset. Cloud Build API for dbt job trigger on git push. Cloud Monitoring API for 06:00 UTC SLA gate webhook to PagerDuty.
target_output.security_and_compliance: GCP IAM: pipeline SA with roles/bigquery.dataEditor on raw dataset only; analyst SA with roles/bigquery.dataViewer on analytics dataset only; no cross-dataset access. Spotify client_secret and Apple Music private key in Secret Manager; fetched at runtime via SDK; never in Airflow Variables or DAG code. BigQuery column-level security on play_events_raw.raw_json restricting access to pipeline SA only. Cloud Audit Logs enabled for BigQuery data access and Secret Manager; retained 2 years for platform compliance audits. Platform data sharing agreements: usage restricted to internal A&R analytics; no redistribution of raw play counts to third parties.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Composer 3 Autopilot manages Airflow infrastructure on GCP with no K8s cluster management required. Cloud Build pipeline on merge to main: dbt parse, dbt test on staging models, dbt docs generate, push docs to GCS; Airflow DAGs auto-synced from GCS DAG bucket via Cloud Build. Environments: dev (separate BigQuery dataset suffix _dev, separate Composer environment), production (partitioned BigQuery datasets with 2-year GCS lifecycle). Rollback: Airflow DAGs versioned by git hash; rollback by reverting git commit; dbt incremental models are idempotent so re-run on prior date partition corrects bad data without full reload.
target_output.observability: Cloud Monitoring: Airflow DAG success_rate, task_duration_p95 by dag_id, BigQuery slot_ms_per_job. Grafana dashboard: daily_ingestion_row_count by source with anomaly band at plus/minus 2 stddev, schema_drift_events counter, dbt_test_failure_rate by model. PagerDuty: SLA miss (fct_play_counts for yesterday not present by 06:00 UTC), row_count below 100 on any source for any day, dbt test failure on fct_play_counts or rpt_geographic_breakdown. dbt test failures post to Slack data-quality channel with model name, test name, and failure count.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Approximately $800/month: Cloud Composer 3 Autopilot $400 (2 worker CPUs average), BigQuery on-demand queries $200 for Looker and dbt runs, GCS storage $50 for raw event archive, Secret Manager $5. Looker billed separately. BigQuery is serverless and handles A&R query spikes without provisioning. Composer Autopilot scales workers on DAG parallelism automatically.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Spotify daily ingestion DAG, raw GCS landing zone, dbt staging and fct_play_counts models, basic geographic breakdown, Looker track performance explore, data quality gates and PagerDuty SLA alerting, Cloud Build CI with dbt test gate. Phase 2 (5 weeks): Apple Music daily ingestion; listener retention curve dbt models with 7/14/30-day cohort; chart_snapshots weekly materialization; Looker retention analysis explore. Phase 3 (4 weeks): on-demand label report generation to GCS; Grafana anomaly detection dashboards; BigQuery column-level security for unreleased track metadata; Cloud Audit Log integration for compliance reporting.
target_output.development_guidelines: dbt: all models have unique and not_null tests on primary keys; source freshness tests with warn_after 25hrs and error_after 26hrs; incremental models use unique_key on track_id+event_date to prevent duplicates on re-run. Python: ruff and mypy in Cloud Build; unit tests for SpotifyExtractor pagination and rate limiter with pytest-httpx mocks. No API credentials in source code or Airflow Variables - always Secret Manager. DAG IDs follow naming convention source_entity_cadence such as spotify_plays_daily. dbt model naming: stg_ for staging, int_ for intermediate, fct_ for facts, rpt_ for reports, dim_ for dimensions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Spotify Charts API schema change silently breaks ingestion. Mitigation: GCS raw NDJSON retention enables replay after schema fix; dbt source freshness tests detect missing data within 26 hours. Risk: BigQuery slot contention during large Looker query spikes. Mitigation: Looker uses separate analyst service account with on-demand billing; dbt pipeline uses reservation slots to isolate. Tradeoff: Cloud Composer over self-managed Airflow on GKE - higher cost but no K8s operational burden for 2-person team. Tradeoff: incremental dbt models over full refresh - requires careful unique_key management but reduces daily BigQuery cost by approximately 80 percent.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected BigQuery over ClickHouse for native Looker integration without additional connectors. Confirmed incremental dbt strategy over full refresh for cost efficiency at scale. Chose Cloud Composer 3 Autopilot over Cloud Run Jobs to get DAG retry, SLA monitoring, and dependency management. Platform API tokens stored in Secret Manager rather than Airflow Connections to avoid plaintext in Composer metadata database.

metadata.notes: Greenfield GCP streaming analytics pipeline with Airflow/dbt/BigQuery for music label A&R; 06:00 UTC SLA, Looker delivery, Secret Manager for platform API tokens.

project_goal.value: Build a low-latency real-time audio streaming backbone for a cloud rehearsal platform enabling distributed bands to play together with sub-50ms end-to-end latency using WebRTC and edge relay nodes
project_goal.rationale: Sub-50ms latency is the physical threshold for musicians to play together in sync; current VoIP-based solutions exceed 100ms and break musical timing

target_users.value: Platform engineering team deploying the relay and signaling infrastructure; band musicians as end users of the cloud rehearsal application connecting through it
target_users.rationale: Infrastructure project serves both the ops team managing nodes and musicians using the platform

project_class.rationale: Platform-level infrastructure project defining relay topology, signaling architecture, and autoscaling — not an application

capabilities.rationale: Infrastructure for WebRTC relay nodes, signaling backend, real-time telemetry, and CDN/DNS integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-region edge relay topology, sub-50ms latency SLA, autoscaling on latency metrics, and WebRTC TURN/STUN at scale

risk_level.rationale: Infrastructure failure impacts all active rehearsal sessions; latency regression directly degrades musical experience but no personal or financial data at risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: Audio streams are not recorded or persisted; only anonymized latency telemetry is retained

external_exposure.rationale: WebRTC relay nodes are publicly reachable via ICE for musician clients on residential internet connections

access_model.value: Session-token-based WebRTC credential issuance; signaling authenticated by JWT from upstream rehearsal application; relay nodes stateless with no persistent auth
access_model.rationale: WebRTC TURN credentials must be time-limited to prevent relay abuse; session-scoped tokens issued by signaling server

feature_scope.value: Multi-region WebRTC relay node deployment, TURN and STUN server provisioning, signaling server for session negotiation and relay assignment, per-stream latency telemetry, autoscaling on median session latency exceeding 40ms, DNS-based geo-steering to nearest relay cluster, relay node health monitoring
feature_scope.rationale: Complete infrastructure stack from relay provisioning to latency-driven autoscaling

mvp_scope.value: Two-region relay deployment (us-east-1 and eu-west-1), TURN server provisioning, signaling server for session and relay assignment, basic latency telemetry, manual scaling playbook
mvp_scope.rationale: Two-region coverage for US and European beta musicians; automated scaling deferred to Phase 2

security_baseline.value: Time-limited TURN credentials with HMAC-SHA1 per RFC 5766; signaling JWT validation; TLS 1.3 on signaling WebSocket; relay nodes in private subnets with security groups allowing only TURN and STUN ports
security_baseline.rationale: TURN relay abuse prevention and signaling authentication are the primary security controls for WebRTC infrastructure

frontend_stack.value: None — infrastructure project; no frontend component
frontend_stack.rationale: Infrastructure project defines relay and signaling stack; frontend is the upstream rehearsal application's responsibility

backend_stack.rationale: Go for low-latency, high-concurrency signaling server with minimal garbage collection impact; custom WebRTC signaling for tight latency control

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for latency telemetry time-series data; Redis 7 for relay node registry and session state
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse's columnar storage is optimal for high-volume per-stream latency metric aggregation; Redis for low-latency relay assignment lookups

hosting_target.value: AWS EC2 bare-metal instances for relay nodes (latency-sensitive, no virtualization overhead); ECS Fargate for signaling server
hosting_target.rationale: Bare-metal eliminates hypervisor jitter that adds unpredictable latency to audio packet forwarding; Fargate for stateless signaling

privacy_retention_policy.value: No audio content recorded or stored; per-stream latency metrics retained 90 days for infrastructure tuning; session metadata (participant count, region, duration) retained 1 year for capacity planning
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No personal data in audio streams; anonymized telemetry retained for operational purposes only

future_scope.value: Additional relay regions (ap-southeast-1, sa-east-1), automated latency-driven relay node autoscaling, adaptive bitrate control, WebRTC SFU for larger ensemble sessions beyond 6 participants
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 and 3 expansion based on beta adoption geography

constraints.value: Sub-50ms end-to-end latency SLA; relay nodes must use bare-metal EC2 to eliminate hypervisor jitter; infrastructure budget under $8,000/month for two regions
constraints.rationale: Latency physics require bare-metal; cost ceiling for pre-revenue infrastructure

observability_baseline.value: Grafana and Prometheus for relay node and signaling metrics; ClickHouse for per-stream latency time-series; PagerDuty alerts for median session latency above 45ms or relay node failure
observability_baseline.rationale: Real-time latency visibility is critical for operating sub-50ms SLA infrastructure

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure as code via Terraform; GitOps with GitHub Actions for relay node image builds and Terraform plan/apply
execution_preference.rationale: Reproducible multi-region deployments require IaC; GitOps ensures infra changes are reviewed and auditable

project.goal: Sub-50ms WebRTC relay infrastructure for cloud band rehearsal platform
project.target_users: Platform engineers and musician end-users

backend.api: WebSocket for signaling, TURN/STUN on UDP/TCP

security.auth: JWT for signaling, time-limited TURN credentials per RFC 5766
security.relay_security: Private subnet placement, security groups for TURN ports only

data.session_registry: Redis 7 for relay assignment and session state

devops.hosting: EC2 bare-metal for relay, ECS Fargate for signaling

open_questions.stun_provider: Self-hosted Coturn vs managed STUN service to be evaluated in week 1

reasoner_reviews.product: The sub-50ms latency target is not arbitrary — music theory and psychoacoustics establish that delays above 30ms disrupt ensemble timing perception, making 50ms the outer acceptable boundary. The relay node assignment algorithm must minimize physical hop count between musicians by selecting the relay geographically closest to the session participant with the highest RTT. Phase 2 WebRTC SFU is the right evolution for sessions larger than 6 participants where peer-mesh topology becomes unmanageable.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Bare-metal EC2 is correct for relay nodes — c6i.metal or c6gn.metal instances eliminate the 1-3ms hypervisor scheduling jitter that would consume a significant fraction of the 50ms budget. Coturn is the established open-source TURN server implementation with production deployment maturity. The signaling server's relay assignment logic should query the Redis relay registry for nodes with median_session_latency_ms below 40ms and select the least-loaded qualifying node rather than pure geo-distance, accounting for node saturation. ClickHouse's materialized views are ideal for computing per-region P50 and P95 latency aggregates over sliding windows for the autoscaling trigger.
reasoner_reviews.security: Time-limited TURN credentials per RFC 5766 using HMAC-SHA1 with a 5-minute validity window prevent relay node abuse by non-session participants. The Coturn server must be configured with the --use-auth-secret flag and the shared secret stored in AWS Secrets Manager, not hardcoded in Coturn configuration. Relay nodes in private subnets with NAT Gateway for outbound; inbound security groups allow only UDP 3478 (STUN/TURN) and TCP 5349 (TURN over TLS).
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Budget of $8,000/month covers two c6i.metal instances per region at approximately $1,900/month each (4 total at $7,600), plus Fargate signaling service, ClickHouse on RDS-compatible managed service or self-hosted, and Redis ElastiCache. Bare-metal instance cost is the dominant expense; the latency requirement justifies it.

backend.service_design: Go signaling server handles WebSocket connections from rehearsal app clients, issues TURN credentials, performs relay assignment by querying Redis relay registry, negotiates SDP offer/answer exchange between session participants, and streams per-connection latency measurements to ClickHouse. Coturn TURN/STUN servers run on bare-metal EC2 instances as systemd services managed by Terraform and Ansible provisioner.
backend.api_patterns: WebSocket endpoint: wss://signaling.rehearse.io/ws. Messages: {type: join_session, session_id, jwt}, {type: relay_assigned, turn_url, turn_credentials}, {type: sdp_offer, sdp}, {type: sdp_answer, sdp}, {type: ice_candidate, candidate}, {type: latency_report, stream_id, rtt_ms}. REST health endpoint: GET /health returns signaling server status and connected session count.
backend.scaling_notes: Signaling server is stateless; horizontal scaling on ECS Fargate with sticky session routing via ALB. Relay nodes are stateful (active TURN allocations); horizontal scaling adds new bare-metal instances registered in Redis relay registry. Scale-out trigger: median_session_latency_ms above 40ms in a region for 5-minute window, queried from ClickHouse materialized view.
backend.failure_handling: Relay node failure detected by Prometheus health check scrape failure. Redis relay registry entry for failed node marked inactive; signaling server excludes it from assignment. Active sessions on failed node receive reconnect signal with new relay assignment. Coturn process supervised by systemd with automatic restart on crash.

security.auth_design: JWT validation on WebSocket handshake: signaling server validates token against public key from upstream rehearsal app auth service. TURN credentials issued per session using RFC 5766 HMAC-SHA1 with 5-minute expiry and rotating shared secret stored in AWS Secrets Manager.
security.secrets_management: Coturn shared secret and signaling JWT public key stored in AWS Secrets Manager. EC2 instance profiles grant Secrets Manager read access to relay nodes. Secrets rotation: TURN shared secret rotated every 24 hours; active sessions reissue credentials transparently 60 seconds before expiry.
security.security_risks: TURN relay abuse if credential expiry is too long. Mitigation: 5-minute TURN credential lifetime; Coturn rate limiting on allocations per source IP. DDoS on UDP TURN port. Mitigation: AWS Shield Standard on EC2 Elastic IPs; Coturn max-bps and max-allocations-per-user limits.

data.entities: relay_nodes (node_id TEXT, region TEXT, ip_address TEXT, status ENUM active/inactive/draining, current_allocations INT, median_latency_ms FLOAT, last_heartbeat TIMESTAMPTZ) stored in Redis HASH per node_id. rehearsal_sessions (session_id TEXT, region TEXT, participant_count INT, relay_node_id TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ended_at TIMESTAMPTZ) stored in Redis with 24-hour TTL. latency_events (session_id TEXT, stream_id TEXT, participant_id TEXT, rtt_ms FLOAT, jitter_ms FLOAT, packet_loss_ratio FLOAT, measured_at DATETIME) ingested to ClickHouse via HTTP insert batches every 5 seconds.
data.consistency_notes: Relay registry reads from Redis with no replication lag concern — single Redis primary with ElastiCache Multi-AZ failover. ClickHouse latency_events table uses ReplicatedMergeTree engine across 2 ClickHouse nodes per region for durability of telemetry data.
data.analytics_events: session_started, session_ended, relay_assigned, relay_reassigned, latency_threshold_exceeded (rtt_ms > 45ms), participant_dropped — all ingested to ClickHouse latency_events with session context for infrastructure capacity planning.

devops.deployment_topology: Two regions: us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Per region: 2 bare-metal EC2 c6i.metal instances running Coturn, deployed via Terraform with Ansible provisioner for Coturn configuration and systemd service setup. 1 ECS Fargate service for Go signaling server (2-6 tasks, ALB sticky session routing). 1 ElastiCache Redis Multi-AZ for relay registry and session state. ClickHouse on 2-node self-hosted EC2 cluster per region. Route53 latency-based routing directs clients to lowest-latency regional signaling endpoint.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Go vet and staticcheck, unit tests for relay assignment logic, Docker build for signaling server, push to ECR, ECS rolling deploy. Terraform plans reviewed in PR via GitHub Actions Terraform plan output; apply on merge to main. Coturn configuration changes trigger Ansible playbook run against relay nodes via GitHub Actions self-hosted runner in the target region.
devops.rollback_strategy: Signaling server: ECS task definition rollback to previous revision via AWS CLI. Coturn configuration: Ansible playbook rollback using prior Coturn config file version from git. Infrastructure: Terraform state rollback via workspace. Relay node failure: automatic exclusion from relay registry within 30 seconds of Prometheus health check failure.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus on EC2 instances scraping Coturn metrics (allocations, bandwidth, errors) and Go signaling server metrics (connected sessions, relay assignments per second, credential issuance rate). Grafana dashboards: per-region P50/P95/P99 session latency from ClickHouse, relay node allocation counts, signaling server session throughput. PagerDuty: P50 session latency above 45ms in any region for 5-minute window, relay node allocation failure rate above 5%, signaling server error rate above 1%.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a high-scale WebRTC relay infrastructure for cloud band rehearsal where the dominant architectural constraint is the sub-50ms end-to-end latency SLA. Bare-metal EC2 is non-negotiable for relay nodes to eliminate hypervisor jitter. The signaling server relay assignment logic must query ClickHouse P50 latency materialized views and Redis relay registry in combination to select uncongested nodes closest to session participants. ClickHouse is well-suited for the high-volume per-stream latency telemetry with materialized views for the autoscaling trigger query. Coturn on systemd with Ansible provisioning is the operational pattern for TURN server lifecycle management.
target_output.title: Cloud Rehearsal Platform WebRTC Relay Infrastructure
target_output.executive_summary: Infrastructure project establishing a multi-region low-latency WebRTC relay backbone for a cloud band rehearsal platform, targeting sub-50ms end-to-end audio latency across distributed musician clients. The architecture deploys Coturn TURN servers on bare-metal EC2 instances in two regions to eliminate hypervisor jitter, a Go WebRTC signaling server on ECS Fargate for session negotiation and latency-aware relay assignment, ClickHouse for per-stream latency telemetry with materialized view aggregates driving autoscaling triggers, and Route53 latency-based routing for geo-steering clients to the lowest-latency regional signaling endpoint.
target_output.architecture_overview: Per-region stack: two bare-metal EC2 c6i.metal instances running Coturn TURN/STUN servers as systemd services, provisioned by Terraform and Ansible. The Go signaling server runs on ECS Fargate (2-6 tasks) behind an ALB with sticky session routing to maintain per-session WebSocket affinity. When a musician client connects, the signaling server validates the JWT, queries the Redis relay registry for active nodes with current median latency below 40ms, selects the least-loaded qualifying node, and issues time-limited RFC 5766 HMAC-SHA1 TURN credentials. Clients use the assigned TURN server as the ICE relay for WebRTC peer connections. Each client reports per-stream RTT and jitter measurements back to the signaling server, which batches them to ClickHouse every 5 seconds. ClickHouse materialized views compute per-region P50/P95 latency over 5-minute tumbling windows, driving the autoscaling signal that provisions additional relay nodes when P50 exceeds 40ms. Route53 latency-based routing directs DNS resolution to the nearest regional signaling endpoint. All relay nodes sit in private subnets; only TURN UDP 3478 and TURN-over-TLS TCP 5349 are open in security groups.
target_output.technology_stack: Relay: Coturn 4.6 TURN/STUN server on Ubuntu 22.04 LTS, bare-metal EC2 c6i.metal instances. Signaling: Go 1.22 with gorilla/websocket for WebSocket handling, custom SDP exchange and ICE negotiation logic. Session Registry: Redis 7 via ElastiCache Multi-AZ per region. Telemetry: ClickHouse on self-hosted 2-node cluster with ReplicatedMergeTree. Infrastructure: Terraform 1.8 for all AWS resources, Ansible for Coturn service provisioning on bare-metal. Monitoring: Prometheus with coturn_exporter and custom Go metrics endpoint, Grafana dashboards with ClickHouse datasource, PagerDuty. CI/CD: GitHub Actions for signaling server builds and Terraform plan/apply.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Regional Relay Cluster: Coturn TURN servers on bare-metal EC2 providing UDP and TCP TURN relay for WebRTC ICE traversal; STUN responses for public IP discovery. Signaling Server: WebSocket-based session negotiation handling client join, relay assignment, SDP offer/answer exchange, and ICE candidate relay between session participants. Relay Assignment Engine: selects lowest-latency uncongested relay node from Redis registry using current median_latency_ms and current_allocations. TURN Credential Issuance: generates per-session time-limited credentials using RFC 5766 HMAC-SHA1 with rotating shared secret from Secrets Manager. Latency Telemetry Pipeline: clients report per-stream RTT and jitter; batched to ClickHouse; materialized views aggregate P50/P95 per region. Autoscaling Trigger: GitHub Actions workflow polling ClickHouse P50 metric and executing Terraform apply to add bare-metal nodes when threshold exceeded. Route53 Geo-Steering: latency-based routing records per region update on node lifecycle events.
target_output.system_components: Bare-Metal EC2 c6i.metal Instances (Coturn TURN/STUN, systemd service, Ansible-provisioned, per region 2 nodes). ECS Fargate Signaling Service (Go WebSocket server, 2-6 tasks, ALB with sticky sessions, per region). ElastiCache Redis Multi-AZ (relay registry HASH entries, session state with 24h TTL, per region). ClickHouse 2-Node Cluster (latency_events table ReplicatedMergeTree, materialized views for P50/P95 windowed aggregates, per region).
