AWS Secrets Manager (Coturn shared secret with 24h rotation, signaling JWT public key). Route53 Hosted Zone (latency-based routing records for signaling endpoints per region). Prometheus (scraping Coturn and signaling metrics). Grafana (latency dashboard consuming Prometheus and ClickHouse). PagerDuty (latency and relay failure alerts).
target_output.workflows: Musician Session Join: musician client connects to wss://signaling.rehearse.io/ws and sends join_session message with session_id and JWT. Signaling server validates JWT against rehearsal app public key. Queries Redis relay registry for nodes in client region with median_latency_ms < 40ms and current_allocations < max_allocations. Selects least-loaded qualifying node. Issues RFC 5766 TURN credentials with 5-minute expiry using HMAC-SHA1 and Secrets Manager shared secret. Sends relay_assigned message with turn_url and time-limited credentials to client. Client performs WebRTC ICE negotiation using assigned TURN; SDP offer/answer exchanged via signaling WebSocket. Latency Telemetry Flow: every 5 seconds each client sends latency_report message with per-stream rtt_ms, jitter_ms, packet_loss_ratio. Signaling server batches reports into ClickHouse HTTP insert to latency_events table. ClickHouse materialized view recomputes regional P50 over 5-minute tumbling window. Relay Node Autoscaling: GitHub Actions latency-watcher workflow polls ClickHouse P50 every 2 minutes. If P50 exceeds 40ms for two consecutive intervals, workflow runs Terraform apply to provision additional c6i.metal instance in the affected region. New node registered in Redis relay registry once Coturn health check passes. Relay Node Failure Recovery: Prometheus scrape of Coturn exporter fails. Alertmanager fires node_unhealthy alert. Ops webhook marks node as inactive in Redis relay registry. Active sessions on failed node receive relay_reassigned signal from signaling server with new TURN credentials. Coturn systemd service auto-restarts; node re-registers after health check recovery.
target_output.data_model: Redis relay_nodes HASH (key: relay:{region}:{node_id}): fields: ip_address, status ENUM active/inactive/draining, current_allocations INT, median_latency_ms FLOAT, max_allocations INT, last_heartbeat UNIX_TIMESTAMP. Redis rehearsal_sessions HASH (key: session:{session_id}): fields: region, participant_count, relay_node_id, created_at, expires TTL 24h. ClickHouse latency_events table (ENGINE ReplicatedMergeTree PARTITION BY toYYYYMMDD(measured_at) ORDER BY (region, session_id, measured_at)): session_id String, stream_id String, participant_id String, region String, rtt_ms Float32, jitter_ms Float32, packet_loss_ratio Float32, measured_at DateTime. ClickHouse materialized view regional_latency_p50_5min: region, window_start DateTime, p50_rtt_ms Float32, p95_rtt_ms Float32, session_count UInt32 aggregated over 5-minute tumbling windows.
target_output.api_design: WebSocket signaling protocol at wss://signaling.{region}.rehearse.io/ws. Messages: client sends {type: join_session, session_id: UUID, jwt: STRING}; server responds {type: relay_assigned, turn_url: STRING, turn_username: STRING, turn_credential: STRING, expires_at: ISO8601}; client sends {type: sdp_offer, target_participant_id: UUID, sdp: STRING}; server relays to target; client sends {type: ice_candidate, target_participant_id: UUID, candidate: STRING}; client sends {type: latency_report, stream_id: UUID, rtt_ms: FLOAT, jitter_ms: FLOAT, packet_loss_ratio: FLOAT}; server sends {type: relay_reassigned, turn_url: STRING, turn_username: STRING, turn_credential: STRING} on node failure. REST: GET /health returns {status: ok, connected_sessions: INT, relay_nodes_active: INT}. Coturn TURN server: UDP 3478 STUN/TURN, TCP 5349 TURN-over-TLS.
target_output.security_and_compliance: TURN credential abuse prevention: RFC 5766 time-limited credentials with 5-minute expiry using HMAC-SHA1 keyed with rotating shared secret. Secret stored in AWS Secrets Manager; rotated every 24 hours. Active sessions reissue credentials transparently 60 seconds before expiry via relay_credential_refresh signaling message. Signaling authentication: JWT validated against rehearsal app RSA public key on every WebSocket connection; sessions rejected on expired or invalid JWT. Relay node network isolation: bare-metal EC2 instances in private subnets; security groups allow inbound UDP 3478 and TCP 5349 from 0.0.0.0/0 only (required for ICE traversal), outbound unrestricted for UDP relay forwarding. No audio content is logged or persisted; only anonymized latency metrics in ClickHouse. Coturn configured with max-bps and max-allocations-per-user limits to mitigate amplification abuse. AWS Shield Standard enabled on relay Elastic IPs.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform workspaces per region (us-east-1, eu-west-1) manage EC2 bare-metal instances, ECS Fargate signaling service, ElastiCache Redis, Secrets Manager secrets, Route53 records, and ALB. Ansible provisioner in Terraform resource runs coturn.yml playbook on each bare-metal instance to install and configure Coturn systemd service, set TURN shared secret from Secrets Manager, and configure Coturn max-allocations and rate limits. GitHub Actions: Go vet, staticcheck, unit tests for relay assignment logic and latency aggregation, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy. Terraform plan output posted to PR as GitHub comment; apply on merge to main via GitHub Actions with manual approval gate for bare-metal instance additions. Two environments: dev with single t3.xlarge relay nodes for signaling integration testing (not bare-metal, latency SLA not enforced); production with c6i.metal. Bare-metal instance provisioning time approximately 15 minutes; autoscaling runbook documents Terraform workspace apply steps. Coturn health check scrape every 15 seconds via Prometheus coturn_exporter; failed scrape for 2 consecutive intervals triggers relay_node_inactive workflow.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes Coturn exporter metrics per relay node: coturn_allocations_total, coturn_bandwidth_bytes_total, coturn_errors_total, coturn_active_connections. Go signaling server exposes /metrics endpoint: signaling_sessions_active, relay_assignments_per_second, turn_credential_issuances_total, sdp_negotiations_duration_seconds histogram. Grafana dashboards: per-region P50/P95/P99 session rtt_ms from ClickHouse with 5-minute resolution, relay node allocation counts per node, signaling server session throughput, TURN bandwidth per relay. ClickHouse Grafana datasource queries regional_latency_p50_5min materialized view for near-real-time latency panels. PagerDuty alert triggers: regional P50 rtt_ms above 45ms for 5-minute window (critical), relay node Prometheus scrape failure (high), signaling server ECS health check failure (critical), TURN error rate above 5% (high). On-call runbook for relay node replacement and Coturn restart documented in ops wiki.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $7,800/month for two regions: 4 x c6i.metal at $1,890/month each totaling $7,560, ECS Fargate signaling service at $120, ElastiCache Redis M6g.large Multi-AZ at $160, ClickHouse on 2 x m5.xlarge at $280, Secrets Manager and data transfer overhead at $80. Bare-metal instances dominate cost; reserved instance 1-year commitment reduces c6i.metal to approximately $1,200/month reducing total to ~$5,400/month at steady state. Scaling ceiling: each c6i.metal instance handles approximately 500 concurrent TURN allocations at 50ms SLA; 4 nodes support ~2,000 concurrent musician connections. Additional bare-metal provisioning takes 15 minutes via Terraform.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP 10 weeks: two-region Coturn deployment on c6i.metal via Terraform and Ansible, Go signaling server on ECS Fargate with relay assignment and TURN credential issuance, RFC 5766 credential generation with Secrets Manager rotation, Redis relay registry, ClickHouse latency_events ingestion pipeline with P50/P95 materialized views, Prometheus and Grafana observability stack, Route53 latency-based routing, GitHub Actions CI/CD, PagerDuty alerting. Manual scaling playbook documented. Phase 2 6 weeks: automated Terraform-based autoscaling triggered by ClickHouse P50 latency threshold via GitHub Actions latency-watcher, relay node graceful draining before decommission, TURN credential rotation for active sessions on node failure, third region ap-southeast-1 for Asia-Pacific beta users. Phase 3 8 weeks: WebRTC SFU (mediasoup or ion-SFU) for sessions with more than 6 participants, adaptive bitrate signaling based on per-stream packet loss ratio, global anycast load balancing for relay UDP traffic.
target_output.development_guidelines: Terraform: modules for relay-cluster (EC2, security groups, Elastic IP), signaling-service (ECS, ALB, ECR), and telemetry (ClickHouse, Redis). Naming convention: {env}-{region}-{component}. All Terraform plan outputs reviewed in PR before apply. Coturn configuration rendered as Jinja2 template by Ansible; configuration version-controlled in git. Go signaling server: staticcheck and go vet in CI, unit tests for relay assignment scoring function with at least 5 test cases covering congested node exclusion and low-latency node selection. ClickHouse schema migrations managed via numbered SQL migration files applied by GitHub Actions workflow. No manual SSH access to relay nodes in production; all changes via Ansible playbooks triggered from CI.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: bare-metal EC2 c6i.metal instance availability limited in some regions. Mitigation: reserve instances with 1-year commitment to guarantee capacity; include c6gn.metal as alternative instance type in Terraform variable. Risk: ClickHouse 2-node cluster is a non-trivial operational dependency for telemetry — failure does not break audio sessions but blinds latency monitoring. Mitigation: Prometheus-based fallback alerting on Coturn bandwidth and connection metrics does not require ClickHouse. Risk: Coturn process crash during active sessions causes all allocations to drop simultaneously. Mitigation: systemd auto-restart with 5-second delay; signaling server detects node unavailability within 30 seconds and sends relay_reassigned messages to affected clients. Tradeoff: self-hosted ClickHouse over AWS Timestream for cost at this telemetry volume; Timestream would be lower-ops at 3-4x higher cost. Tradeoff: bare-metal EC2 over Graviton metal for x86 Coturn binary compatibility; evaluate ARM Coturn build for cost reduction in Phase 2.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Bare-metal EC2 confirmed over Fargate for relay nodes to eliminate hypervisor scheduling jitter. Coturn selected over self-built TURN server for IETF RFC compliance maturity and production deployment track record. ClickHouse selected over TimescaleDB for latency_events due to columnar aggregation performance on high-frequency per-stream measurements. RFC 5766 HMAC-SHA1 credentials with 5-minute expiry selected as TURN authentication scheme per WebRTC infrastructure best practices. Route53 latency-based routing selected over GeoDNS for dynamic latency measurement rather than static geographic rules.

metadata.notes: High-scale WebRTC relay infrastructure for cloud band rehearsal; bare-metal EC2 Coturn nodes, Go signaling server with latency-aware relay assignment, ClickHouse telemetry with materialized views driving autoscaling.

profile.domain: Live Performance Setlist and IEM Mix Mobile App

project_goal.value: Mobile app for gigging musicians that decomposes a monolithic setlist manager into microservices handling set scheduling, live chord chart display, in-ear monitor mix control, and venue logistics - all synced across bandmates' devices in real time
project_goal.rationale: Core product: give performing musicians a unified mobile interface backed by independent microservices for each show-day function

target_users.value: Gigging musicians - bandleaders, session players, touring bands - and their sound engineers
target_users.rationale: Primary users are performers needing reliable real-time coordination during soundcheck and live performance

project_class.rationale: Primary interface is iOS and Android mobile app; backend microservices support it

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, realtime, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Real-time sync across devices, backend microservices, Firebase Auth, venue hardware integration

complexity_level.rationale: Microservices decomposition, sub-500ms real-time sync, IEM hardware integration, multi-device state management

risk_level.rationale: Personal musician data and setlist IP; reliability during live performance is the primary non-functional risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: GDPR applies to musician personal and location data stored in show schedules and venue logistics

external_exposure.rationale: App accessible only to authenticated band members; no public API exposure

access_model.value: Firebase Auth with bandleader and band_member roles; band-scoped data isolation
access_model.rationale: Each band is an isolated tenant; bandleaders have write access to setlists and scheduling

feature_scope.value: Real-time setlist editor and display, chord chart viewer per song, IEM monitor mix control panel, venue and schedule management, bandmate device sync, show schedule calendar
feature_scope.rationale: Four distinct show-day capabilities mapped to four microservices

mvp_scope.value: Setlist real-time sync across band devices, chord chart display per song, band member management with Firebase Auth
mvp_scope.rationale: Core setlist sync is the highest-value MVP feature; IEM and venue integrations follow

security_baseline.value: Firebase Auth ID tokens on all API calls, band-scoped data access validation, TLS 1.3, no chord chart or setlist data accessible across band boundaries
security_baseline.rationale: Band data is IP; cross-band data leakage would be a critical privacy violation

frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform iOS and Android from single codebase; team has RN expertise

backend_stack.rationale: Fastify for low-latency microservices; Node ecosystem aligns with TypeScript end-to-end

data_platform.value: MongoDB 7 for documents; Redis 7 for real-time session state and sync queues
data_platform.rationale: MongoDB document model fits setlists, chord charts, and show schedules; Redis pub/sub for sub-500ms device sync

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Run per microservice
hosting_target.rationale: Cloud Run scale-to-zero suits event-driven show traffic with spikes during touring periods

privacy_retention_policy.value: Musician personal data and show schedules retained while band account active; deleted within 30 days of account closure; GDPR right to erasure honored within 72 hours
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR obligations for EU musician users; show schedule contains personal location data

future_scope.value: Streaming platform setlist sync (Spotify set history), merchandise inventory integration, fan-facing setlist reveal feed
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch community and platform integrations

constraints.value: Setlist updates must propagate to all band devices within 500ms; IEM mix control must maintain sub-50ms command latency to venue hardware; offline mode for chord charts during connectivity outage
constraints.rationale: Live performance reliability requirements; 500ms sync maintains show flow; offline chord charts prevent show stoppers

observability_baseline.value: Firebase Crashlytics for mobile crash reporting; Grafana plus Prometheus for microservice latency and WebSocket connection metrics
observability_baseline.rationale: Mobile crash visibility critical for show-day support; backend latency metrics for 500ms sync SLA

execution_preference.value: Microservices deployed independently; mobile app release on iOS TestFlight and Google Play beta tracks
execution_preference.rationale: Independent microservice deployments allow IEM service to be updated without touching setlist sync

compliance_context.value: GDPR applies to musician personal data and location data in show schedules; EU users covered by data processing agreement
compliance_context.rationale: GDPR for EU musician users whose personal and location data are stored in show schedules

project.goal: Real-time setlist and IEM mix app with microservices backend for touring bands
project.target_users: Gigging musicians and sound engineers
project.timeline: Live beta for summer touring season

frontend.state: Zustand for local state, React Query for server state
frontend.realtime: WebSocket client for sync service connection

backend.api: REST for CRUD, WebSocket for real-time sync

security.auth: Firebase Auth with custom claims for bandleader role
security.isolation: band_id validation middleware on all data endpoints

data.sync: Redis pub/sub for cross-device setlist events

reasoner_reviews.product: The 500ms setlist sync SLA is the core reliability promise for live performance. Any architecture decision that risks exceeding that latency - like synchronous database writes in the sync critical path - should be deferred or made async. The chord chart offline mode is a hard requirement: musicians cannot rehearse a song switch if their device loses venue WiFi.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Redis pub/sub over WebSocket is the right pattern for sub-500ms fan-out to multiple band devices. The sync service must be stateless so Cloud Run can scale horizontally during busy touring periods. MongoDB document model aligns well with setlists (ordered list of song documents) and chord charts (structured chord data per song section). The IEM mix microservice is the most latency-sensitive; it should have a dedicated Cloud Run service with minimum 1 instance to avoid cold start on mix commands during soundcheck.
reasoner_reviews.security: Band data isolation is the critical security control - a bug allowing Band A to read Band B's setlist would expose IP and touring information. Every MongoDB query must include band_id in the filter; a middleware layer should enforce this rather than relying on developer discipline per route. Firebase Auth custom claims carrying band_id and role must be re-verified server-side on every request.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Cloud Run cold starts (200-400ms) are a real risk for the IEM mix service which needs sub-50ms command latency. Minimum instance count of 1 for the IEM service eliminates cold starts. The sync service's WebSocket connections are long-lived; Cloud Run's request timeout must be configured to allow persistent WebSocket sessions, which requires Cloud Run session affinity.

frontend.app_structure: React Native app with four feature modules: SetlistModule (live editor, reorder drag-and-drop, real-time WebSocket sync), ChordChartModule (offline-first with WatermelonDB local storage), IEMModule (mix fader UI with optimistic updates and low-latency command dispatch), VenueModule (schedule, logistics, contact info)
frontend.pages_and_flows: Show Home (active setlist with next-song highlight, sync status indicator), Setlist Editor (draggable song list, add/remove songs, bandleader-only write access), Chord Chart Viewer (full-screen per-song, offline from WatermelonDB cache), IEM Mix Panel (per-instrument fader grid, master level, monitor send), Venue Info (address, load-in time, FOH contact)
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for show-day state (active song, sync connection status, IEM mix values). React Query for REST data fetching (setlist CRUD, venue info). WebSocket event handler updates Zustand setlist order on sync messages.
frontend.frontend_risks: WebSocket reconnection logic must handle venue WiFi drops gracefully, queuing setlist changes locally and replaying on reconnect. IEM fader optimistic updates must roll back if the hardware integration service returns an error.

backend.service_design: Four Cloud Run microservices: setlist-service (Fastify, REST + WebSocket, Redis pub/sub publisher), chord-chart-service (Fastify, REST, chord data CRUD), iem-service (Fastify, REST, venue hardware integration, minimum 1 instance), venue-service (Fastify, REST, show schedule and logistics CRUD)
backend.api_patterns: setlist-service: POST /setlists, PUT /setlists/{id}/songs (reorder), WS /setlists/{id}/sync (WebSocket for real-time updates). chord-chart-service: GET /songs/{id}/chords, PUT /songs/{id}/chords. iem-service: POST /shows/{id}/iem/commands (fader set, mute, solo). venue-service: GET /shows, POST /shows, PUT /shows/{id}
backend.failure_handling: setlist-service Redis pub/sub failure falls back to polling every 2 seconds. iem-service hardware command timeout (50ms) returns error to app for optimistic rollback. MongoDB write failures surface as 503 with retry-after header.
backend.backend_risks: IEM hardware integration is venue-dependent (different mixer OSC protocols per venue); abstraction layer needed to support Yamaha CL series and Behringer X32 OSC schemas.

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth ID tokens validated on every request via Firebase Admin SDK middleware. Custom claims: band_id (UUID), role (bandleader/band_member). Token refresh handled by React Native Firebase SDK.
security.authorization_model: Middleware extracts band_id from verified ID token and injects into all MongoDB queries as mandatory filter. Bandleader role required for setlist write operations (PUT /setlists/{id}/songs). Band members read-only on setlists and chord charts.
security.privacy_controls: MongoDB documents scoped to band_id; cross-band query impossible by construction. GDPR deletion: Fastify endpoint DELETE /bands/{id}/data triggers cascaded delete of setlists, shows, chord charts, and band member user records within 72 hours via async worker.

data.entities: setlists (band_id, show_id, songs: [{song_id, title, key, bpm, chord_chart_id, notes}], updated_at, version INT for optimistic concurrency). songs (band_id, title, artist, key, bpm, default_chord_chart_id, created_at). chord_charts (band_id, song_id, sections: [{name, chords: [{beat, chord}]}], updated_at). show_schedules (band_id, venue_name, venue_address, load_in_at, soundcheck_at, show_at, set_length_minutes, foh_contact). band_members (band_id, firebase_uid, display_name, instrument, role, joined_at, location_country). monitor_mixes (band_id, show_id, member_id, channel_levels: [{channel, level_db}], updated_at).
data.storage_design: MongoDB 7 Atlas (M30 cluster, multi-AZ, GCP europe-west1). Collections band-scoped with compound indexes on {band_id, show_id}. Redis Cloud Memorystore for setlist sync pub/sub channel per band_id. WatermelonDB (SQLite) on device for offline chord chart storage.
data.migration_notes: Monolith migration: read setlists and chord charts from legacy flat schema, transform to new document model, write to MongoDB Atlas via one-time migration script with idempotency key per band.

devops.deployment_topology: GCP Cloud Run services: setlist-service (0-10 instances, autoscale on concurrent WebSocket connections), chord-chart-service (0-5 instances), iem-service (1-5 instances, min-instances=1 to prevent cold start), venue-service (0-5 instances). Cloud Endpoints for API authentication forwarding. Firebase hosting for any static content.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint + TypeScript check, Jest unit tests (80% coverage), Docker build, push to Artifact Registry, Cloud Run deploy via gcloud CLI. Separate workflow for React Native: Expo EAS Build for TestFlight and Play Store beta.
devops.observability_stack: Firebase Crashlytics for mobile crash reporting. Grafana + Prometheus for Cloud Run metrics: setlist_sync_latency_p99_ms (alert above 500), iem_command_latency_p99_ms (alert above 50), websocket_connection_count, sync_event_fan_out_error_rate. PagerDuty on sync latency SLA breach.

ISS-001.title: No offline fallback for setlist sync - show breaks on venue WiFi outage
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan describes Redis pub/sub WebSocket sync but does not specify how the app behaves when the venue WiFi drops mid-show. Musicians need to see the current setlist and navigate songs even when disconnected.

history.note: Identified in round 1 audit; offline resilience not designed

ISS-002.title: IEM service cold start violates 50ms latency constraint
ISS-002.detail: iem-service deployed with zero minimum instances on Cloud Run. Cold start latency (200-400ms) exceeds the 50ms command latency SLA required for in-ear monitor control during soundcheck.

history.note: Flagged in round 1 audit; min-instances not configured

ISS-003.title: MongoDB band_id isolation not enforced at middleware level
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 plan relies on developers adding band_id filter to each MongoDB query. No middleware enforcement means a single missing filter could expose cross-band data.

history.note: Security review flagged missing middleware enforcement in round 1

latest_recommendations: Add WatermelonDB offline sync layer for setlist state with conflict resolution on reconnect
Set min-instances=1 for iem-service Cloud Run deployment to eliminate cold start during soundcheck
Implement Fastify middleware that extracts band_id from Firebase token claims and injects it as mandatory filter on all MongoDB repository methods
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Setlist and IEM App - Round 1 Microservices Architecture

convergence_state.reason: Three unresolved issues including one high-severity offline resilience gap require architectural changes before convergence

previous_audits.summary: Microservices decomposition is well-structured and the Redis pub/sub WebSocket sync design is sound, but three issues put live performance reliability at risk: no offline fallback, IEM service cold start exceeds SLA, and missing band_id middleware enforcement creates potential cross-band data exposure.
strengths: Clean four-service microservices decomposition aligned to show-day capability domains
Redis pub/sub fan-out design correctly targets sub-500ms setlist sync
WatermelonDB for chord charts already in the frontend spec - just needs to be extended to setlist offline state
concerns: No offline behavior specified for setlist sync during venue WiFi drops - showstopper for live performance

band_id MongoDB isolation relies on per-developer discipline rather than enforced middleware
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Setlist offline fallback required before production show use
recommendations: Extend WatermelonDB usage from chord charts to setlist state; implement sync-on-reconnect with last-writer-wins conflict resolution
Set min-instances=1 on iem-service Cloud Run deployment; document Cloud Run session affinity requirement for WebSocket connections in setlist-service
Build BandScopedMongoRepository wrapper that injects band_id from Fastify request context into all find/update/delete operations; register as Fastify plugin

previous_plan.title: Setlist and IEM App - Round 1 Microservices Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: React Native mobile app backed by four Fastify microservices on Cloud Run: setlist sync via Redis pub/sub WebSocket, chord chart CRUD, IEM mix control with hardware integration, and venue/schedule management. Firebase Auth with band-scoped isolation.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Four Cloud Run microservices per capability domain. WebSocket sync service fans out setlist changes to band devices via Redis pub/sub. MongoDB Atlas stores band-scoped documents. Cloud Run autoscales on traffic.
previous_plan.system_components: Cloud Run (setlist-service, chord-chart-service, iem-service, venue-service), MongoDB Atlas, Redis Memorystore, Firebase Auth, GitHub Actions
previous_plan.workflows: Bandleader reorders setlist; setlist-service publishes event to Redis band channel; all connected bandmates receive WebSocket update. Sound engineer adjusts IEM fader; iem-service sends OSC command to venue mixer hardware.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses three issues: offline setlist resilience (ISS-001) solved by extending WatermelonDB to setlist state with sync-on-reconnect, IEM cold start (ISS-002) solved by min-instances=1 on Cloud Run, and band_id isolation (ISS-003) solved by a BandScopedMongoRepository Fastify plugin that injects band_id as mandatory query filter. These are targeted fixes; the core microservices design from round 1 is preserved.

fix_report.action_taken: Setlist state is now persisted to WatermelonDB local SQLite on the device in addition to the WebSocket sync stream. On venue WiFi disconnect, the app switches to offline mode displaying the last-synced setlist from WatermelonDB. On reconnect, the app compares local version INT against the server; server version wins on conflict (last-writer-wins at the setlist level since only the bandleader can edit). A sync status indicator in the app header shows connected/offline/syncing states to band members.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Band members can navigate setlist songs and view chord charts during venue WiFi outage. Setlist state recovers automatically on reconnect without manual refresh.

fix_report.action_taken: iem-service Cloud Run deployment updated to min-instances=1. Cloud Run session affinity (--session-affinity flag) enabled on setlist-service to keep WebSocket connections routed to the same instance. Both changes documented in the Cloud Run Terraform module with explanatory comments on why minimum instances are required for low-latency services.
fix_report.expected_outcome: iem-service has zero cold start latency; IEM mix commands consistently under 50ms. WebSocket connections on setlist-service maintain session affinity, preventing mid-show disconnect from Cloud Run scale-out instance switches.

fix_report.action_taken: BandScopedMongoRepository Fastify plugin implemented: decorates the Fastify instance with a getMongoDB(bandId) method that returns a MongoDB repository wrapper injecting {band_id: bandId} into every find, findOne, updateOne, deleteOne, and aggregate query filter. Band_id extracted from the verified Firebase ID token custom claim in the auth middleware and attached to the Fastify request object. Direct MongoDB collection access outside the BandScopedMongoRepository is prohibited by ESLint rule (no-direct-mongo-collection) added to the project ESLint config.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cross-band data access is architecturally impossible for any route that uses BandScopedMongoRepository. ESLint rule prevents regression of direct collection access. Security audit can verify band isolation by reviewing plugin implementation rather than inspecting every route.

target_output.title: Setlist and IEM App - Offline Resilience, IEM Latency, and Band Isolation Fixes
target_output.executive_summary: A React Native mobile app for gigging musicians backed by four Fastify microservices on GCP Cloud Run. This revision resolves three live performance reliability issues: setlist offline mode via WatermelonDB persistence and sync-on-reconnect, IEM mix service minimum instance configuration to eliminate cold start latency, and a BandScopedMongoRepository plugin enforcing mandatory band_id isolation on all MongoDB operations.
target_output.architecture_overview: Four GCP Cloud Run microservices handle distinct show-day capabilities: setlist-service (Fastify with REST and WebSocket, Redis pub/sub fan-out to band devices), chord-chart-service (Fastify, REST, MongoDB chord document CRUD), iem-service (Fastify, min-instances=1, OSC integration to venue mixer hardware), and venue-service (Fastify, REST, show schedule management). React Native app uses WatermelonDB for offline-first setlist and chord chart persistence; on reconnect, version reconciliation with server restores latest state. Firebase Auth ID tokens carry band_id and role custom claims; a Fastify BandScopedMongoRepository plugin enforces band_id as mandatory filter on every MongoDB operation. MongoDB 7 Atlas stores band-scoped documents; Redis 7 Memorystore powers the setlist sync pub/sub channel per band. All microservices deployed independently on Cloud Run with Prometheus metrics and Grafana dashboards for sync latency SLA monitoring.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native 0.74, TypeScript, WatermelonDB (offline SQLite), Zustand, React Query, React Native Firebase. Backend: Node.js 22, Fastify 4 (four microservices), Firebase Admin SDK, MongoDB Node driver with BandScopedMongoRepository plugin. Data: MongoDB 7 Atlas (GCP europe-west1), Redis 7 Cloud Memorystore. Auth: Firebase Auth with custom claims (band_id, role). Infrastructure: GCP Cloud Run (session affinity for setlist-service, min-instances=1 for iem-service), Cloud Endpoints. CI: GitHub Actions (server), Expo EAS Build (mobile). Monitoring: Firebase Crashlytics, Grafana plus Prometheus.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Setlist Sync: bandleader edits setlist in real time; changes fan out via Redis pub/sub to all connected band devices within 500ms; offline mode serves last-synced WatermelonDB state during WiFi outage; sync-on-reconnect resolves version conflicts server-side. Chord Chart Viewer: full-screen per-song chord display with section navigation; synced to WatermelonDB for offline access; bandleader can annotate chords per show. IEM Mix Control: per-musician fader grid with per-channel level and mute controls; Fastify iem-service translates mix commands to OSC messages for Yamaha CL and Behringer X32 venue mixers; min-instances=1 ensures sub-50ms command latency. Venue and Schedule: show schedule calendar, venue details, load-in times, FOH contact info; read-only for band members, writable by bandleader.
target_output.system_components: GCP Cloud Run: setlist-service (0-10 instances, session affinity enabled, WebSocket + REST), chord-chart-service (0-5 instances, REST), iem-service (1-5 instances, min-instances=1, REST + OSC client), venue-service (0-5 instances, REST). MongoDB 7 Atlas M30 cluster (GCP europe-west1, multi-AZ). Redis 7 Cloud Memorystore (pub/sub for setlist sync). Firebase Auth (ID token issuance, custom claim injection). WatermelonDB SQLite (on-device offline storage for setlists and chord charts). Firebase Crashlytics (mobile crash reporting). Grafana plus Prometheus (Cloud Run microservice metrics). GitHub Actions plus Expo EAS Build (CI/CD).
target_output.workflows: Setlist Update (online): Bandleader drags song to new position in SetlistEditor. React Native dispatches PUT /setlists/{id}/songs to setlist-service with new songs array and version INT. setlist-service validates bandleader role, saves to MongoDB with incremented version, publishes SetlistUpdated event to Redis band_{band_id} channel. All WebSocket subscribers (band member devices) receive event and update Zustand store + WatermelonDB local row. Setlist Recovery (offline to online): Device loses venue WiFi. App switches to offline mode, reads setlist from WatermelonDB, displays with offline banner. On WiFi reconnect, WebSocket client reconnects to setlist-service. App GETs current setlist version from server. If server version > local version, server state overwrites WatermelonDB and Zustand. If equal, no action. IEM Mix Control: Sound engineer opens IEM Mix Panel, adjusts vocalist monitor send fader to +2dB. React Native sends POST /shows/{id}/iem/commands with channel=vocals, level_db=2, action=set. iem-service (warm, no cold start) validates band scope, translates to OSC /ch/01/mix/fader message, sends to venue mixer IP via UDP, returns 200 within 30ms. App updates optimistic UI.
target_output.data_model: setlists (band_id ObjectId, show_id ObjectId, songs: [{song_id ObjectId, title String, key String, bpm Int, chord_chart_id ObjectId, notes String}], version Int, updated_at Date, updated_by String). songs (band_id ObjectId, title String, artist String, key String, bpm Int, default_chord_chart_id ObjectId, created_at Date). chord_charts (band_id ObjectId, song_id ObjectId, sections: [{name String, chords: [{beat Int, chord String}]}], updated_at Date). show_schedules (band_id ObjectId, venue_name String, venue_address String, venue_mixer_ip String, load_in_at Date, soundcheck_at Date, show_at Date, foh_contact String). band_members (band_id ObjectId, firebase_uid String, display_name String, instrument String, role ENUM bandleader/band_member, joined_at Date, country_code String). monitor_mixes (band_id ObjectId, show_id ObjectId, member_id ObjectId, channel_levels: [{channel String, level_db Float, is_muted Bool}], updated_at Date). All collections indexed on {band_id, _id}; BandScopedMongoRepository enforces band_id on every query.
target_output.api_design: setlist-service: PUT /setlists/{id}/songs (bandleader, update song order with version for optimistic concurrency), GET /setlists/{id} (all band members), WS /setlists/{id}/sync (WebSocket session affinity connection). chord-chart-service: GET /songs/{id}/chords (returns sections array), PUT /songs/{id}/chords (bandleader only), GET /songs/bulk-export (offline bundle for WatermelonDB sync). iem-service: POST /shows/{id}/iem/commands (body: channel, level_db, action ENUM set/mute/solo), GET /shows/{id}/iem/state (current mix snapshot). venue-service: GET /shows (band schedule list), POST /shows (create show), PUT /shows/{id} (update venue details). Auth: Firebase ID token in Authorization header; band_id extracted from token custom claims by BandScopedMongoRepository plugin on all endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth ID tokens validated on every request via Firebase Admin SDK verifyIdToken middleware. Custom claims (band_id, role) extracted and attached to request context. BandScopedMongoRepository Fastify plugin injects band_id as mandatory MongoDB query filter on every find, findOne, updateOne, deleteOne, and aggregate call - cross-band queries are structurally impossible. ESLint no-direct-mongo-collection rule prevents regression. Bandleader role enforced in route guards for setlist write and IEM command endpoints. GDPR: DELETE /bands/{id}/data cascade deletes all documents with matching band_id across all collections within 72h via async Cloud Run job. Personal data (band member display_name, country_code, firebase_uid) deleted per GDPR right-to-erasure request. TLS 1.3 on all Cloud Run endpoints via GCP managed certificates.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline per microservice: ESLint plus TypeScript check, Jest 80% coverage, Docker build, Artifact Registry push, Cloud Run deploy via gcloud run deploy. setlist-service deployed with --session-affinity to maintain WebSocket connection routing. iem-service deployed with --min-instances=1 to eliminate cold start latency. Cloud Run Terraform module documents min-instances rationale in code comments. Expo EAS Build for React Native: TestFlight and Google Play internal track on each main branch merge. Environments: dev (all services on Cloud Run, MongoDB Atlas M10), staging (M20, mirrors production), production (M30 multi-AZ, EU region). Secrets: GCP Secret Manager for MongoDB Atlas connection string and Firebase service account key.
target_output.observability: Firebase Crashlytics reports React Native crashes with session context (active screen, setlist song count, sync state). Prometheus metrics on each Cloud Run service via Fastify prometheus plugin. Grafana dashboards: setlist_sync_latency_p99_ms (alert above 500ms threshold), iem_command_latency_p99_ms (alert above 50ms), websocket_active_connections, sync_reconnection_rate, band_id_isolation_violation_count (should always be zero). PagerDuty alert on sync latency SLA breach or iem latency SLA breach during show hours (18:00-24:00 local).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloud Run scales to zero between shows; cost driven by show-day concurrency. 4 services at peak (touring season): ~$80/month. MongoDB Atlas M30: ~$540/month. Redis Memorystore Basic (5GB): ~$80/month. Firebase Auth: free tier covers typical band size. Expo EAS Build: free tier for open beta. Total ~$700/month steady state; reduces to ~$200/month off-season with M10 Atlas downscale.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP (8 weeks): Setlist sync with WatermelonDB offline mode, chord chart viewer with offline bundle sync, Firebase Auth with band_id custom claims, BandScopedMongoRepository plugin, all four Cloud Run microservices deployed, GitHub Actions CI plus Expo EAS Build. Phase 2 - IEM and Venue (4 weeks): IEM Mix Panel with Yamaha CL and Behringer X32 OSC integration, min-instances=1 for iem-service, venue schedule and logistics CRUD, show calendar view. Phase 3 - Hardening (2 weeks): GDPR deletion workflow, Grafana sync latency dashboards, PagerDuty show-hours alerting, offline WatermelonDB conflict resolution stress testing.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode across all microservices and React Native app. BandScopedMongoRepository is the only permitted MongoDB access pattern; ESLint rule enforces this. All Fastify route handlers typed with request generic carrying band_id from auth context. WatermelonDB schema changes require migration scripts - no silent schema drops. React Native WebSocket client implements exponential backoff reconnection (1s, 2s, 4s, max 30s) with WatermelonDB offline fallback activated after first failed reconnect attempt. 80% Jest coverage gate on all Cloud Run services.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Venue WiFi packet loss causes WebSocket ping/pong timeouts, triggering false offline mode during a show. Mitigation: 30-second WebSocket timeout before offline fallback activation; heartbeat ping every 10 seconds. Risk: Yamaha CL and Behringer X32 OSC protocol differences cause iem-service to send malformed commands to unrecognized venue mixer models. Mitigation: mixer_type field in show_schedules with protocol adapter pattern; unknown mixer type returns 422 with supported model list. Tradeoff: WatermelonDB adds ~2MB to React Native bundle size but is necessary for offline-first reliability guarantee. Tradeoff: min-instances=1 on iem-service costs ~$15/month idle but is required for sub-50ms soundcheck latency SLA.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Resolved ISS-001: WatermelonDB extended to setlist state with server-wins conflict resolution on reconnect - eliminates show-breaking offline gap. Resolved ISS-002: min-instances=1 and session affinity configured on Cloud Run deployments for iem-service and setlist-service respectively. Resolved ISS-003: BandScopedMongoRepository Fastify plugin with ESLint enforcement provides architectural band isolation guarantee. Confirmed version INT for optimistic concurrency on setlist documents over MongoDB transactions to avoid performance overhead on sync-critical path.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision for gigging musicians app; resolves offline resilience (ISS-001), IEM cold start (ISS-002), and band isolation middleware (ISS-003).

project_goal.value: Migrate a music streaming platform's audio transcoding and CDN delivery infrastructure from on-premise bare metal to a multi-region cloud architecture on AWS, achieving sub-500ms global audio start latency and zero-downtime cutover
project_goal.rationale: On-premise hardware approaching end-of-life; CDN migration unlocks global audience growth without capex investment

target_users.value: Internal DevOps and platform engineering teams operating the transcoding and delivery infrastructure; end-user streaming audience is indirect beneficiary
target_users.rationale: Infrastructure project with no direct end-user UI; engineering team is the operational consumer

project_class.rationale: Platform/environment architecture using Terraform and AWS services with no application code layer

capabilities.rationale: Terraform IaC, transcoding job orchestration, CDN routing, origin server integration

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-region AWS deployment, zero-downtime DNS cutover, parallel transcoding validation, streaming SLA requirements

risk_level.rationale: Live streaming platform with SLA obligations; infrastructure migration risks listener experience degradation and label contract violations

data_sensitivity.rationale: Transcoding job metadata and CDN configurations are internal; audio files are licensed content but not PII

external_exposure.rationale: CDN delivery endpoints serve public streaming audience globally

access_model.value: AWS IAM roles for ECS and Lambda service principals; Cognito for internal ops dashboard; Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB lock and restricted IAM
access_model.rationale: Infrastructure access controlled via IAM role assumption; no public management endpoints

feature_scope.value: Terraform modules for multi-region ECS Fargate transcoding clusters, CloudFront CDN distributions, S3 origin buckets with replication, MediaConvert integration, DNS cutover automation, migration validator comparing on-premise and cloud transcoding checksums
feature_scope.rationale: Full infrastructure migration scope from compute to delivery layer

mvp_scope.value: Single-region us-east-1 transcoding pipeline on ECS Fargate with CloudFront CDN and S3 origin; DNS traffic shifting starting at 5% of streaming audience
mvp_scope.rationale: Single-region MVP validates cloud transcoding quality before multi-region rollout

security_baseline.value: VPC with private subnets for ECS transcoding workers; WAF on CloudFront for DDoS protection; S3 bucket policies blocking public access; KMS encryption for audio assets at rest; CloudTrail for all AWS API calls
security_baseline.rationale: Label distribution agreements require content security; WAF protects CDN endpoints from abuse

frontend_stack.value: None - infrastructure project; internal ops dashboard is a Grafana instance
frontend_stack.rationale: No application frontend; Grafana dashboards for infrastructure monitoring

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin for transcoding job orchestration API and migration validator service
backend_stack.rationale: Go for low-overhead transcoding job dispatch and checksum comparison at high file volume

data_platform.value: DynamoDB for transcoding job state and format_profiles; Redis 7 for job queue and CDN cache invalidation tracking; S3 for audio asset storage
data_platform.rationale: DynamoDB for high-throughput job state; S3 for durable audio asset storage with replication

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate for transcoding workers; CloudFront with multi-origin failover; us-east-1 primary, eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1 secondary regions
hosting_target.rationale: ECS Fargate eliminates EC2 capacity management; CloudFront provides global PoP coverage for streaming latency

privacy_retention_policy.value: Transcoding job logs retained 90 days in CloudWatch; S3 audio assets retained per label licensing terms; CDN access logs retained 30 days for abuse investigation
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Label distribution agreements specify content retention obligations; CDN logs for DMCA compliance

future_scope.value: Per-listener adaptive bitrate profiles, AI-powered mastering normalization pre-transcode, edge compute for near-listener transcoding
future_scope.rationale: Phase 3 roadmap after multi-region migration completes

constraints.value: Zero-downtime cutover with rollback capability within 5 minutes; maintain 99.95% availability SLA during migration; audio file checksum validation required before any format profile is promoted to production
constraints.rationale: SLA defined in label licensing contracts; checksum validation prevents encoding regressions reaching listeners

observability_baseline.value: Datadog infrastructure monitoring with ECS and CloudFront metrics; custom transcoding_job_duration_seconds and cdn_cache_hit_rate dashboards; PagerDuty for SLA breach and migration validator failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Infrastructure migration requires real-time visibility into transcoding throughput and CDN performance during cutover

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure-as-code with Terraform modules; GitOps workflow; incremental traffic shifting via Route53 weighted routing during cutover
execution_preference.rationale: Terraform enables repeatable multi-region deployments; weighted routing allows risk-controlled traffic cutover

compliance_context.value: Content security requirements under label distribution agreements; infrastructure must meet 99.95% uptime SLA defined in licensing contracts; CloudTrail audit trail required for content access logging
compliance_context.rationale: Risk level high and external exposure public_internet triggers compliance context requirement

project.goal: Migrate on-premise audio transcoding and CDN delivery to multi-region AWS
project.target_users: DevOps and platform engineering teams

backend.framework: Gin for transcoding orchestration API and migration validator
backend.iac: Terraform modules for ECS, CloudFront, S3, Route53

data.cache: Redis 7 for job queue and CDN invalidation tracking

constraints.validation: checksum validation before format profile promotion

reasoner_reviews.product: The incremental traffic shifting approach via Route53 weighted routing is the correct zero-downtime strategy. Starting at 5% and increasing in 10% increments over 2 weeks gives the team recovery time if transcoding quality issues emerge.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The migration validator comparing on-premise and cloud transcoding checksums is the critical risk mitigation component. Round 1 plan lacked detail on how format_profiles are promoted and how the validator handles format variants like 128kbps vs 320kbps HLS segments independently. This round must address that.
reasoner_reviews.security: Content security requirements under label agreements mean S3 bucket policies must block all public access and rely exclusively on CloudFront OAI for content delivery. KMS encryption for audio assets at rest is mandatory. CloudTrail must capture all S3 GetObject calls during migration for content access audit.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 5-minute rollback via Route53 weighted routing back to on-premise origin is achievable; TTL on the weighted record must be set to 30 seconds during migration window. The migration validator should gate each traffic increment, blocking promotion if checksum mismatch rate exceeds 0.01%.

backend.service_design: TranscodingOrchestrator (Go/Gin): REST API accepting transcoding job submissions, dispatching to SQS queue, polling DynamoDB for job state. MigrationValidator (Go): compares SHA-256 checksums of on-premise and CloudFront-served audio segments per format_profile; emits pass/fail per profile per batch. FormatProfileService: CRUD for format_profiles (bitrate, codec, container, segment_duration for HLS) stored in DynamoDB
backend.api_patterns: REST: POST /jobs (submit transcoding job), GET /jobs/{id} (poll status), GET /format-profiles (list active profiles), POST /validator/run (trigger checksum comparison batch), GET /validator/report/{batch_id}. Internal SQS for job dispatch to ECS Fargate workers.
backend.failure_handling: ECS task failure: Fargate task restarts up to 3 times; on 3rd failure DynamoDB job status set to failed and PagerDuty alert fires. Migration validator checksum mismatch above 0.01% threshold: MigrationValidator sets promotion_blocked flag on format_profile in DynamoDB; Route53 weighted routing increment halted until resolved.
backend.scaling_notes: ECS Fargate auto-scaling on SQS queue depth via Application Auto Scaling; target 10 concurrent transcoding tasks per region; CloudFront handles CDN burst scaling automatically

security.auth_design: ECS task roles via IAM instance profile: S3 read for source audio, S3 write for transcoded output, MediaConvert submit, DynamoDB read/write for job state. No public IAM credentials.
security.secrets_management: MediaConvert endpoint and label DRM keys stored in Secrets Manager; ECS tasks fetch at container startup via SDK; rotated every 90 days.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail data events enabled on audio S3 buckets capturing GetObject, PutObject; forwarded to CloudWatch Logs with 90-day retention. S3 server access logs for CDN delivery compliance.

data.entities: transcoding_jobs (job_id STRING PK, audio_asset_id STRING, source_s3_key STRING, format_profile_id STRING, status ENUM queued/processing/complete/failed, output_s3_key STRING, checksum_sha256 STRING, duration_ms INT, created_at STRING, updated_at STRING). format_profiles (profile_id STRING PK, codec ENUM aac/mp3/opus, bitrate_kbps INT, container ENUM hls/dash/mp4, segment_duration_sec INT, active BOOL, promoted_at STRING). audio_assets (asset_id STRING PK, isrc STRING, title STRING, artist STRING, label_id STRING, source_s3_key STRING, duration_seconds REAL, ingested_at STRING). delivery_routes (route_id STRING PK, asset_id STRING, format_profile_id STRING, cloudfront_distribution_id STRING, origin_type ENUM on_premise/aws, traffic_weight INT, updated_at STRING). migration_batches (batch_id STRING PK, asset_count INT, checksum_pass_count INT, checksum_fail_count INT, mismatch_rate REAL, promotion_blocked BOOL, created_at STRING).
data.storage_design: DynamoDB single-table design for transcoding_jobs and migration_batches with GSI on audio_asset_id. S3 us-east-1 primary bucket with cross-region replication to eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1 for CDN origin proximity. Redis 7 on ElastiCache for SQS overflow queue and CDN invalidation batch tracking.

devops.deployment_topology: Terraform module hierarchy: vpc_module (VPC, subnets, security groups per region), ecs_module (Fargate cluster, task definitions, auto-scaling), cdn_module (CloudFront distribution, OAI, S3 origin, WAF), dns_module (Route53 weighted records for traffic shifting). State in S3 with DynamoDB lock and workspace per region.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: terraform validate, terraform plan in PR comment, terraform apply on merge to main for staging; production apply requires manual approval gate. Go unit tests for TranscodingOrchestrator and MigrationValidator with mock SQS and DynamoDB.
devops.rollback_strategy: Route53 weighted record cutover: Route53 weighted routing with 30s TTL during migration; rollback sets on-premise origin weight to 100 and CloudFront to 0 within 30 seconds. ECS rollback: previous task definition revision re-registered via GitHub Actions rollback workflow. Terraform rollback: revert git commit and apply previous state.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog Agent on ECS tasks for transcoding_job_duration_seconds histogram, transcoding_error_rate counter, queue_depth gauge. CloudFront access logs to S3 with Datadog S3 log forwarder for cache_hit_rate, origin_latency_p99 dashboards. PagerDuty for SLA breach and migration_validator promotion_blocked events.

ISS-001.title: Migration validator does not handle per-format-profile checksum validation independently
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan describes a single checksum comparison pass across all format_profiles in a batch, but a mismatch in the 128kbps HLS profile should not block the 320kbps AAC profile from promotion. Validator must report pass/fail per format_profile_id independently.

ISS-002.title: Route53 weighted routing TTL not specified during cutover window
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan references DNS cutover but does not specify the TTL value on the weighted record during migration. A high TTL (300s default) would mean 5-minute rollback is not achievable; TTL must be reduced to 30s before any traffic shifting begins.

focus_issues.title: Migration validator does not handle per-format-profile checksum validation independently
focus_issues.detail: Validator must report pass/fail per format_profile_id; promotion gate must be evaluated per-profile, not globally across all profiles in a batch.

focus_issues.title: Route53 weighted routing TTL not specified during cutover window
focus_issues.detail: TTL must be reduced to 30s on weighted records before traffic shifting begins to enable 5-minute rollback SLA.

latest_recommendations: Redesign MigrationValidator to run per format_profile_id and report promotion_blocked flag independently per profile in DynamoDB format_profiles table
Add pre-migration checklist step in runbook: reduce Route53 weighted record TTL to 30s at least 5 minutes before first traffic shift

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues from first audit; ISS-001 is high severity blocking production promotion safety

strengths: Terraform module hierarchy with per-region workspace is well-structured for multi-region rollout
ECS Fargate auto-scaling on SQS queue depth is the correct approach for variable transcoding load
S3 cross-region replication for CDN origin proximity addresses global latency requirement

DNS TTL value during cutover not specified; default 300s TTL prevents 5-minute rollback
blocking_issues: MigrationValidator must be redesigned for per-format-profile independent pass/fail reporting

Add runbook step: reduce Route53 weighted record TTL to 30s before any traffic shifting begins

previous_plan.title: Music Streaming Infrastructure Migration - Round 1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Terraform-based migration of on-premise audio transcoding and CDN delivery to AWS ECS Fargate and CloudFront. Round 1 established multi-region Terraform module structure and ECS auto-scaling design but left migration validator per-profile granularity and DNS TTL cutover details unresolved.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: ECS Fargate transcoding workers pull jobs from SQS, write transcoded audio to S3, and CloudFront serves content via OAI. Route53 weighted routing shifts traffic incrementally from on-premise origin to CloudFront.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: Terraform modules for vpc, ecs, cdn, dns. GitHub Actions CI with terraform plan in PRs and manual production apply gate. Route53 weighted routing for incremental traffic shifting.
previous_plan.workflows: Transcoding job submission via POST /jobs API; SQS dispatch to ECS Fargate workers; migration validator compares checksums between on-premise and cloud output before promotion.

best_plan.title: Music Streaming Infrastructure Migration - Round 1 Architecture
best_plan.executive_summary: Terraform-based migration of on-premise audio transcoding and CDN delivery to AWS ECS Fargate and CloudFront. Multi-region module structure and ECS auto-scaling established; per-profile validator and DNS TTL details pending.
best_plan.architecture_overview: ECS Fargate transcoding workers with SQS dispatch, S3 output storage, CloudFront CDN with OAI, Route53 weighted routing for incremental traffic cutover.
best_plan.deployment_and_operations: Terraform modules with per-region workspace. GitHub Actions with manual production gate. Weighted routing rollback procedure.
best_plan.workflows: Transcoding submission API, SQS-driven worker dispatch, checksum validation before format profile promotion.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 resolves both issues: ISS-001 requires redesigning MigrationValidator to run per format_profile_id and update the promotion_blocked flag independently in DynamoDB format_profiles, rather than aggregating across a whole batch. ISS-002 requires specifying a 30s TTL reduction step in the cutover runbook. Both fixes are surgical and do not change the core Terraform or ECS architecture from Round 1.

fix_report.action_taken: Redesigned MigrationValidator to run per format_profile_id: for each active format_profile, the validator samples 1000 audio_asset checksums, computes mismatch_rate = failed/total, and sets promotion_blocked = true in DynamoDB format_profiles if mismatch_rate exceeds 0.01%. The migration_batches table now stores per-profile results keyed by batch_id+format_profile_id composite key. Route53 traffic increment gate checks promotion_blocked on all active format_profiles before allowing the next increment.
fix_report.expected_outcome: A checksum regression in the 128kbps HLS profile blocks only that profile from promotion while allowing 320kbps AAC to proceed independently; ISS-001 resolved

fix_report.action_taken: Added pre-migration TTL reduction step to the cutover runbook: T-10min: terraform apply dns_module with ttl=30 on weighted records; T-5min: verify TTL propagation via dig +short; T-0: begin traffic shifting. Documented in deployment_and_operations as a mandatory pre-condition. Terraform dns_module parameterized with cutover_ttl_seconds variable (default 300, set to 30 during migration).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Route53 weighted record TTL is 30s during entire migration window, enabling 5-minute rollback by resetting on-premise origin weight to 100; ISS-002 resolved

target_output.title: Music Streaming Infrastructure Migration - Round 2 Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Terraform-driven migration of a music streaming platform's audio transcoding and CDN delivery from on-premise bare metal to multi-region AWS, using ECS Fargate transcoding workers, CloudFront CDN with S3 origins, and Route53 weighted routing for zero-downtime incremental traffic cutover. Round 2 resolves the migration validator per-format-profile promotion gate and DNS TTL cutover specification, completing a production-safe migration architecture for 99.95% SLA compliance.
target_output.architecture_overview: On-premise transcoding workers and CDN origin are replaced incrementally with AWS ECS Fargate transcoding clusters and CloudFront distributions backed by S3 origins with cross-region replication. Route53 weighted routing shifts streaming traffic from on-premise origin servers to CloudFront in 10% increments, gated by the MigrationValidator which independently validates per-format-profile checksum accuracy before allowing each increment. Terraform manages the full infrastructure lifecycle across us-east-1 (primary), eu-west-1, and ap-southeast-1 via per-region workspace modules. The TranscodingOrchestrator Go service dispatches jobs via SQS to ECS Fargate workers, storing job state in DynamoDB. Audio assets are stored in S3 with KMS encryption and cross-region replication, served via CloudFront OAI.
target_output.technology_stack: IaC: Terraform 1.8 with modular workspace-per-region design. Transcoding Orchestration: Go 1.22, Gin, AWS SDK v2 for SQS/DynamoDB/MediaConvert. CDN: CloudFront with S3 OAI origins, WAF web ACL. Storage: S3 with SSE-KMS and cross-region replication to eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1. Job State: DynamoDB single-table with GSIs. Cache: Redis 7 on ElastiCache for SQS overflow. DNS: Route53 weighted routing with 30s TTL during cutover. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Terraform plan/apply and manual production gate. Monitoring: Datadog, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Transcoding Pipeline: ECS Fargate workers consume SQS jobs, invoke MediaConvert for each format_profile (128kbps HLS, 320kbps AAC, 256kbps Opus, 1080p MP4), write output to S3 origin bucket with DynamoDB job state update. Migration Validator: per-format-profile SHA-256 checksum comparison between on-premise encoded segments and CloudFront-served segments; promotion_blocked flag per profile in DynamoDB. CDN Delivery: CloudFront distributions in us-east-1/eu-west-1/ap-southeast-1 with S3 OAI; format_profiles mapped to CloudFront behaviors by path pattern. Traffic Cutover Automation: Route53 weighted routing increments (5%, 15%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%) gated by MigrationValidator promotion_blocked check across all active format_profiles.
target_output.system_components: Terraform modules: vpc_module (VPC, private subnets, NAT gateway, security groups per region), ecs_module (Fargate cluster, task definitions with MediaConvert task role, SQS consumer, Application Auto Scaling on queue depth), cdn_module (CloudFront distribution, OAI, WAF web ACL, S3 origin with replication), dns_module (Route53 weighted records with cutover_ttl_seconds variable). TranscodingOrchestrator (Go/Gin :8080): POST /jobs, GET /jobs/{id}, GET /format-profiles. MigrationValidator (Go service): per-format-profile batch comparison job run on schedule or on-demand, updates DynamoDB format_profiles.promotion_blocked independently per profile. DynamoDB single-table: transcoding_jobs, format_profiles, delivery_routes, migration_batches (composite PK batch_id+format_profile_id).
target_output.workflows: Transcoding Job Submission: client calls POST /jobs with audio_asset_id and target format_profile_ids; TranscodingOrchestrator writes DynamoDB job record with status queued and publishes to SQS; ECS Fargate worker picks up message, retrieves source audio from S3, invokes MediaConvert job for each format_profile, writes output to S3 with checksum, updates DynamoDB status to complete. Migration Validation and Traffic Increment: ops engineer triggers POST /validator/run with batch_size=1000; MigrationValidator samples 1000 audio_assets per format_profile, downloads segments from both on-premise origin and CloudFront edge, compares SHA-256 checksums; sets promotion_blocked=false in DynamoDB format_profiles where mismatch_rate is below 0.01%; ops reviews GET /validator/report/{batch_id} per-profile results; if all active profiles unblocked, Terraform dns_module apply increases CloudFront weight by 10 percent. DNS Cutover Pre-check (ISS-002 fix): T-10min: terraform apply -var=cutover_ttl_seconds=30 for dns_module; T-5min: ops verifies dig result showing 30s TTL; T-0: first Route53 weight increment to 5 percent CloudFront. Rollback: set on-premise origin weight to 100 in dns_module variables and apply; Route53 propagates within 30s.
target_output.data_model: transcoding_jobs (job_id STRING PK, audio_asset_id STRING GSI, source_s3_key STRING, format_profile_id STRING GSI, status ENUM queued/processing/complete/failed, output_s3_key STRING, checksum_sha256 STRING, duration_ms INT, created_at STRING). format_profiles (profile_id STRING PK, codec ENUM aac/mp3/opus, bitrate_kbps INT, container ENUM hls/dash/mp4, segment_duration_sec INT, active BOOL, promoted_at STRING, promotion_blocked BOOL, last_validator_batch_id STRING). audio_assets (asset_id STRING PK, isrc STRING, title STRING, artist STRING, label_id STRING, source_s3_key STRING, duration_seconds REAL, ingested_at STRING). delivery_routes (route_id STRING PK, asset_id STRING, format_profile_id STRING, cloudfront_distribution_id STRING, origin_type ENUM on_premise/aws, traffic_weight INT, updated_at STRING). migration_batches (PK batch_id+format_profile_id, batch_id STRING, format_profile_id STRING, asset_count INT, checksum_pass_count INT, checksum_fail_count INT, mismatch_rate REAL, promotion_blocked BOOL, created_at STRING).
target_output.api_design: TranscodingOrchestrator REST (Go/Gin): POST /jobs {audio_asset_id, format_profile_ids[]} -> {job_id, status:queued} | GET /jobs/{id} -> {status, output_s3_key, checksum_sha256, duration_ms} | GET /format-profiles -> [{profile_id, codec, bitrate_kbps, active, promotion_blocked}] | PATCH /format-profiles/{id} {active:bool}. MigrationValidator REST: POST /validator/run {batch_size:1000, format_profile_ids[]} -> {batch_id} | GET /validator/report/{batch_id} -> [{format_profile_id, asset_count, mismatch_rate, promotion_blocked}]. Internal SQS queue for ECS worker dispatch. All management endpoints require IAM SigV4 signed requests; no public access.
target_output.security_and_compliance: IAM task roles for ECS containers: S3 read on source bucket, S3 write on transcoded output bucket, MediaConvert submit, DynamoDB read/write on job state table; no wildcard permissions. S3 bucket policy blocks all public access; CloudFront OAI is the only allowed principal for GetObject. KMS encryption (SSE-KMS) on all audio S3 buckets with CloudTrail data events capturing GetObject and PutObject for label content access audit. WAF web ACL on CloudFront with AWS managed rule group for DDoS mitigation and rate limit 10000 req/min per IP. Secrets Manager for MediaConvert endpoint and label DRM keys; ECS task fetches at startup; rotated every 90 days. CloudTrail organization trail with 90-day CloudWatch Logs retention for all AWS API calls.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform module hierarchy with per-region workspace (workspaces: us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1). GitHub Actions: terraform validate and plan on PR with plan diff in comment; terraform apply on merge to main for staging environment; production apply requires manual approval from platform-lead team. Pre-migration cutover checklist: T-10min apply dns_module with cutover_ttl_seconds=30; T-5min verify TTL propagation via dig; T-0 begin Route53 weight increments (5, 15, 25, 50, 75, 100 percent) with MigrationValidator gate at each step. Rollback: dns_module variable on_premise_weight=100 and cloudfront_weight=0 applied within 30s propagation. ECS rollback: previous task definition revision via GitHub Actions rollback workflow. Terraform state: S3 backend with DynamoDB lock table; workspace isolation prevents cross-region state collision.
target_output.observability: Datadog Agent ECS sidecar on transcoding workers: transcoding_job_duration_seconds histogram by format_profile_id, transcoding_error_rate counter, sqs_queue_depth gauge. CloudFront access logs to S3 with Datadog S3 log forwarder: cdn_cache_hit_rate, origin_latency_p99, requests_per_second by region. MigrationValidator metrics: migration_validator_mismatch_rate gauge by format_profile_id, promotion_blocked_profiles gauge. PagerDuty escalation: transcoding_error_rate above 1 percent, SQS queue depth above 500, migration_validator promotion_blocked event, CloudFront origin error rate above 0.1 percent. Grafana dashboard: traffic weight by origin, per-region CDN latency, transcoding throughput per format_profile.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $2800/month at steady state: ECS Fargate transcoding $1200 (variable with transcoding volume), CloudFront CDN $800 (at 10TB/month delivery), S3 storage and replication $400, DynamoDB $100, ElastiCache Redis $150, other services $150. ECS auto-scaling on SQS queue depth with target 10 concurrent tasks; scales to 50 tasks during catalog migration bursts. CloudFront scales automatically with streaming audience. Reserved ECS capacity for baseline transcoding throughput reduces cost by 25 percent versus on-demand.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (6 weeks): Terraform modules for us-east-1 VPC, ECS Fargate cluster, S3 origin bucket, CloudFront distribution. TranscodingOrchestrator API and MigrationValidator with per-format-profile reporting. GitHub Actions CI pipeline with staging environment. DNS cutover pre-check runbook with 30s TTL step. Phase 2 (4 weeks): MigrationValidator gate integrated into Route53 weight increment workflow; begin traffic shifting at 5 percent for 500 random audio assets; monitor mismatch_rate per format_profile for 48 hours before next increment. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Scale to 100 percent CloudFront traffic in us-east-1; decommission us-east-1 on-premise origin servers. Deploy eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1 Terraform modules with S3 replication; Route53 latency-based routing for multi-region CDN. Phase 4 (2 weeks): Full multi-region cutover; on-premise hardware decommission; cost reconciliation and reserved capacity purchase.
target_output.development_guidelines: Terraform: all modules have variable validation blocks; no hard-coded ARNs or region strings; workspace isolation enforced via backend config. Go: golangci-lint and go test in GitHub Actions; unit tests for MigrationValidator checksum comparison logic with mock S3 client; table-driven tests for format_profile promotion gate. Terraform plan output required in PR review for any infrastructure change. ADRs in /docs/adr/ for module design decisions. Runbook for each migration phase stored in /docs/runbooks/ and reviewed before execution.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: On-premise origin servers fail during migration window before CloudFront reaches 100 percent traffic. Mitigation: keep on-premise origin fully operational until CloudFront weight reaches 100 percent and 48-hour stability window passes; PagerDuty alerts on on-premise origin health. Risk: S3 cross-region replication lag causes eu-west-1 CloudFront to serve stale audio during high-upload periods. Mitigation: CloudFront origin failover with primary us-east-1 S3 and secondary eu-west-1 S3 until replication lag is under 5 minutes. Tradeoff: DynamoDB over RDS for transcoding job state - DynamoDB scales to 10000 TPS for job state reads without provisioning but requires careful key design for GSI query patterns. Tradeoff: per-format-profile migration validator over monolithic batch - adds validator complexity but is essential for safe independent profile promotion.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected CloudFront OAI over signed URLs for audio delivery; OAI provides continuous access control without per-request signing overhead at streaming scale. Confirmed DynamoDB single-table design over separate tables per entity for cost efficiency at high transcoding job throughput. Chose SQS over Kafka for transcoding job queue since ordering is not required and SQS visibility timeout handles ECS task failure retry natively. Route53 weighted routing chosen over ALB weighted target groups since origin switching involves separate DNS endpoints for on-premise and CloudFront.

metadata.notes: Round 2 infrastructure migration resolving per-format-profile migration validator and DNS TTL cutover gap; Terraform multi-region with Route53 weighted traffic shifting.
