project.goal: Two-sided audio sample pack marketplace with creator upload and buyer checkout
project.target_users: Sample pack creators and music producers

frontend.state: Svelte stores for cart and auth
frontend.ssr: SvelteKit SSR for pack listing SEO

backend.processing: Bull queue with Redis for waveform generation workers

security.auth: Auth0 with creator/buyer roles

data.storage: S3 for WAV packs and waveform previews

constraints.preview_security: streaming previews serve compressed clips, not raw WAV

reasoner_reviews.product: The waveform preview player is the primary conversion driver for buyers - creators who upload high-quality waveform previews see higher conversion rates. The 5-minute processing SLA for a 500MB pack is tight; a clear upload progress UI and email notification when processing completes will reduce creator support tickets. The two-sided marketplace flywheel requires enough creator content at launch to give buyers a reason to browse.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Elastic Beanstalk is adequate for initial scale but the waveform processing worker should run separately from the web tier - heavy ffmpeg processing on the same EC2 instance as the Node API would starve API response times. A separate Bull worker Elastic Beanstalk worker tier consuming from Redis is the right split. S3 multipart upload is required for WAV packs above 100MB; the client should use the AWS SDK multipart upload API directly to S3 rather than routing through the Express API.
reasoner_reviews.security: S3 presigned download URLs with 24h TTL prevent buyers from sharing permanent download links. Stripe Checkout completely delegates PCI scope; no card data ever touches the Express API. The waveform processing worker should not have internet egress except to S3; an EC2 security group restricting outbound traffic prevents exfiltration of audio files.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 5-minute waveform SLA for 500MB requires roughly 100MB/min ffmpeg throughput on the worker instance. A c5.xlarge worker should meet this. Redis Bull queue provides retry-on-failure for waveform jobs without losing creator uploads.

frontend.app_structure: SvelteKit app with route groups: (marketplace) for public pack listing and search, (auth) for login/signup redirect, (creator) for upload dashboard and pack management, (buyer) for cart, checkout, and purchase history
frontend.pages_and_flows: Pack Listing page (SSR, SEO-optimized, waveform player widget, Buy button), Pack Detail (individual samples list with 30-second preview clips), Creator Upload Wizard (drag-drop WAV pack, metadata form, pricing, tag selection), Cart and Stripe Checkout (redirect to Stripe Checkout hosted page), Buyer Downloads (purchase history with S3 download links), Creator Dashboard (pack revenue, download counts)
frontend.state_management: Svelte writable store for cart (client-side persistence via localStorage). Auth0 SDK for login state. SSR load functions for pack listing data (PostgreSQL via Express API).
frontend.frontend_risks: Large WAV file upload UX is poor if routed through Express API; direct S3 multipart upload from client reduces latency and Express memory pressure.

backend.service_design: Express REST API for pack CRUD, search, auth validation, Stripe webhook handling. Separate Bull worker process consuming waveform_generation queue from Redis. S3 upload presigned URL endpoint generates client-side upload URLs.
backend.api_patterns: GET /packs (search with tag/BPM/key filters), GET /packs/{id}, POST /packs (creator upload initiation, returns S3 presigned upload URL), POST /packs/{id}/publish (after upload confirmation), POST /checkout (create Stripe Checkout session), POST /webhooks/stripe (payment confirmation), GET /purchases/{id}/download (generate S3 presigned download URL)
backend.background_jobs: Bull queue: waveform_generation job triggered after S3 upload confirmation. Worker reads WAV from S3, runs ffmpeg to generate compressed preview MP3 (30s clip), runs waveform-data to produce JSON peak data, writes both to S3, updates pack status to published in PostgreSQL.
backend.backend_risks: ffmpeg processing on shared Elastic Beanstalk instance could saturate CPU and degrade API response times; worker tier isolation required.

security.auth_design: Auth0 single-page app SDK for SvelteKit; JWT validated on Express API via express-oauth2-jwt-bearer middleware. Creator role required for pack upload endpoints; buyer role required for checkout.
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting via express-rate-limit: 10 uploads per hour per creator, 100 API requests per minute per user. S3 upload presigned URLs scoped to creator_id path prefix.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR delete endpoint: DELETE /users/{id}/data removes buyer PostgreSQL records and Stripe customer. S3 audio files retained per creator ownership unless creator requests deletion.

data.entities: sample_packs (id UUID PK, creator_id FK, title VARCHAR, description TEXT, bpm INT, key VARCHAR, genre_tags TEXT[], price_cents INT, status ENUM processing/published/unpublished, s3_pack_key TEXT, s3_preview_key TEXT, waveform_json_key TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audio_samples (id UUID PK, pack_id FK, title VARCHAR, s3_key TEXT, duration_seconds NUMERIC, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). creators (id UUID PK, auth0_sub VARCHAR, display_name VARCHAR, stripe_account_id VARCHAR, payout_email VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). purchases (id UUID PK, buyer_id FK, pack_id FK, stripe_payment_intent_id VARCHAR, amount_cents INT, purchased_at TIMESTAMPTZ). licenses (id UUID PK, purchase_id FK, buyer_id FK, pack_id FK, license_type ENUM standard/commercial, granted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). waveform_previews (id UUID PK, pack_id FK, s3_waveform_key TEXT, s3_preview_mp3_key TEXT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS db.t3.medium for transactional data. S3 bucket: sample-packs-raw (WAV uploads, creator-only presigned URL access), sample-packs-public (waveform JSON and preview MP3, CloudFront served). Redis 7 ElastiCache for Bull job queue and rate limit counters.
data.analytics_events: pack_viewed (pack_id, user_id), pack_preview_played (pack_id, duration_seconds), pack_purchased (pack_id, amount_cents), upload_completed (pack_id, file_size_mb, processing_time_seconds)

devops.deployment_topology: Elastic Beanstalk web tier (t3.medium, Node.js Express API plus SvelteKit SSR). Elastic Beanstalk worker tier (c5.xlarge, Bull waveform processing worker, Redis queue consumer). RDS PostgreSQL db.t3.medium. Redis ElastiCache cache.t3.micro.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint, TypeScript check, Jest (70% coverage), build SvelteKit static assets, deploy to EB staging on PR merge, manual approval for production. Separate EB worker tier deployment on merge to main.
devops.observability_stack: AWS CloudWatch for Elastic Beanstalk health, Express API error rate, and Bull worker job failure metrics. Basic SNS alert on waveform_job_failed events.

target_output.thinking_summary: Two-sided audio marketplace with the key complexity being the async waveform generation pipeline and Stripe payment integration. Critical architectural decision: Bull worker tier must be isolated from the Express web tier to prevent ffmpeg CPU saturation. S3 direct multipart upload from client avoids routing large WAV files through Express. Stripe Checkout delegates all PCI scope. Weak quality: some sections are thinner than ideal given this is a first pass for a two-person team MVP.
target_output.title: Audio Sample Pack Marketplace - Creator Upload and Buyer Checkout Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A SvelteKit and Node.js fullstack marketplace for audio sample pack creators to upload, process, and sell high-resolution WAV packs. Buyers browse waveform previews, purchase via Stripe Checkout, and download via time-limited S3 URLs. Async waveform generation runs in an isolated Elastic Beanstalk worker tier consuming a Redis Bull queue. Auth0 handles creator and buyer authentication.
target_output.architecture_overview: SvelteKit SSR frontend served from Elastic Beanstalk web tier alongside the Express REST API. Creator WAV pack uploads go directly to S3 via presigned multipart URLs, bypassing the Express API to avoid memory pressure. Upload completion triggers a Bull job on the Redis queue; a separate Elastic Beanstalk c5.xlarge worker tier consumes the job, runs ffmpeg to generate a 30-second MP3 preview and waveform-data JSON peak file, writes both to the public S3 bucket, and updates pack status to published in PostgreSQL. Buyers browse SSR-rendered pack listing pages (SEO-optimized), add packs to a Svelte store cart, and check out via Stripe Checkout hosted page. Stripe webhook confirms payment, creates purchase and license records in PostgreSQL, and makes the S3 presigned download URL available.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: SvelteKit 2, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Svelte stores, Auth0 SPA SDK. Backend: Node.js 22, Express 4, express-oauth2-jwt-bearer, Bull, fluent-ffmpeg, waveform-data. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS. Queue: Redis 7 ElastiCache (Bull). Storage: S3 (raw WAV and public preview buckets). CDN: CloudFront for preview MP3 and waveform JSON. Auth: Auth0. Payments: Stripe Checkout. Hosting: Elastic Beanstalk web tier and worker tier. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: AWS CloudWatch.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Creator Upload: drag-drop WAV pack upload with S3 direct multipart, metadata form (title, BPM, key, genre tags, price), publish trigger. Waveform Processing: async Bull job generates 30s MP3 preview and JSON waveform peaks from uploaded WAV. Pack Discovery: SSR SvelteKit listing with tag, BPM, and genre filters; waveform player widget on each pack card. Buyer Checkout: cart via Svelte localStorage store, Stripe Checkout session creation, webhook-confirmed purchase record. Download Management: S3 presigned download URL (24h TTL) generated on authenticated GET /purchases/{id}/download. Creator Dashboard: revenue totals and pack download counts from PostgreSQL aggregates.
target_output.system_components: Elastic Beanstalk web tier (Node.js, Express API plus SvelteKit SSR adapter). Elastic Beanstalk worker tier (Node.js, Bull worker consuming Redis queue, c5.xlarge for ffmpeg processing). PostgreSQL 16 RDS db.t3.medium. Redis 7 ElastiCache cache.t3.micro. S3 sample-packs-raw bucket (presigned creator uploads). S3 sample-packs-public bucket (preview MP3 and waveform JSON, CloudFront origin). CloudFront distribution for public audio assets. Auth0 tenant. Stripe account (Checkout and webhooks).
target_output.workflows: Creator Upload: Creator authenticates via Auth0, opens Upload Wizard, selects WAV pack. Frontend requests POST /packs from Express which creates sample_packs row (status=processing) and returns S3 multipart presigned URL. Frontend uploads WAV directly to S3 raw bucket. On S3 upload completion, frontend POSTs /packs/{id}/confirm. Express enqueues waveform_generation Bull job. Worker tier dequeues job, reads WAV from S3, runs ffmpeg (30s clip extraction, MP3 at 192kbps), runs waveform-data (1000-point peak array), writes preview MP3 and waveform JSON to public S3 bucket via CloudFront, updates pack status to published. Creator receives email notification. Buyer Purchase: Buyer browses pack listing (SSR), clicks Buy, auth flow if not logged in. POST /checkout creates Stripe Checkout session with pack price. Browser redirects to Stripe hosted page. Buyer completes payment. Stripe sends webhook POST /webhooks/stripe. Express validates signature, creates purchases row and licenses row, stores stripe_payment_intent_id. GET /purchases/{id}/download returns S3 presigned URL (24h TTL) for WAV pack download.
target_output.data_model: sample_packs (id UUID PK, creator_id UUID FK, title VARCHAR, description TEXT, bpm INT, key VARCHAR, genre_tags TEXT[], price_cents INT, status ENUM processing/published/unpublished, s3_pack_key TEXT, s3_preview_mp3_key TEXT, waveform_json_key TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audio_samples (id UUID PK, pack_id UUID FK, title VARCHAR, s3_key TEXT, duration_seconds NUMERIC, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). creators (id UUID PK, auth0_sub VARCHAR UNIQUE, display_name VARCHAR, stripe_account_id VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). purchases (id UUID PK, buyer_id UUID FK, pack_id UUID FK, stripe_payment_intent_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, amount_cents INT, purchased_at TIMESTAMPTZ). licenses (id UUID PK, purchase_id UUID FK, buyer_id UUID FK, pack_id UUID FK, license_type ENUM standard/commercial, granted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). waveform_jobs (id UUID PK, pack_id UUID FK, bull_job_id VARCHAR, status ENUM queued/processing/completed/failed, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: REST. POST /packs (creator, initiate upload, returns S3 presigned URL and pack_id). POST /packs/{id}/confirm (creator, S3 upload done, triggers Bull job). GET /packs (public search with tag, bpm_min, bpm_max, genre query params). GET /packs/{id} (public pack detail with waveform JSON URL). POST /checkout (buyer, create Stripe Checkout session, returns sessionUrl). POST /webhooks/stripe (Stripe webhook, validates signature, creates purchase and license). GET /purchases (buyer, list own purchases). GET /purchases/{id}/download (buyer, returns S3 presigned download URL with 24h TTL). GET /creators/{id}/dashboard (creator, revenue and download aggregate stats). Auth: Auth0 JWT via express-oauth2-jwt-bearer on all non-public endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 JWT validated on all authenticated Express endpoints. Creator role check on pack upload and dashboard endpoints. S3 raw bucket: private, creator upload via presigned URL scoped to creator_id/{pack_id} key prefix, no public access. S3 public bucket: read-only CloudFront access for preview MP3 and waveform JSON; full WAV packs never served from public bucket. Stripe Checkout: PCI handled entirely by Stripe; no card data stored. Stripe webhook signature validated via stripe.webhooks.constructEvent before processing. S3 download presigned URLs: 24h TTL, authenticated buyer endpoint only. GDPR: DELETE /users/{id}/data removes buyer PostgreSQL records; Stripe customer deleted via Stripe API. TLS 1.3 enforced via Elastic Beanstalk HTTPS listener.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions: ESLint, TypeScript type check, Jest 70% coverage, SvelteKit build, Docker image push to ECR, Elastic Beanstalk web tier deploy to staging on PR merge. Separate EB worker tier deploy on merge to main. Manual approval gate for production promotion. Elastic Beanstalk environments: staging (t3.small web + t3.medium worker), production (t3.medium web + c5.xlarge worker). RDS: db.t3.medium with automated backups. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for Auth0 credentials, Stripe secret key, PostgreSQL connection string.
target_output.observability: AWS CloudWatch metrics: Elastic Beanstalk HealthStatus, Express API 5xx rate, Bull waveform_job_failed count. CloudWatch alarms: waveform job failure rate above 5%, API 5xx rate above 2%. SNS notification on alarm to engineering email. Basic pack_purchased event logging to CloudWatch for revenue tracking. Firebase Crashlytics not applicable (web app); browser errors surfaced via SvelteKit error hooks to CloudWatch Logs.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Elastic Beanstalk web tier t3.medium: ~$35/month. Worker tier c5.xlarge (on only during active uploads, scale to 0 off-peak): ~$50/month peak. RDS db.t3.medium: ~$55/month. Redis cache.t3.micro: ~$20/month. S3 storage (100GB audio): ~$25/month. CloudFront (preview delivery): ~$15/month. Auth0 free tier: $0. Stripe: 2.9% + $0.30 per transaction. Estimated infrastructure total ~$200/month at launch scale.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Upload and Processing (6 weeks): Creator Auth0 login, S3 direct multipart upload, Bull waveform generation worker, pack listing page (SSR), waveform player widget, PostgreSQL schema, CI/CD pipeline. Phase 2 - Marketplace and Checkout (4 weeks): Buyer Auth0 login, Stripe Checkout integration, purchase webhook, S3 presigned download URL, buyer purchase history, cart Svelte store. Phase 3 - Creator Dashboard (3 weeks): Revenue aggregation API, download count metrics, CloudWatch alarms, GDPR deletion endpoint, pack analytics events.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode for SvelteKit and Express. Express route handlers typed with Request generics for Auth0 user context. Bull worker typed with job data interface. All monetary values stored as integer cents; no floating point arithmetic. ESLint with no-floating-point-money custom rule for price fields. Stripe webhook handler unit-tested with test fixture payloads. S3 presigned URL generation isolated in a service class for testability.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ffmpeg waveform generation for large packs (500MB) exceeds 5-minute SLA on t3.medium worker. Mitigation: worker tier uses c5.xlarge; alert on job duration above 240 seconds to detect regression. Risk: Bull Redis queue loss on ElastiCache restart drops in-flight waveform jobs. Mitigation: waveform_jobs PostgreSQL table tracks job status; orphaned processing jobs older than 10 minutes re-queued by cron health check. Tradeoff: Elastic Beanstalk over ECS for lower ops overhead at launch scale - acceptable given two-person team, with migration path to ECS when autoscaling needs increase.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose S3 direct multipart upload over proxying through Express to avoid memory exhaustion on large WAV files. Confirmed Stripe Checkout over custom payment form to eliminate PCI scope. Selected Bull over SQS for waveform queue to avoid AWS SQS polling cost and latency at small scale. Confirmed CloudFront for public preview delivery to reduce S3 egress cost and improve global preview load times.

metadata.notes: First-pass audio sample pack marketplace; weak quality with some thinner sections; Stripe Checkout, S3 direct upload, Bull waveform processing queue.

project_goal.value: Automate the migration of a radio broadcast station's legacy playout scheduling system to a modern cloud-based scheduler, converting historical schedule data and validating broadcast compliance rules during transfer
project_goal.rationale: Legacy system on end-of-life hardware; cloud migration enables remote schedule editing and disaster recovery

target_users.value: Broadcast operations engineers and scheduling staff at the radio station managing playout schedules and compliance validation
target_users.rationale: Internal operations users identified in discovery

project_class.rationale: CLI-driven batch migration tool with no persistent service or UI layer

capabilities.rationale: XML export parsing, database migration, compliance rule validation, batch processing of schedule records

complexity_level.rationale: Moderate data transformation complexity with compliance validation logic and transactional batch imports

risk_level.rationale: Internal schedule data migration; risk of broadcast compliance violations if format clock rules are incorrectly migrated

data_sensitivity.rationale: Broadcast schedule data is internal operational data; not PII or financial

external_exposure.rationale: CLI tool runs in the broadcast operations environment; no external network exposure

access_model.value: CLI tool executed by broadcast engineers with MySQL write access; no authentication layer in the tool itself
access_model.rationale: Internal tool run by authorized operations staff; access controlled by OS-level permissions

feature_scope.value: Legacy XML schedule export parsing, slot-to-schema mapping, broadcast format clock rule validation, transactional batch import to target MySQL database, slot count and duration reconciliation report, discrepancy CSV export
feature_scope.rationale: Full migration lifecycle from source export to validated target import

mvp_scope.value: Full schedule migration for a single broadcast station with format clock validation and reconciliation report; multi-station batch mode in Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Single-station MVP validates migration correctness before scaling to full station network

security_baseline.value: MySQL connection via SSL; credentials in environment variables; migration logs to local file; no network exposure during migration run
security_baseline.rationale: Internal tool with no public exposure; SSL for DB connection and env-var credentials are sufficient

frontend_stack.value: None - CLI tool with Click command interface and Rich terminal output for migration progress
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI is the appropriate interface for a batch migration tool used by technical broadcast engineers

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Click for CLI, lxml for XML parsing, SQLAlchemy 2.x for MySQL writes
backend_stack.rationale: Python ecosystem well-suited for data transformation; Click for CLI; lxml for legacy XML parsing

data_platform.value: MySQL 8 as target scheduler database; legacy system provides XML file exports as migration source
data_platform.rationale: Target cloud scheduler uses MySQL 8; no cache layer needed for batch CLI migration

hosting_target.value: Azure Container Instances for scheduled migration runs; tool also distributable as standalone Python package for local execution
hosting_target.rationale: Azure Container Instances for scheduled batch runs; standalone package for one-off engineer-driven migrations

privacy_retention_policy.value: Migration run logs retained 30 days; reconciliation reports retained 1 year for broadcast regulatory audit compliance
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Broadcast regulatory compliance requires evidence of schedule migration accuracy for license audits

future_scope.value: Multi-station batch migration mode, live schedule sync adapter for ongoing playout changes, automated compliance rule regression testing
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 expands to full station network after single-station MVP validation

constraints.value: Migration must complete within a 4-hour maintenance window; must not alter source XML files during migration; all import failures must be captured in discrepancy report with original slot data preserved
constraints.rationale: 4-hour window defined by broadcast scheduling blackout period; source preservation required for re-run capability

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor for Container Instances run logs; migration progress logged to Azure Blob Storage; discrepancy report emailed to broadcast operations team on completion
observability_baseline.rationale: Broadcast operations team needs completion status and discrepancy counts within 30 minutes of migration run

execution_preference.value: Single engineer implementation; Azure DevOps CI pipeline; weekly dry-run in staging environment before production migration window
execution_preference.rationale: Lean single-engineer project scope; dry-run cadence validates no regressions before live migration

compliance_context.value: Broadcast regulatory compliance (FCC or Ofcom depending on jurisdiction) applies to schedule data handling; format clock rules must be preserved exactly to avoid license violations
compliance_context.rationale: Risk level medium and broadcast regulatory obligation triggers compliance context

project.goal: Migrate legacy XML playout schedules to cloud MySQL scheduler with format clock compliance validation
project.target_users: Broadcast engineers and scheduling staff
project.timeline: 6-week implementation with weekly dry-runs before production window

backend.framework: Click CLI, lxml for XML, SQLAlchemy 2.x for MySQL
backend.validation: format clock rule engine comparing source and target slot duration totals

data.source: Legacy XML exports from playout system

constraints.source_preservation: source XML files must not be modified
constraints.failure_handling: all import failures captured in discrepancy report

reasoner_reviews.product: The migration scope is well-defined. The format clock rule validation is the highest-risk component: if broadcast_schedules are migrated with incorrect hourly slot duration totals, the station could face regulatory violations. The discrepancy report must capture not just failed imports but also format clock rule mismatches where totals are off by even one second.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Python Click with SQLAlchemy transactional batch writes is the right approach. The migration validator from round 1 was underspecified for format clock compliance - it only checked slot counts, not segment duration totals per clock hour. This round should add a ComplianceValidator class that computes hourly duration totals from source and target and flags violations.
reasoner_reviews.security: MySQL SSL connection and env-var credentials are sufficient for a CLI tool running in the controlled Azure Container Instances environment. The main security gap from round 1 was that migration logs containing schedule data were written to local disk without specifying retention or access controls.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 4-hour maintenance window is achievable for single-station migration; timing tests show Python SQLAlchemy batch imports of 50K schedule slots complete in approximately 45 minutes leaving buffer for validation.

backend.service_design: Click CLI with commands: migrate (full migration run), dry-run (validate without writing), validate (compliance check only), report (generate discrepancy CSV). ScheduleParser (lxml): parses legacy XML exports into BroadcastSchedule Python dataclasses. SchemaMapper: maps legacy slot fields to target MySQL schema. ComplianceValidator: computes hourly format clock totals from source and target, flags hours where total segment duration differs by more than 1 second. BatchImporter (SQLAlchemy): inserts mapped slots in transactional batches of 1000 with rollback on failure.
backend.business_modules: FormatClockEngine: validates each broadcast_schedule hour has correct talk/music/ad segment ratio per compliance_rules table. ReconciliationReporter: compares source slot count and total duration against target after migration, generates CSV with per-show discrepancies.
backend.failure_handling: SQLAlchemy transaction rollback on batch failure; failed slots written to discrepancy report with original XML data; migration continues to next batch rather than aborting entire run; final exit code non-zero if any failures exist

security.secrets_management: MySQL credentials in environment variables (MYSQL_HOST, MYSQL_USER, MYSQL_PASS); SSL cert path in MYSQL_SSL_CA env var; Azure Container Instances reads from Azure Key Vault via managed identity at container startup
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Structured JSON migration logs to Azure Blob Storage container with SAS URL expiring 30 days; log entries include slot_id, action ENUM parsed/mapped/imported/failed, timestamp, compliance_rule_id if applicable

data.entities: broadcast_schedules (id INT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, station_id VARCHAR, schedule_date DATE, day_of_week ENUM mon/tue/wed/thu/fri/sat/sun, status ENUM draft/approved/live, created_at DATETIME). playlist_slots (id INT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, schedule_id FK, slot_order INT, start_time TIME, end_time TIME, segment_type ENUM music/talk/ad/news/promo, duration_seconds INT, track_title VARCHAR, artist VARCHAR, isrc VARCHAR). playout_logs (id INT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, slot_id FK, played_at DATETIME, actual_duration_seconds INT, deviation_seconds INT). compliance_rules (id INT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, rule_name VARCHAR, rule_type ENUM min_music_pct/max_ad_duration/format_clock_hour, parameter_json JSON, jurisdiction ENUM fcc/ofcom, active BOOL). migration_batches (id INT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, run_id VARCHAR, batch_number INT, slots_attempted INT, slots_imported INT, slots_failed INT, started_at DATETIME, completed_at DATETIME). format_clocks (id INT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, station_id VARCHAR, hour_of_day INT, segment_type ENUM music/talk/ad, required_minutes INT, tolerance_seconds INT).
data.migration_notes: Source XML fields: ProgramItem/SegmentId maps to playlist_slots.isrc where type=music; ProgramItem/Duration in PT format parsed to duration_seconds; ClockHour elements map to format_clocks validation; legacy status codes Active/Inactive map to draft/live

devops.deployment_topology: Azure Container Instances: Docker image from Azure Container Registry; Azure Key Vault for MySQL credentials via managed identity; Azure Blob Storage for migration logs and discrepancy reports
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps pipeline: pytest with 80% coverage gate, mypy type check, Docker build and push to ACR; staging environment runs dry-run command against staging MySQL on schedule weekly; production pipeline requires manual approval and maintenance window confirmation
devops.observability_stack: Azure Monitor for Container Instances exit codes and run duration; migration run summary written to Azure Blob Storage as JSON with slot_count, failure_count, compliance_violations, duration_seconds; email notification to broadcast ops team via Azure Communication Services on completion

ISS-001.title: ComplianceValidator only checks slot counts, not format clock hourly duration totals
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 implementation validates that slot count in target matches source, but does not validate that hourly segment duration totals (music/talk/ad minutes per clock hour) match compliance_rules. A station could pass slot count validation but still violate FCC minimum music percentage requirements if segment durations are incorrectly mapped.

ISS-002.title: Migration logs written to local container filesystem without Azure Blob persistence
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 logs were written to /tmp/migration.log inside the container and lost when the Container Instance terminated. Fixed in round 2 by streaming logs to Azure Blob Storage via azure-storage-blob SDK.

focus_issues.title: ComplianceValidator only checks slot counts, not format clock hourly duration totals
focus_issues.detail: ComplianceValidator must compute hourly segment duration totals from both source and target and compare against compliance_rules and format_clocks tables; flagging any hour where music/talk/ad duration deviates beyond tolerance_seconds.

latest_recommendations: Extend ComplianceValidator to compute hourly format clock totals per segment_type from target MySQL and compare against format_clocks table tolerance bounds
Add compliance_violations list to discrepancy report with hour_of_day, segment_type, required_minutes, actual_minutes, and deviation_seconds fields

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 focuses on ISS-001: the ComplianceValidator gap for format clock hourly duration totals. Adding a FormatClockValidator class that sums duration_seconds per segment_type per hour_of_day from the migrated target data and compares against format_clocks tolerance bounds. ISS-002 resolved in this round via Azure Blob log streaming.

fix_report.action_taken: Added FormatClockValidator class: after batch import completes, queries MySQL target to sum duration_seconds per segment_type per hour_of_day for the migrated schedule; compares totals against format_clocks.required_minutes and tolerance_seconds; appends compliance_violations list to discrepancy report with fields hour_of_day, segment_type, required_minutes, actual_minutes, deviation_seconds, jurisdiction. Migration exit code set to non-zero if any compliance_violations exist.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Format clock hourly duration violations detected before broadcast goes live; ISS-001 resolved

target_output.title: Radio Playout Schedule Migration Tool - Round 2 Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Python Click CLI automation tool migrating a radio broadcast station's legacy XML playout schedules to a modern cloud-hosted MySQL 8 scheduler, with full format clock compliance validation. Runs in Azure Container Instances within a 4-hour maintenance window. Round 2 adds FormatClockValidator computing hourly segment duration totals against FCC/Ofcom compliance_rules, completing the compliance validation gap from round 1.
target_output.architecture_overview: Standalone Python 3.12 CLI built with Click, structured as a pipeline of composable stages: ScheduleParser (lxml XML to Python dataclasses), SchemaMapper (legacy field normalization), FormatClockValidator (hourly duration compliance), BatchImporter (SQLAlchemy transactional MySQL writes), and ReconciliationReporter (CSV discrepancy output). The tool runs in Azure Container Instances pulling credentials from Azure Key Vault via managed identity. Migration logs stream to Azure Blob Storage. A Redis cache layer handles intermediate state across migration stages.
target_output.technology_stack: CLI Framework: Python 3.12, Click 8.x, Rich for terminal progress display. XML Parsing: lxml 5.x. ORM: SQLAlchemy 2.x async with aiomysql driver for MySQL 8. Validation: custom FormatClockValidator against compliance_rules and format_clocks tables. Infrastructure: Azure Container Instances, Azure Container Registry, Azure Key Vault (managed identity), Azure Blob Storage for logs. CI/CD: Azure DevOps. Cache: Redis 7 for intermediate migration state.
target_output.functional_feature_map: migrate command: full migration run with XML parsing, schema mapping, format clock validation, transactional batch import, and reconciliation report. dry-run command: full pipeline with database writes skipped; outputs what would be imported and any compliance violations. validate command: runs FormatClockValidator against already-migrated target data without re-importing. report command: generates discrepancy CSV from migration_batches and compliance_violations tables. notify command: emails discrepancy report to broadcast operations team via Azure Communication Services.
target_output.system_components: ScheduleParser: lxml-based XML parser handling legacy ProgramItem and ClockHour elements, outputting BroadcastSchedule and PlaylistSlot dataclasses. SchemaMapper: maps legacy field names and enums to target MySQL schema; handles PT duration format to integer seconds conversion. FormatClockValidator: queries migrated target MySQL to sum duration_seconds per segment_type per hour_of_day; compares against format_clocks table; outputs list of FormatClockViolation objects. BatchImporter: SQLAlchemy sessionmaker with batch_size=1000; transaction per batch with rollback on constraint violation; writes migration_batches progress records. ReconciliationReporter: compares source XML slot count and total duration against target MySQL counts; generates CSV with per-show and per-hour discrepancies including compliance_violations. Web Dashboard: internal Streamlit dashboard for viewing historical migration run reports and compliance violation trends.
target_output.workflows: Full Migration Run: engineer runs `python migrate.py migrate --schedule-file station_WXYZ_2026-03.xml --station-id WXYZ`; ScheduleParser reads XML and yields PlaylistSlot objects; SchemaMapper normalizes fields; BatchImporter writes to MySQL in transactional batches of 1000 with progress bar via Rich; after all batches, FormatClockValidator queries target MySQL and sums duration_seconds per segment_type per clock hour; violations appended to compliance_violations list; ReconciliationReporter generates discrepancy.csv with slot counts, duration totals, and compliance_violations per hour; exit code 0 if no failures and no violations, non-zero otherwise; logs streamed to Azure Blob Storage. Dry-Run Validation: same pipeline with BatchImporter in no-write mode; compliance violations detected and reported without database changes; used weekly by broadcast engineers to validate XML exports before migration window.
target_output.data_model: broadcast_schedules (id INT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, station_id VARCHAR(20), schedule_date DATE, day_of_week ENUM mon/tue/wed/thu/fri/sat/sun, status ENUM draft/approved/live, created_at DATETIME). playlist_slots (id INT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, schedule_id FK, slot_order INT, start_time TIME, end_time TIME, segment_type ENUM music/talk/ad/news/promo, duration_seconds INT, track_title VARCHAR(255), artist VARCHAR(255), isrc CHAR(12)). compliance_rules (id INT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, rule_name VARCHAR, rule_type ENUM min_music_pct/max_ad_duration/format_clock_hour, parameter_json JSON, jurisdiction ENUM fcc/ofcom, active BOOL). format_clocks (id INT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, station_id VARCHAR(20), hour_of_day INT, segment_type ENUM music/talk/ad, required_minutes INT, tolerance_seconds INT). migration_batches (id INT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, run_id VARCHAR(36), batch_number INT, slots_attempted INT, slots_imported INT, slots_failed INT, started_at DATETIME, completed_at DATETIME). playout_logs (id INT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, slot_id FK, played_at DATETIME, actual_duration_seconds INT, deviation_seconds INT).
target_output.api_design: CLI commands (no HTTP API): `migrate --schedule-file PATH --station-id ID [--dry-run] [--batch-size INT=1000]` | `validate --station-id ID --schedule-date DATE` | `report --run-id UUID --output-format csv|json` | `notify --run-id UUID --recipients EMAIL`. Internal integration: SQLAlchemy connection to target MySQL 8 (SSL required). Azure Blob Storage SDK for log upload (azure-storage-blob). Azure Key Vault SDK for credential retrieval (azure-identity managed identity). Azure Communication Services Email SDK for completion notification.
target_output.security_and_compliance: MySQL connection via SSL with CA cert in MYSQL_SSL_CA env var; credentials fetched from Azure Key Vault via Container Instances managed identity at startup; never in source code. Migration logs (structured JSON) streamed to Azure Blob Storage with 30-day retention via SAS URL; access restricted to broadcast operations team via Azure Active Directory. Format clock compliance validation aligned with FCC minimum music percentage and maximum ad duration rules per jurisdiction setting; violations fail the migration run requiring engineer review before broadcast. Reconciliation reports retained 1 year in Blob Storage for regulatory audit evidence.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure Container Instances: Docker image built and pushed to Azure Container Registry via Azure DevOps pipeline; run triggered manually by broadcast engineer or on Azure Logic Apps schedule. Azure Key Vault integration: Container Instances managed identity with Key Vault Secrets User role. Azure DevOps CI: pytest with 80% coverage gate including FormatClockValidator unit tests, mypy type check, Docker build and ACR push on merge to main. Staging environment: weekly dry-run against staging MySQL populated from anonymized source XML exports; any compliance_violations or slot count mismatches fail the staging run and block production merge. Production: manual trigger by broadcast operations lead during 4-hour maintenance window.
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor for Container Instances: exit code tracking, run duration metric, memory usage. Migration run summary JSON written to Azure Blob Storage on completion: run_id, station_id, slots_attempted, slots_imported, slots_failed, compliance_violations_count, duration_seconds, completed_at. Email notification to broadcast ops team via Azure Communication Services with summary and discrepancy CSV attachment. Alert rule in Azure Monitor: exit code non-zero triggers email escalation to broadcast director.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Minimal cost: Azure Container Instances billed per-second during migration run (estimated 45 minutes for single station, approximately $0.05/run). Azure Blob Storage for logs and reports under $5/month. Azure Key Vault under $1/month. Azure DevOps free tier for single-engineer project. Multi-station batch mode in Phase 2 would run parallel Container Instances per station at proportional cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase one delivers the complete legacy-to-modern schedule migration pipeline: XML ingestion and parsing, field normalization via SchemaMapper, format-clock compliance validation, transactional batch import to the target MySQL database, and a reconciliation report flagging any schedule discrepancies. Phase two adds comprehensive format clock compliance validation and reporting capabilities to ensure regulatory requirements are met across the board. Phase three delivers advanced features including multi-station batch mode and live schedule sync.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: ruff and mypy strict in Azure DevOps CI; pytest with 80% coverage including unit tests for FormatClockValidator hourly aggregation logic and SchemaMapper PT duration conversion. Click commands tested with CliRunner in pytest. No hardcoded station IDs or schedule dates in source code. Separate config for FCC vs Ofcom jurisdiction compliance rules. Docker image pinned to specific Python 3.12 base image SHA for reproducible builds.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Legacy XML exports from some stations use non-standard ProgramItem schemas. Mitigation: ScheduleParser includes a schema_version detection step; unsupported schemas fail fast with descriptive error before any database writes. Risk: Format clock rules differ between FCC (US) and Ofcom (UK) jurisdictions; incorrect jurisdiction setting could allow violations. Mitigation: jurisdiction required CLI parameter with no default; validated against compliance_rules table on startup. Tradeoff: SQLAlchemy over raw SQL for MySQL writes - adds ORM overhead but enables transactional batch rollback and migration to other DB engines if scheduler vendor changes. Tradeoff: Azure Container Instances over Azure Functions - ACI provides more predictable memory limits for large XML file parsing without cold start concerns.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected lxml over Python stdlib xml.etree for legacy XML parsing due to XPath support needed for ProgramItem/ClockHour element extraction. Confirmed SQLAlchemy batch size of 1000 slots per transaction as safe for 4-hour window with 50K slots typical single-station schedule. Azure Key Vault over environment variable secrets for production to avoid credential exposure in Container Instances environment variable logs. FormatClockValidator runs post-import query rather than in-memory validation to catch any ORM mapping errors that would only surface after database write.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed: Redis cache referenced in architecture_overview and technology_stack but not in contract and inappropriate for CLI migration tool; phased_implementation uses forbidden boilerplate language; system_components includes a Streamlit web dashboard inconsistent with automation_tool/CLI project class.

project_goal.value: Static documentation and showcase site for an open-source audio ML library that performs genre classification, presenting API docs, benchmark results, and interactive in-browser WASM demos
project_goal.rationale: Primary goal is developer adoption via clear docs and hands-on demos

target_users.value: Open-source developers and ML practitioners evaluating the genre classification library
target_users.rationale: Core audience identified as developers exploring audio ML tooling

project_class.rationale: No server-side runtime required; pure static delivery via CDN

capabilities.rationale: Site needs rendered docs and visitor analytics; no auth or backend

complexity_level.rationale: Static site with MDX content and WASM demo embed; minimal interactivity

risk_level.rationale: Public docs site with no user data collection or auth

data_sensitivity.rationale: No personal or sensitive data collected or stored

external_exposure.rationale: Open docs accessible to anyone; no authentication required

access_model.value: Fully public, no authentication
access_model.rationale: Open-source library docs must be freely accessible

feature_scope.rationale: Core content types covering the library's value proposition

mvp_scope.value: API reference docs, one interactive WASM genre demo, and benchmark result tables for v1 release
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum content needed for public v1 library launch

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Cloudflare, Content Security Policy headers, no third-party scripts except analytics
security_baseline.rationale: Static site security baseline; WASM sandbox prevents JS escape

frontend_stack.rationale: Astro excels at content-heavy static sites with partial hydration for WASM demo island

backend_stack.value: None - fully static, no server-side runtime
backend_stack.rationale: Static website project class; all content pre-rendered at build time

data_platform.value: None - no database; content sourced from MDX files and JSON benchmark fixtures
data_platform.rationale: Static content model; no persistence layer required

hosting_target.value: Cloudflare Pages with global edge delivery
hosting_target.rationale: Zero-cost CDN hosting with automatic HTTPS and edge caching for global developer audience

privacy_retention_policy.value: Cloudflare Web Analytics collects no cookies and stores no personal data; no retention obligations
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Cookie-free analytics satisfy GDPR without consent banner

future_scope.value: Versioned docs for multiple library releases, community-contributed audio demo gallery
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch roadmap once community adoption grows

constraints.rationale: Open-source project has no budget for hosted infrastructure

observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight analytics sufficient for a docs site with no SLA requirements

project.goal: Publish docs, benchmarks, and interactive demos for the audio genre classification library
project.target_users: ML developers evaluating the open-source library

reasoner_reviews.product: The docs site must clearly communicate the genre classifier's accuracy, supported audio formats, and WASM compatibility so developers can evaluate integration fit without reading source code. Benchmark tables comparing the library against librosa and Essentia baselines are the highest-value conversion asset.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro's island architecture is the right fit here - the WASM demo requires client-side JavaScript hydration while the rest of the site can be fully static HTML. MDX enables API reference pages authored close to source. Cloudflare Pages edge delivery keeps global latency low for the international developer audience.
reasoner_reviews.security: Static site attack surface is minimal, but the WASM demo island must use a restrictive CSP to prevent arbitrary script injection. Cloudflare WAF and DDoS protection are included in the free tier and provide adequate baseline protection for a public docs site.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero hosting cost is satisfied by Cloudflare Pages free tier. Build times under 60 seconds are achievable with Astro incremental builds. No server-side runtime means no scaling concerns.

frontend.app_structure: Astro project with src/pages for API reference routes, src/components for demo island and benchmark table, src/content for MDX doc collections

frontend.performance_notes: Static HTML pre-rendering for all doc pages; WASM module lazy-loaded only when demo island is visible using Intersection Observer; audio sample files served from Cloudflare CDN with long cache TTL
frontend.frontend_risks: WASM binary size (~2MB for the genre model) may slow initial demo load on mobile; mitigation is deferred loading and a streaming WASM instantiation pattern

security.abuse_prevention: Cloudflare WAF at edge blocks malicious traffic before reaching origin; rate limiting not needed for static assets

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a zero-backend static site for an open-source audio ML library. Key decisions: Astro islands for surgical WASM hydration without shipping JS to doc pages, MDX for API reference co-located with content, Cloudflare Pages for global zero-cost CDN delivery. The WASM demo is the primary interactive differentiator and must load efficiently on metered connections.
target_output.title: Audio Genre Classifier Library - Documentation and Demo Site
target_output.executive_summary: A fully static Astro site delivering API reference documentation, benchmark comparison tables, and an in-browser WASM genre classification demo for an open-source audio ML library. Hosted on Cloudflare Pages with edge delivery to a global developer audience. No server-side runtime, no database, and no personal data collection.
target_output.architecture_overview: Static site architecture built on Astro 4 with MDX content collections. All pages pre-rendered at build time to static HTML; the WASM genre demo is the sole interactive island, hydrated client-side using Astro client:visible directive so the WebAssembly module loads only when the demo section scrolls into view. API reference pages are generated during the Astro build from typedoc-plugin-markdown output pointed at the library source. Benchmark fixture data is a JSON file committed to the repository and consumed by the BenchmarkTable component at build time. Cloudflare Pages serves all assets from edge PoPs globally. GitHub Actions orchestrates the build pipeline and triggers deploys on merge to main.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4, MDX, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Astro Islands (client:visible). Demo: WASM binary compiled from the genre classifier library, Web Audio API for file input in the browser. Build tooling: Bun, typedoc with typedoc-plugin-markdown for API doc generation. Hosting: Cloudflare Pages (CDN plus HTTPS plus WAF). CI: GitHub Actions. Analytics: Cloudflare Web Analytics (cookieless).
target_output.functional_feature_map: API Reference module: multi-page docs auto-generated from JSDoc annotations covering classifier constructor, predict(), loadModel(), and AudioBuffer utilities. Benchmark Table: interactive sortable table comparing genre classification accuracy (%) and inference latency (ms) against Librosa and Essentia baselines across five datasets (GTZAN, FMA-small, MagnaTagATune, NSynth, AudioSet-balanced). WASM Demo Island: browser-side demo accepting file upload or microphone input, running the compiled genre model, and displaying top-3 genre predictions with confidence bars. Changelog: version-tagged release notes rendered from MDX. Contributor Gallery: GitHub handle cards auto-populated from contributors.json.
target_output.system_components: Cloudflare Pages edge network serving pre-built dist/ assets. GitHub Actions build runner executing Astro build pipeline. WASM binary (genre_classifier.wasm) bundled into Astro output and lazy-loaded via client:visible island. typedoc-plugin-markdown generating API reference MDX from library TypeScript types at build time. benchmark_results.json fixture providing static data to BenchmarkTable component. Cloudflare WAF providing edge security.
target_output.workflows: Developer Docs Visit: A developer navigates to /api-reference/classifier, reads the statically rendered predict() docs with parameter types and return value examples, then clicks 'Try in Browser' which lazy-loads the WASM island, uploads a 10-second WAV file, and sees the top genre prediction ('Electronic - 87%') rendered within 400ms of model execution. Build and Deploy: A maintainer merges a PR updating benchmark_results.json; GitHub Actions runs 'bun run build', typedoc regenerates API pages, Astro builds static HTML, Cloudflare Wrangler deploys the dist/ folder to Pages, and the new benchmark row is live globally within 30 seconds.
target_output.data_model: doc_pages (slug VARCHAR PK, title, section ENUM api_reference/benchmarks/guides/changelog, content_mdx TEXT, last_updated DATE). benchmark_results (model_name VARCHAR, dataset ENUM gtzan/fma_small/magnatagatune/nsynth/audioset, accuracy_pct NUMERIC, inference_ms NUMERIC, model_params_millions NUMERIC). audio_demos (id, label, wasm_input_format, expected_top_genre, confidence_threshold NUMERIC). changelog_entries (version SEMVER, release_date DATE, highlights TEXT[]). contributor_profiles (github_handle VARCHAR PK, avatar_url, contribution_count INT). All entities stored as JSON and MDX files in the git repository with no runtime database.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Build-time data access only: typedoc CLI invoked via 'bun run docs:generate' reads TypeScript source and emits MDX to src/content/api/. Astro content collections expose getCollection('api') for page routing. Static JSON endpoints served as CDN assets: GET /benchmark-data.json (sortable benchmark fixture), GET /contributors.json (contributor gallery data). No REST or GraphQL endpoints at runtime.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced globally via Cloudflare TLS termination. Content Security Policy in Cloudflare Pages _headers file: default-src self; script-src self wasm-unsafe-eval; style-src self unsafe-inline; media-src blob: - wasm-unsafe-eval required for WASM execution while blocking arbitrary inline scripts. X-Frame-Options DENY. Referrer-Policy strict-origin-when-cross-origin. Cloudflare WAF blocks common web attack patterns at edge. GDPR compliance via Cloudflare Web Analytics cookieless tracking with no consent banner required.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions workflow on push to main: checkout repo, setup Bun, run 'bun run docs:generate' (typedoc), run 'bun run build' (astro build), deploy to Cloudflare Pages via Wrangler action with CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN secret. PR workflow creates preview deployments automatically via Cloudflare Pages GitHub integration - each PR gets a unique preview URL. Rollback via Cloudflare Pages deployment history UI - one-click revert to any prior deployment. No staging environment needed; PR previews serve that validation function.
target_output.observability: Cloudflare Web Analytics dashboard tracks page views per doc section, WASM demo engagement rate (clicks on 'Try in Browser'), and geographic distribution of developer visitors. GitHub Actions build logs surface typedoc generation errors and Astro build failures. Cloudflare Pages deployment log provides deploy duration and asset count per release.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Hosting: $0/month on Cloudflare Pages free tier (500 builds per month, unlimited bandwidth). WASM binary served from edge cache with 1-year Cache-Control; subsequent visits load model from browser cache. GitHub Actions CI free tier covers open-source repository. Total infrastructure cost: zero ongoing spend.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Launch (2 weeks): Configure Astro project with MDX content collections, implement WASM demo island with Web Audio API file input, integrate typedoc pipeline, deploy to Cloudflare Pages, configure CSP and security headers. Phase 2 - Content Enrichment (1 week): Add all five benchmark dataset rows, populate contributor gallery from contributors.json, write quickstart guide and integration examples in MDX. Phase 3 - Versioned Docs (post-launch): Add version selector dropdown, configure content collection versioning, set up redirect rules for legacy doc URLs.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode throughout Astro components. Tailwind CSS only for styling. WASM demo island isolated in src/components/GenreDemo.tsx with no shared global state. Benchmark data changes update benchmark_results.json only, requiring no code changes. PR previews required before merging. Bun used for all package management and script execution.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: 2MB WASM binary degrades demo experience on slow mobile connections. Mitigation: streaming WASM instantiation via WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming(), progressive loading indicator, and client:visible deferred hydration. Risk: typedoc API generation silently diverges when library TypeScript types change without docs update. Mitigation: typedoc runs in CI and fails the build on generation errors. Tradeoff: Astro islands over a full SPA avoids shipping React to all doc pages but requires maintainers to understand island hydration semantics.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose Cloudflare Pages over Netlify for zero-cost WASM-compatible CDN with no function invocation limits. Confirmed cookieless Cloudflare Web Analytics eliminates GDPR consent requirements. Decided WASM demo accepts file upload and microphone input for maximum developer exploration value.

metadata.notes: Static docs and demo site for open-source audio genre classification library; Astro WASM island architecture; weak quality per manifest.
